title: The Paradox of Brazil’s Micro-Enterprises
text:
Background
Brazil’s CNPJ registry provides a rare window into the formal business sector of Latin America’s largest economy. The dataset contains companies filtered to those with capital stock exceeding 150,000 BRL (~$30,000 USD), meaning we are examining firms that have already crossed a meaningful capitalization threshold. If capital concentration exists even within this subset of more substantial businesses, it points to deeper structural patterns in how Brazilian firms accumulate resources.
In development economics, the relationship between firm size and capital accumulation matters because it reveals whether formal registration alone is enough to put small businesses on a growth trajectory, or whether structural barriers—credit constraints, regulatory burdens, market concentration—keep them from scaling up even after they enter the formal sector.
crumbs: 3  The Paradox of Brazil's Micro-Enterprises

title: The Paradox of Brazil’s Micro-Enterprises
text:
Motivation & Research Question
Across developing economies, micro-enterprises make up the vast majority of registered firms. Policymakers often celebrate high rates of business formalization as a sign of economic dynamism. But if the smallest formal businesses cannot accumulate capital at rates proportional to their numbers, that dynamism may be hollow: a large base of firms that are formally registered yet structurally unable to grow. Understanding whether this “capital trap” exists in Brazil—and how it varies by legal structure—can help distinguish between inclusive growth and mere headcount expansion.
Do micro-enterprises in Brazil face a capital trap, where they constitute the majority of registered firms yet control a disproportionately small share of total capital stock?
crumbs: 3  The Paradox of Brazil's Micro-Enterprises

title: The Paradox of Brazil’s Micro-Enterprises
text:
Data & Analysis

Show codelibrary(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(readr)
library(scales)
library(stringr)
library(showtext)

# Load Google Font for professional typography
showtext_auto()



Show code# Load data (comma-delimited)
companies <- read_csv(
  'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2026/2026-01-27/companies.csv',
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# Prepare summary data
size_summary <- companies |>
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(company_size), !is.na(capital_stock)) |>
  dplyr::group_by(company_size) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n_companies = n(),
    total_capital = sum(capital_stock, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_capital = mean(capital_stock, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_capital = median(capital_stock, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    pct_companies = n_companies / sum(n_companies) * 100,
    pct_capital = total_capital / sum(total_capital) * 100,
    capital_gap = pct_companies - pct_capital,
    company_size = dplyr::case_when(
      company_size == "micro-enterprise" ~ "Micro",
      company_size == "small-enterprise" ~ "Small",
      TRUE ~ "Medium/Large"
    ),
    company_size = factor(company_size, levels = c("Micro", "Small", "Medium/Large"))
  )
crumbs: 3  The Paradox of Brazil's Micro-Enterprises

title: The Paradox of Brazil’s Micro-Enterprises
section: Visualization 1: Micro-Enterprises Hold 80% of Firms but a Fraction of Capital
text:
Visualization 1: Micro-Enterprises Hold 80% of Firms but a Fraction of Capital

Show code# Reshape for paired bar chart — separate panels by metric per professor's suggestion
plot_data <- size_summary |>
  dplyr::select(company_size, pct_companies, pct_capital) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = c(pct_companies, pct_capital),
    names_to = "metric",
    values_to = "percentage"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    metric = dplyr::if_else(metric == "pct_companies", "Share of Companies", "Share of Capital"),
    metric = factor(metric, levels = c("Share of Companies", "Share of Capital"))
  )

# Define colors
colors <- c("Share of Companies" = "#264653", "Share of Capital" = "#e76f51")

ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = company_size, y = percentage, fill = metric)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = sprintf("%.1f%%", percentage)),
    vjust = -0.5,
    size = 4.5,
    fontface = "bold",
    family = "source"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~metric) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = colors, guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 90),
    breaks = seq(0, 80, 20),
    labels = label_percent(scale = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Micro-enterprises make up ~80% of firms but hold a small fraction of total capital",
    subtitle = "Distribution of company counts and capital stock by firm size, Brazilian CNPJ registry (capital > 150k BRL)",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    caption = "Source: Brazilian CNPJ Registry (Receita Federal) via TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 4 · Zhijun He · 2026-02-11"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14, base_family = "source") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 17, color = "#264653", margin = margin(b = 5)),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12, color = "gray40", margin = margin(b = 20), lineheight = 1.2),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 9, color = "gray50", hjust = 0, margin = margin(t = 15)),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 12, color = "gray30"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13, color = "#264653"),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "gray95", color = NA),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "gray90"),
    panel.spacing = unit(2, "lines"),
    plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 20, 20)
  )






Figure 3.1




The side-by-side panels make the paradox visually immediate. On the left, micro-enterprises dominate the count; on the right, their share of capital shrinks dramatically. Medium/Large firms show the exact inverse: a small slice of firms commanding the lion’s share of capital. Small enterprises sit in between, roughly proportional on both dimensions.
crumbs: 3  The Paradox of Brazil's Micro-Enterprises

title: The Paradox of Brazil’s Micro-Enterprises
section: Visualization 2: Capital Concentration Across Legal Structures
text:
Visualization 2: Capital Concentration Across Legal Structures

Show code# Calculate metrics by company size AND legal nature (top categories)
top_legal <- companies |>
  dplyr::count(legal_nature, sort = TRUE) |>
  dplyr::slice_head(n = 5) |>
  dplyr::pull(legal_nature)

combined_summary <- companies |>
  dplyr::filter(
    !is.na(company_size), 
    !is.na(capital_stock),
    !is.na(legal_nature),
    legal_nature %in% top_legal
  ) |>
  dplyr::group_by(company_size, legal_nature) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n_companies = dplyr::n(),
    total_capital = sum(capital_stock, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_capital = mean(capital_stock, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_capital = median(capital_stock, na.rm = TRUE),
    # Capital concentration ratio (mean/median - higher = more skewed)
    concentration_ratio = mean_capital / median_capital,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  # Calculate percentages within each legal nature
  dplyr::group_by(legal_nature) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    pct_companies = n_companies / sum(n_companies) * 100,
    pct_capital = total_capital / sum(total_capital) * 100,
    capital_gap = pct_capital - pct_companies
  ) |>
  dplyr::ungroup() |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    company_size = dplyr::case_when(
      company_size == "micro-enterprise" ~ "Micro",
      company_size == "small-enterprise" ~ "Small",
      TRUE ~ "Medium/Large"
    ),
    company_size = factor(company_size, levels = c("Micro", "Small", "Medium/Large")),
    legal_nature_short = dplyr::case_when(
      stringr::str_detect(legal_nature, "LLC") ~ "LLC",
      stringr::str_detect(legal_nature, "Individual") ~ "Individual\nEntrepreneur",
      stringr::str_detect(legal_nature, "Corporation") ~ "Corporation",
      stringr::str_detect(legal_nature, "Simple") ~ "Simple\nCompany",
      TRUE ~ stringr::str_trunc(legal_nature, 15)
    )
  )

ggplot(combined_summary, 
       aes(x = pct_companies, y = pct_capital)) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray60", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(xend = pct_companies, yend = pct_companies, color = company_size),
    linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(size = total_capital / 1e9, fill = company_size),
    shape = 21, alpha = 0.85, stroke = 1.2, color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = sprintf("%.1fx", concentration_ratio)),
    size = 2.8, fontface = "bold", color = "gray20",
    vjust = -1.8, family = "source"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~legal_nature_short, nrow = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 100),
    breaks = c(0, 50, 100),
    labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 100),
    breaks = c(0, 50, 100),
    labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")
  ) +
  scale_size_continuous(
    range = c(4, 25),
    labels = scales::label_number(suffix = "B BRL"),
    name = "Total Capital",
    breaks = c(1, 10, 50, 100)
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Micro" = "#e76f51", "Small" = "#f4a261", "Medium/Large" = "#264653"),
    name = "Company Size"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Micro" = "#e76f51", "Small" = "#f4a261", "Medium/Large" = "#264653"),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "The capital trap persists across all major legal structures",
    subtitle = "Points above the diagonal control more capital than their numbers suggest; labels show mean/median ratio (higher = more internal inequality)",
    x = "Share of Companies →",
    y = "← Share of Capital",
    caption = "Source: Brazilian CNPJ Registry via TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 4 · Bubble size = total capital stock · Zhijun He · 2026-02-11"
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text", x = 85, y = 15, 
    label = "Capital\nDeficit\nZone", 
    color = "#e76f51", size = 3.5, fontface = "italic",
    family = "source", lineheight = 0.9
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text", x = 15, y = 85, 
    label = "Capital\nSurplus\nZone", 
    color = "#264653", size = 3.5, fontface = "italic",
    family = "source", lineheight = 0.9
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13, base_family = "source") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 17, color = "#264653", margin = margin(b = 5)),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10.5, color = "gray40", margin = margin(b = 15), lineheight = 1.2),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 9, color = "gray50", hjust = 0, margin = margin(t = 15)),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "horizontal",
    legend.margin = margin(t = 10),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12, color = "#264653"),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "gray95", color = NA),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "gray90", linewidth = 0.3),
    panel.spacing = unit(1.5, "lines"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 11, color = "gray40"),
    plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 20, 20)
  ) +
  guides(
    fill = guide_legend(order = 1, override.aes = list(size = 6)),
    size = guide_legend(order = 2)
  )






Figure 3.2




The capital trap is not an artifact of a single legal form. Across LLCs, Individual Entrepreneurs, Corporations, and Simple Companies, micro-enterprises (orange) consistently sit below the diagonal in the “Capital Deficit Zone”—they account for more firms than their capital share warrants. Medium/Large firms (dark teal) sit above the diagonal in every panel. The mean/median ratio labels reveal that internal inequality is highest within Corporations and LLCs, where a few large firms pull the mean far above the median.
crumbs: 3  The Paradox of Brazil's Micro-Enterprises

title: The Paradox of Brazil’s Micro-Enterprises
text:
Key Finding
The visualization reveals a stark capital paradox in Brazil’s corporate structure. Micro-enterprises constitute the largest share of registered companies yet control a disproportionately small fraction of total capital stock. Medium/Large firms show the inverse pattern—fewer in number but commanding the majority of capital. This pattern persists even after filtering for firms with capital above 150,000 BRL, suggesting that the barriers to capital accumulation for small firms are structural rather than simply a function of including marginal businesses.
crumbs: 3  The Paradox of Brazil's Micro-Enterprises

title: The Paradox of Brazil’s Micro-Enterprises
text:
Implications
For development practitioners and policymakers, this finding raises important questions. First, do micro-enterprises face credit constraints that prevent capital accumulation? Second, are compliance costs disproportionately affecting smaller firms’ ability to reinvest? Third, does Brazil’s economic structure favor consolidation over broad-based firm growth? The concentration of capital in larger firms—even within a dataset of already-formalized, reasonably capitalized businesses—suggests that Brazil’s development challenge is not just about formalizing the informal sector, but about enabling smaller formal firms to grow.
crumbs: 3  The Paradox of Brazil's Micro-Enterprises

title: The Resource Appetite of Edible Plants
text:
Background
The Edible Plant Database (EPD) catalogues 146 edible species from the GROW Observatory, a European citizen-science project on growing food. Each species is characterized by its sunlight, water, nutrient, and temperature requirements, along with cultivation class (e.g., Brassica, Legume, Cucurbit) and soil preferences. The database was designed to help growers answer practical questions such as “What can I plant now?” based on local conditions.
For home gardeners, one of the most consequential planning decisions is understanding which plants are resource-intensive and which are low-maintenance. A plant’s demand for sunlight, water, and nutrients are not independent: some species are “hungry” across all three dimensions while others are frugal. Knowing whether these resource demands tend to cluster—and which cultivation families are the most demanding—can help gardeners design beds that group compatible species together and allocate scarce inputs (especially water) more efficiently.
crumbs: 4  The Resource Appetite of Edible Plants

title: The Resource Appetite of Edible Plants
text:
Motivation & Research Question
Beginner gardeners often learn the hard way that planting a water-hungry cucumber next to drought-tolerant rosemary leads to trouble. Resource mismatches between neighboring plants are a leading cause of disappointing harvests in small gardens. If we could visualize how water, sunlight, and nutrient demands co-vary across plant families, gardeners could plan beds more intentionally.
This analysis asks: How do water, sunlight, and nutrient requirements jointly vary across cultivation groups in the Edible Plant Database, and which families are the most versus least resource-intensive overall?
crumbs: 4  The Resource Appetite of Edible Plants

title: The Resource Appetite of Edible Plants
text:
Data Preparation

Show code# Standardize the categorical variables
plants_clean <- edible_plants |>
  mutate(
    # Normalize case inconsistencies
    water = str_to_title(water),
    sunlight = str_to_title(sunlight),
    nutrients = str_to_title(nutrients),
    # Simplify sunlight into ordered categories
    sunlight_simple = case_when(
      str_detect(sunlight, "Full Shade|Full Sun/Partial Shade/Full Shade") ~ "Any (incl. full shade)",
      str_detect(sunlight, "Partial Shade") & !str_detect(sunlight, "Full Sun") ~ "Partial shade only",
      str_detect(sunlight, "Full Sun/Partial Shade") ~ "Full sun / partial shade",
      str_detect(sunlight, "Full Sun") ~ "Full sun only",
      TRUE ~ sunlight
    ),
    # Collapse water to clean ordered factor
    water_clean = case_when(
      water %in% c("Very Low") ~ "Very Low",
      water %in% c("Low") ~ "Low",
      water %in% c("Medium") ~ "Medium",
      water %in% c("High") ~ "High",
      water %in% c("Very High") ~ "Very High",
      TRUE ~ water
    ),
    # Collapse nutrients
    nutrients_clean = case_when(
      str_detect(nutrients, "Low") ~ "Low",
      str_detect(nutrients, "Medium") ~ "Medium",
      str_detect(nutrients, "High") ~ "High",
      TRUE ~ nutrients
    ),
    # Merge Brassicas typo with Brassica; Solanum with Solanaceae
    cultivation_clean = case_when(
      cultivation %in% c("Brassica", "Brassicas") ~ "Brassica",
      cultivation %in% c("Solanum") ~ "Solanaceae",
      TRUE ~ cultivation
    )
  ) |>
  # Assign numeric scores for resource intensity

  mutate(
    water_score = case_match(
      water_clean,
      "Very Low" ~ 1, "Low" ~ 2, "Medium" ~ 3, "High" ~ 4, "Very High" ~ 5
    ),
    sunlight_score = case_match(
      sunlight_simple,
      "Any (incl. full shade)" ~ 1,
      "Partial shade only" ~ 2,
      "Full sun / partial shade" ~ 3,
      "Full sun only" ~ 4
    ),
    nutrient_score = case_match(
      nutrients_clean,
      "Low" ~ 1, "Medium" ~ 2, "High" ~ 3
    )
  )

# Filter to cultivation groups with >= 3 species for meaningful comparison
cult_counts <- plants_clean |> count(cultivation_clean) |> filter(n >= 3)
plants_filtered <- plants_clean |> filter(cultivation_clean %in% cult_counts$cultivation_clean)
crumbs: 4  The Resource Appetite of Edible Plants

title: The Resource Appetite of Edible Plants
section: Visualization 1: Water Demand Varies Sharply Across Cultivation Families
crumbs: 4  The Resource Appetite of Edible Plants

title: The Resource Appetite of Edible Plants
section: Visualization 2: A Resource-Intensity Heatmap of Cultivation Families
text:
Visualization 2: A Resource-Intensity Heatmap of Cultivation Families

Show code# Compute mean resource scores per cultivation group
resource_summary <- plants_filtered |>
  filter(!is.na(water_score), !is.na(sunlight_score), !is.na(nutrient_score)) |>
  group_by(cultivation_clean) |>
  summarise(
    Water = mean(water_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    Sunlight = mean(sunlight_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    Nutrients = mean(nutrient_score, na.rm = TRUE),
    n = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  # Compute overall intensity (mean of the three)
  mutate(overall = (Water + Sunlight + Nutrients) / 3) |>
  arrange(desc(overall))

# Pivot long for heatmap
resource_long <- resource_summary |>
  select(cultivation_clean, Water, Sunlight, Nutrients) |>
  pivot_longer(-cultivation_clean, names_to = "resource", values_to = "score") |>
  mutate(
    cultivation_clean = factor(
      cultivation_clean,
      levels = resource_summary |> arrange(overall) |> pull(cultivation_clean)
    ),
    resource = factor(resource, levels = c("Sunlight", "Water", "Nutrients"))
  )

ggplot(resource_long, aes(x = resource, y = cultivation_clean, fill = score)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = round(score, 1)),
    color = "white", fontface = "bold", size = 4.5
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#2c7bb6", mid = "#fdae61", high = "#d7191c",
    midpoint = 2.5, limits = c(1, 4),
    name = "Mean\ndemand\nscore"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Cucurbits demand the most across all three resources; herbs demand the least",
    subtitle = "Average resource-demand score (1 = lowest, 4–5 = highest) per cultivation family",
    x = NULL, y = NULL,
    caption = "Source: Edible Plant Database (GROW Observatory) · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 5 · Zhijun He · 2026-02-11"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    plot.caption = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 11)
  )







The heatmap reveals that resource demands do tend to cluster together. Cucurbits are the most demanding family across all three dimensions: they need the most sunlight, the most water, and the most nutrients. Solanaceae (tomatoes, peppers, potatoes) and Salad greens are close behind. On the other end, Lamiaceae herbs (mint, oregano, basil) and Chenopodiaceae (spinach, chard) are consistently frugal—they are strong candidates for low-maintenance beds. Miscellaneous plants (a catch-all group that includes fruits, trees, and other species) sit in the middle, reflecting the group’s heterogeneity.
crumbs: 4  The Resource Appetite of Edible Plants

title: The Resource Appetite of Edible Plants
text:
Takeaway
Resource demands across the three key inputs—sunlight, water, and nutrients—are positively correlated at the family level: plant groups that are hungry for one resource tend to be hungry for all three. For gardeners, this means grouping plants by cultivation family is a reasonable heuristic for resource compatibility. The practical lesson is simple: plant your cucumbers with your melons (and water them generously), and keep your herbs together in a drier, leaner bed.
crumbs: 4  The Resource Appetite of Edible Plants

title: The Rhythm of the Rings
text:
Background
The 2026 Winter Olympics in Milano Cortina, Italy, will feature 1,866 scheduled sessions across 16 sport disciplines, spanning from February 4 through February 23. These sessions include official training runs, qualification rounds, preliminary matches, and medal events. The schedule dataset captures start and end times, venue information, and crucially whether each session is a training run, a competition, or a medal-deciding event. This year’s Games also introduce ski mountaineering as a new Olympic discipline.
Not all Olympic days are created equal. The opening days are dominated by training sessions and preliminary rounds, while the final weekend packs in a concentrated burst of gold-medal moments. For fans deciding when to watch—and for broadcasters planning coverage—the distribution of medal events over time is what defines the dramatic arc of the Games.
crumbs: 5  The Rhythm of the Rings

title: The Rhythm of the Rings
text:
Motivation & Research Question
Olympic organizers face a scheduling puzzle: spread medal events too thin and individual days feel uneventful; cluster them too tightly and viewers miss key moments. The resulting schedule reflects deliberate decisions about pacing and drama. Understanding this tempo can help fans prioritize which days to follow most closely and reveals how different sports contribute to the Games’ overall rhythm.
This analysis asks: How does the Milano Cortina 2026 schedule distribute medal events across its 20-day span, and which disciplines drive the peaks in medal-event intensity?
crumbs: 5  The Rhythm of the Rings

title: The Rhythm of the Rings
text:
Data Preparation

Show code# Parse dates and compute event duration
sched_clean <- schedule |>
  mutate(
    date = as.Date(date),
    # Compute duration in minutes from UTC times
    duration_min = as.numeric(
      difftime(end_datetime_utc, start_datetime_utc, units = "mins")
    ),
    # Create a session type label
    session_type = case_when(
      is_medal_event ~ "Medal event",
      is_training    ~ "Training",
      TRUE           ~ "Competition (non-medal)"
    ),
    session_type = factor(
      session_type,
      levels = c("Training", "Competition (non-medal)", "Medal event")
    ),
    # Day number relative to opening ceremony (Feb 6)
    day_number = as.integer(date - as.Date("2026-02-06")) + 1
  )

# Quick summary
cat("Total sessions:", nrow(sched_clean), "\n")

Total sessions: 1866 

Show codecat("Date range:", as.character(min(sched_clean$date)), "to",
    as.character(max(sched_clean$date)), "\n")

Date range: 2026-02-04 to 2026-02-22 

Show codecat("Disciplines:", n_distinct(sched_clean$discipline_name), "\n")

Disciplines: 16 

Show codecat("Medal events:", sum(sched_clean$is_medal_event), "\n")

Medal events: 344 

Show codecat("Training sessions:", sum(sched_clean$is_training), "\n")

Training sessions: 246
crumbs: 5  The Rhythm of the Rings

title: The Rhythm of the Rings
section: Visualization 1: Medal Events Surge in the Final Week While Training Fades After Opening Days
text:
Visualization 1: Medal Events Surge in the Final Week While Training Fades After Opening Days

Show code# Count sessions per day by type
daily_counts <- sched_clean |>
  count(date, session_type) |>
  # Fill in any missing combinations with 0
  complete(date, session_type, fill = list(n = 0))

# Mark opening ceremony date
opening <- as.Date("2026-02-06")

ggplot(daily_counts, aes(x = date, y = n, fill = session_type)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = opening, linetype = "dashed",
    color = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text", x = opening + 0.3, y = Inf, label = "Opening\nCeremony",
    hjust = 0, vjust = 1.3, size = 3, color = "grey30"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Training"               = "#b8d4e3",
      "Competition (non-medal)" = "#4a90d9",
      "Medal event"             = "#d4af37"
    ),
    name = "Session type"
  ) +
  scale_x_date(
    date_labels = "%b %d", date_breaks = "2 days",
    expand = expansion(add = 0.5)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  labs(
    title = "Medal events surge in the final week while training fades after opening days",
    subtitle = "Number of scheduled sessions per day by type, Milano Cortina 2026",
    x = NULL, y = "Number of sessions",
    caption = "Source: Milano Cortina 2026 Schedule · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 6 · Zhijun He · 2026-02-11"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    plot.caption = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank()
  )







The schedule has a clear dramatic arc. The first few days (Feb 4–5) are almost entirely training sessions as athletes complete mandatory official runs. After the Opening Ceremony on February 6, competition begins in earnest. The gold bars of medal events grow steadily thicker through the middle of the Games, then peak in the final days as multiple disciplines hold their finals simultaneously. The blue competition sessions remain relatively steady throughout, providing a constant backdrop of qualification rounds, preliminary matches, and heats.
crumbs: 5  The Rhythm of the Rings

title: The Rhythm of the Rings
section: Visualization 2: Ice Hockey and Curling Fill the Schedule; Alpine Skiing and Biathlon Deliver the Most Gold
text:
Visualization 2: Ice Hockey and Curling Fill the Schedule; Alpine Skiing and Biathlon Deliver the Most Gold

Show code# Compute share of total sessions and share of medal events by discipline
discipline_summary <- sched_clean |>
  group_by(discipline_name) |>
  summarise(
    total_sessions = n(),
    medal_sessions = sum(is_medal_event),
    training_sessions = sum(is_training),
    competition_sessions = total_sessions - training_sessions,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    pct_total = total_sessions / sum(total_sessions),
    pct_medal = medal_sessions / sum(medal_sessions)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_sessions))

# Reshape for a paired bar chart
disc_long <- discipline_summary |>
  select(discipline_name, `Share of all sessions` = pct_total,
         `Share of medal events` = pct_medal) |>
  pivot_longer(-discipline_name, names_to = "measure", values_to = "share") |>
  mutate(
    discipline_name = fct_reorder(discipline_name, share, .fun = max, .desc = FALSE)
  )

ggplot(disc_long, aes(x = share, y = discipline_name, fill = measure)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.6) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Share of all sessions" = "#4a90d9",
      "Share of medal events" = "#d4af37"
    ),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Ice Hockey and Curling dominate the schedule, but Alpine Skiing and Biathlon deliver the most medals",
    subtitle = "Each discipline's share of total sessions vs. share of medal events",
    x = "Share", y = NULL,
    caption = "Source: Milano Cortina 2026 Schedule · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 6 · Zhijun He · 2026-02-11"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 10.5),
    plot.caption = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )







This comparison reveals a key asymmetry in the Olympic schedule. Ice Hockey and Curling account for an outsized share of total sessions because their tournament formats require dozens of preliminary matches, but each discipline awards only a handful of medals. By contrast, Alpine Skiing and Biathlon pack many medal events into fewer sessions—each race is a standalone final. For medal-focused viewers, this chart is a guide to where the action is: if you want to see gold decided, follow the skiing and biathlon; if you want the drama of extended tournament play, tune into hockey and curling.
crumbs: 5  The Rhythm of the Rings

title: The Rhythm of the Rings
text:
Takeaway
The Milano Cortina 2026 schedule is deliberately structured to build momentum. Early days are spent on training and qualification, the middle stretch introduces a steady stream of medal events, and the final weekend delivers a crescendo of gold-medal moments. But the sports contributing to this rhythm play very different roles: tournament-format sports like Ice Hockey and Curling fill the calendar with sessions, while individual-event sports like Alpine Skiing and Biathlon are the primary engines of the medal count. Knowing this distinction helps fans plan their viewing—or, if you are in Minneapolis in February, a good excuse to stay inside and watch all of it.
crumbs: 5  The Rhythm of the Rings

title: The Pastoral Pivot
text:
Background
New Zealand’s pastoral identity is inseparable from the image of its hillsides white with sheep. For much of the twentieth century, wool and lamb exports underpinned the national economy, and by 1982 the country grazed nearly 70 million sheep—roughly 22 for every person alive. That ratio has since collapsed: today there are about 4.5 sheep per person. Yet New Zealand’s farms remain as productive as ever. The question is not whether the countryside changed, but what replaced the sheep.
This 90-year statistical record from Stats NZ captures the breadth of that transition: livestock counts, crop yields, fertiliser use, and the total area under farm management. Together, these series trace not merely a decline in one sector but an entire agricultural reorientation—from extensive wool-and-lamb production toward dairy intensification and higher-value cropping.

title: The Pastoral Pivot
text:
Motivation & Research Question
The sheep narrative is well-known, but it risks framing New Zealand’s agricultural history as a story of loss. A richer reading asks: After the sheep-farming peak of 1982, which sectors contracted and which expanded—and does the data reveal a coherent “pastoral pivot” toward a new agricultural model?

title: The Pastoral Pivot
text:
Data Preparation

Show code# Focal measures chosen for coverage across most of the 90-year span
focal <- c(
  "Total Sheep",
  "Rising 1 Year Old Dairy Heifers and Heifer Calves",
  "Wheat (yield)",
  "Barley (yield)",
  "Total All Fertilisers"
)

labels_clean <- c(
  "Total Sheep"                                          = "Total Sheep",
  "Rising 1 Year Old Dairy Heifers and Heifer Calves"   = "Dairy Heifers (cattle)",
  "Wheat (yield)"                                        = "Wheat Yield (tonnes)",
  "Barley (yield)"                                       = "Barley Yield (tonnes)",
  "Total All Fertilisers"                                = "Fertiliser Use (tonnes)"
)

ds_clean <- dataset |>
  filter(measure %in% focal) |>
  mutate(
    measure_label = recode(measure, !!!labels_clean),
    measure_label = factor(
      measure_label,
      levels = c(
        "Total Sheep",
        "Dairy Heifers (cattle)",
        "Wheat Yield (tonnes)",
        "Barley Yield (tonnes)",
        "Fertiliser Use (tonnes)"
      )
    )
  )

# Index each series to its value at 1982 (sheep peak year)
ds_indexed <- ds_clean |>
  group_by(measure_label) |>
  filter(!is.na(value)) |>
  mutate(
    base_value = value[year_ended_june == 1982],
    index = value / base_value * 100
  ) |>
  ungroup()

cat("Years spanned:", min(ds_clean$year_ended_june), "–",
    max(ds_clean$year_ended_june), "\n")

Years spanned: 1935 – 2024 

Show codecat("Measures in analysis:", n_distinct(ds_clean$measure_label), "\n")

Measures in analysis: 5

title: The Pastoral Pivot
section: Visualization 1: The Pastoral Pivot — All Sectors Indexed to 1982
text:
Visualization 1: The Pastoral Pivot — All Sectors Indexed to 1982

Show codepalette <- c(
  "Total Sheep"            = "#8B7355",
  "Dairy Heifers (cattle)" = "#4a90d9",
  "Wheat Yield (tonnes)"   = "#d4af37",
  "Barley Yield (tonnes)"  = "#6aaa64",
  "Fertiliser Use (tonnes)"= "#e07b54"
)

# Key annotation points
annotations <- tibble(
  year = c(1982, 1984),
  index = c(100, 80),
  label = c("1982\nSheep peak\n(70M head)", "1984\nMarket\nliberalisation"),
  measure_label = c("Total Sheep", "Total Sheep")
)

ggplot(
  ds_indexed |> filter(year_ended_june >= 1957),
  aes(x = year_ended_june, y = index, colour = measure_label)
) +
  # Shade the post-reform era
  annotate(
    "rect", xmin = 1984, xmax = Inf,
    ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
    fill = "grey92", alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text", x = 1985, y = 330,
    label = "Post-1984\nreform era",
    hjust = 0, vjust = 1, size = 3.2, color = "grey50"
  ) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1982, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1984, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "solid",
             colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.35) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = palette, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1960, 2025, 10)) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = function(x) paste0(x),
    name   = "Index (1982 = 100)"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Sheep collapsed after 1982; fertiliser use and dairy heifers have held or grown",
    subtitle = "Each series indexed to its 1982 value (= 100). Shaded area marks the post-market-reform era.",
    x        = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: Stats NZ via figure.nz · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 7 · Zhijun He · 2026-02-25"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    legend.position = "top",
    legend.key.width = unit(1.2, "cm"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )







The most striking feature of this chart is the asymmetry of the post-1984 era. New Zealand’s 1984 economic reforms (the “Rogernomics” liberalisation) ended the agricultural subsidies that had kept sheep-farming artificially profitable. Total sheep numbers dropped from 70 million in 1982 to roughly 28 million today—a fall to about 40 on this index. But fertiliser use has remained near its 1982 level, suggesting that the land itself did not become less intensively managed; it was repurposed. Dairy heifers, a proxy for the dairy sector’s herd-building, have held up considerably better than sheep, consistent with New Zealand’s well-documented turn toward milk solids for export markets. Wheat and barley yields have fluctuated with global commodity prices but show no comparable structural collapse.

title: The Pastoral Pivot
section: Visualization 2: A Century in Two Snapshots — The Crop Mix Before and After the Pivot
text:
Visualization 2: A Century in Two Snapshots — The Crop Mix Before and After the Pivot

Show code# Compare grain crop yields at two anchor points: 1980 and most recent year
crop_measures <- c("Wheat (yield)", "Barley (yield)", "Oats (yield)", "Maize (yield)")

crops <- dataset |>
  filter(measure %in% crop_measures) |>
  mutate(
    crop = str_extract(measure, "^[^(]+") |> str_trim(),
    era  = case_when(
      year_ended_june %in% 1978:1982 ~ "Late-1970s/early-1980s\n(pre-reform baseline)",
      year_ended_june %in% 2018:2022 ~ "2018–2022\n(recent average)",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(era)) |>
  group_by(crop, era) |>
  summarise(mean_value = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(
    crop = factor(crop, levels = c("Wheat", "Barley", "Oats", "Maize")),
    era  = factor(era, levels = c(
      "Late-1970s/early-1980s\n(pre-reform baseline)",
      "2018–2022\n(recent average)"
    ))
  )

ggplot(crops, aes(x = crop, y = mean_value / 1000, fill = era)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(round(mean_value / 1000, 0), "k")),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.7),
    vjust = -0.5, size = 3.2, colour = "grey30"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Late-1970s/early-1980s\n(pre-reform baseline)" = "#b8d4e3",
      "2018–2022\n(recent average)"                   = "#d4af37"
    ),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = function(x) paste0(x, "k"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12)),
    name   = "Average annual yield (thousand tonnes)"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Barley holds; wheat and oats have sharply declined since the pre-reform era",
    subtitle = "Mean annual grain yield averaged over two five-year windows, thousands of tonnes",
    x        = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: Stats NZ via figure.nz · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 7 · Zhijun He · 2026-02-25"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 10.5),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank()
  )







Among arable crops, the post-reform transition has been equally stark. Wheat and oats have both contracted significantly since their mid-century peaks, with wheat yields roughly halving between the late-1970s baseline and the 2018–2022 average. Barley is the notable exception, maintaining its output, consistent with its role as a feed crop for the expanding dairy sector. Maize, though small in absolute volume, has roughly held. This pattern reinforces the interpretation: the crop mix pivoted to support the animal that replaced the sheep—not a crop export economy, but dairy.

title: The Pastoral Pivot
text:
Takeaway
The data tell a coherent story of deliberate transformation rather than simple decline. New Zealand’s 1984 market liberalisation removed the subsidies propping up an overstocked sheep industry and allowed the sector to reorient toward its comparative advantage in dairy. Sheep numbers collapsed, wheat and oats retreated, but fertiliser inputs and dairy herd-building have persisted—the farms did not become less productive, they became differently productive. Ninety years of agricultural statistics reveal that the hillsides are still intensively managed; they have simply traded wool for milk solids, and lamb chops for a latte.

title: The Few and the Funded
text:
Background
Science Foundation Ireland (SFI) was established in 2000 as Ireland’s first dedicated science funding agency, modelled on the US National Science Foundation, and dissolved on 31 July 2024 when it was absorbed into a new, broader research body. In its 24-year lifespan it committed billions of euros to research in STEM fields across Irish higher education. SFI’s founding mandate was explicitly selective: it would back researchers most likely to produce world-class outputs, rather than distributing small grants widely. That competitive philosophy raises a natural empirical question about how funding was actually distributed across the landscape of Irish universities.
Ireland’s higher education system is dominated by a handful of large research-intensive institutions—Trinity College Dublin, University College Dublin, University College Cork, University of Galway, and University of Limerick—alongside specialised institutes such as the Tyndall National Institute. Whether SFI’s commitment to excellence led to increasing concentration of funding in the top institutions, or whether it spread more evenly as more universities built research capacity, is a question the data can answer.
crumbs: 7  The Few and the Funded

title: The Few and the Funded
text:
Motivation & Research Question
A funding agency that declares itself selective has an internal tension: it can either track genuine excellence wherever it emerges (dispersing grants over time) or it can reinforce existing capacity advantages at established institutions (concentrating grants). Over a 24-year lifecycle, did SFI’s grant portfolio become more or less concentrated, and which institutions drove the dynamics at the top and bottom of the distribution?
Research question: Did SFI funding become more concentrated in a small number of institutions over its lifespan, and which research bodies anchored the top tier throughout?
crumbs: 7  The Few and the Funded

title: The Few and the Funded
text:
Data Preparation

Show codegrants_clean <- sfi_grants |>
  mutate(
    year = lubridate::year(start_date),
    # Shorten institution names for plotting
    institution = case_when(
      str_detect(research_body, "Trinity")          ~ "TCD",
      str_detect(research_body, "University College Dublin") ~ "UCD",
      str_detect(research_body, "University College Cork")   ~ "UCC",
      str_detect(research_body, "Galway")           ~ "Galway",
      str_detect(research_body, "Limerick")         ~ "UL",
      str_detect(research_body, "Dublin City")      ~ "DCU",
      str_detect(research_body, "Maynooth")         ~ "MU",
      str_detect(research_body, "Tyndall")          ~ "Tyndall",
      str_detect(research_body, "Technological University Dublin") ~ "TU Dublin",
      str_detect(research_body, "RCSI")             ~ "RCSI",
      TRUE                                           ~ "Other"
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(current_total_commitment), !is.na(year))

cat("Total grants:", nrow(grants_clean), "\n")

Total grants: 7269 

Show codecat("Years spanned:", min(grants_clean$year), "–", max(grants_clean$year), "\n")

Years spanned: 2001 – 2025 

Show codecat("Total committed (€M):", round(sum(grants_clean$current_total_commitment) / 1e6, 1), "\n")

Total committed (€M): 4178.7 

Show codecat("Institutions:", n_distinct(grants_clean$research_body), "\n")

Institutions: 139
crumbs: 7  The Few and the Funded

title: The Few and the Funded
section: Visualization 1: Annual Funding Commitment and Its Concentration Among Institutions
text:
Visualization 1: Annual Funding Commitment and Its Concentration Among Institutions

Show code# Top institutions by total funding across all years
top_institutions <- grants_clean |>
  group_by(institution) |>
  summarise(total = sum(current_total_commitment), .groups = "drop") |>
  slice_max(total, n = 7) |>
  pull(institution)

# Recode everything else as "Other"
grants_plot <- grants_clean |>
  mutate(
    inst_grp = if_else(institution %in% top_institutions, institution, "Other"),
    inst_grp = factor(
      inst_grp,
      levels = c(rev(top_institutions[top_institutions != "Other"]), "Other")
    )
  )

annual_stack <- grants_plot |>
  group_by(year, inst_grp) |>
  summarise(funding_m = sum(current_total_commitment) / 1e6, .groups = "drop")

# Colour palette (TCD gets a warm gold; UCD blue; rest sequential)
pal <- c(
  "TCD"     = "#d4af37",
  "UCD"     = "#4a90d9",
  "UCC"     = "#5aab6d",
  "Tyndall" = "#7b5ea7",
  "Galway"  = "#e07b54",
  "DCU"     = "#3c9fa3",
  "MU"      = "#c75f8c",
  "Other"   = "#c8c8c8"
)

ggplot(annual_stack, aes(x = year, y = funding_m, fill = inst_grp)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.85) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal, name = "Institution",
                    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2024, 2)) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_dollar(prefix = "€", suffix = "M"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "SFI annual grant commitments, 2000–2024: TCD and UCD anchor the top tier throughout",
    subtitle = "Total value of grants starting in each year, by receiving institution (€ millions)",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Grant commitments (€M)",
    caption  = "Source: Ireland Open Data Portal · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 8 · Zhijun He · 2026-02-25"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    legend.position   = "right",
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )







Two features stand out immediately. First, annual commitments were not steady: there is a pronounced mid-period surge roughly between 2008 and 2016, likely reflecting the Celtic Tiger recovery period and Ireland’s strategic bet on science as an economic engine post-financial crisis. Second, Trinity College Dublin (TCD, gold) and University College Dublin (UCD, blue) occupy the top of nearly every annual bar. The concentration at these two institutions is consistent year after year, regardless of whether total funding was high or low.
crumbs: 7  The Few and the Funded

title: The Few and the Funded
text:
Visualization 2: Herfindahl-Style Concentration Over Time

Show code# Compute an annual share-based concentration measure:
# sum of squared market shares (HHI), then scale 0–1 for readability
hhi_annual <- grants_clean |>
  group_by(year) |>
  mutate(total_year = sum(current_total_commitment)) |>
  group_by(year, institution) |>
  summarise(
    share = sum(current_total_commitment) / first(total_year),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(
    hhi        = sum(share^2),
    n_inst     = n_distinct(institution),
    top2_share = sum(sort(share, decreasing = TRUE)[1:min(2, n())]),
    .groups    = "drop"
  )

# Dual-axis: HHI left, top-2 share right
ggplot(hhi_annual, aes(x = year)) +
  geom_ribbon(
    aes(ymin = 0, ymax = hhi),
    fill = "#d4af37", alpha = 0.25
  ) +
  geom_line(aes(y = hhi, colour = "HHI (left axis)"),
            linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = top2_share / 3, colour = "Top-2 share ÷ 3 (right axis)"),
            linewidth = 1.0, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(
      "HHI (left axis)"            = "#d4af37",
      "Top-2 share ÷ 3 (right axis)" = "#4a90d9"
    ),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name     = "Herfindahl–Hirschman Index (HHI)",
    labels   = number_format(accuracy = 0.01),
    sec.axis = sec_axis(
      ~ . * 3,
      name   = "Top-2 institution share",
      labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)
    ),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2024, 2)) +
  annotate(
    "text", x = 2001.5, y = 0.48,
    label = "Early years: few grants,\nhigh concentration",
    size = 3.2, colour = "grey40", hjust = 0
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text", x = 2014, y = 0.21,
    label = "Maturation: more\ninstitutions compete",
    size = 3.2, colour = "grey40", hjust = 0
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Funding concentration fell as SFI matured, but TCD + UCD still capture ~40–50% most years",
    subtitle = "HHI and top-2 institution share of annual grant commitments, 2000–2024",
    x        = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: Ireland Open Data Portal · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 8 · Zhijun He · 2026-02-25"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 10.5),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )







The Herfindahl–Hirschman Index (HHI) is a standard measure of market concentration: a value of 1.0 means one institution receives everything; values below 0.10 indicate broad dispersion. SFI’s early years (2000–2003) show very high HHI values, an artifact of the small number of initial grants and the fact that a handful of large Principal Investigator awards went almost entirely to TCD, UCD, and UCC. As the portfolio grew through the 2000s and 2010s, more institutions entered the picture and concentration fell. The dashed blue line confirms the same story from a different angle: TCD and UCD together captured over 60% of commitments in some early years, declining to around 40–45% in the mature phase. The answer to the research question is therefore nuanced — SFI did become less concentrated over time as a system, but the same two institutions remained structurally dominant throughout, never falling below roughly 35% of annual commitments combined.
crumbs: 7  The Few and the Funded

title: The Few and the Funded
text:
Takeaway
Science Foundation Ireland’s 24-year grant history reveals a dual narrative. At the level of the whole system, funding dispersed as the agency matured: more institutions competed successfully and the extreme concentration of the early 2000s gave way to a broader distribution. But at the top of the hierarchy, Trinity College Dublin and University College Dublin proved remarkably durable. Their combined share never collapsed; it moderated. This pattern is consistent with a model in which competitive excellence funding does, over time, allow challengers to enter, but cannot easily displace the compounding advantage of institutions that were already well-resourced at the outset. When SFI closed its doors in 2024, the landscape it left behind was more plural than the one it inherited — but still decisively shaped by the same two institutions that dominated its founding awards.
crumbs: 7  The Few and the Funded

title: A Sex-Specific Penalty
text:
Background
Golem Grad is a small rocky island in Lake Prespa, on the border between North Macedonia, Albania, and Greece. It holds one of the densest known populations of Hermann’s tortoises (Testudo hermanni) anywhere in Europe, and over sixteen years of capture–recapture work Ljiljana Tomović and colleagues documented something unusual: the island’s sex ratio is extraordinarily male-biased, and the island’s females are in trouble. The 2026 Ecology Letters paper by the Tomović group reports that island females are smaller, reproduce less frequently, produce smaller clutches, and die younger than females on the neighbouring mainland. The New York Times covered the paper under the headline “Constant Sexual Aggression Drives Female Tortoises to Walk Off Cliffs.” The authors project, from sixteen years of demography, that the last island female will die in 2083.
The mechanism proposed is specific. Island males so vastly outnumber females that every female is repeatedly pursued and mounted; copulatory injuries are severe; harassed females flee onto the plateau’s sheer limestone edges and fall. Under this account, the cost of island life is not evenly distributed between the sexes. It is borne, almost entirely, by females.
TidyTuesday has now released two of the study’s underlying datasets: a body-condition file covering sixteen years of individual measurements, and a clutch-size file covering reproductive output.

title: A Sex-Specific Penalty
text:
Motivation & Research Question
A “harsh-island” story and a “male-harassment” story both predict that island tortoises will be in worse condition than mainland ones. They diverge on one specific point: whether the penalty falls on both sexes, or on females only. If harsh environment is the driver, island males should look about as thin as island females. If male harassment is the driver, island males should look fine and the penalty should concentrate in the female half of the population.
Research question: Is the island condition penalty sex-specific — concentrated in females, as the paper’s harassment mechanism predicts, rather than borne equally by both sexes?

title: A Sex-Specific Penalty
text:
Data Preparation

Show code# Body condition: keep one row per individual-year-season, filter implausible BCI
# (a handful of entries have BCI > 8.5, which sits far above the distribution and
# is almost certainly a recording error).
body_clean <- body |>
  filter(!is.na(body_condition_index),
         !is.na(sex),
         between(body_condition_index, 3.5, 8.5)) |>
  mutate(
    sex      = recode(sex, m = "Male", f = "Female"),
    locality = factor(locality, levels = c("Konjsko", "Beach", "Plateau"))
  )

# Clutch: add a locality label that flags mainland vs island clearly
clutch_clean <- clutch |>
  filter(!is.na(eggs), !is.na(body_mass_grams)) |>
  mutate(
    region = if_else(locality == "Konjsko", "Mainland (Konjsko)", "Island (Golem Grad)"),
    locality = factor(locality, levels = c("Konjsko", "Beach", "Plateau"))
  )

cat("Body-condition records:", nrow(body_clean), "\n")

Body-condition records: 9806 

Show codecat("Distinct individuals:  ", n_distinct(body_clean$individual), "\n")

Distinct individuals:   1985 

Show codecat("Years covered:         ", min(body_clean$year), "–", max(body_clean$year), "\n")

Years covered:          2008 – 2023 

Show codecat("Localities in body data:", paste(levels(droplevels(body_clean$locality)),
                                      collapse = ", "), "\n")

Localities in body data: Konjsko, Beach, Plateau 

Show codecat("\nClutch records:        ", nrow(clutch_clean), "\n")


Clutch records:         53 

Show codecat("Localities in clutch:  ", paste(levels(droplevels(clutch_clean$locality)),
                                     collapse = ", "), "\n")

Localities in clutch:   Konjsko, Beach, Plateau

title: A Sex-Specific Penalty
section: Visualization 1: Body Condition by Sex and Locality
text:
Visualization 1: Body Condition by Sex and Locality

Show code# Drop any unused locality levels so the plot only shows what's in the data
body_plot <- body_clean |>
  mutate(locality = droplevels(locality))

pal <- c(
  "Konjsko" = "#3c9fa3",
  "Beach"   = "#e07b54",
  "Plateau" = "#d4af37"
)

# Group medians for reference annotation
medians <- body_plot |>
  group_by(sex, locality) |>
  summarise(med = median(body_condition_index), n = n(), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(body_plot, aes(x = locality, y = body_condition_index, fill = locality)) +
  geom_violin(colour = NA, alpha = 0.55, trim = TRUE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.18, outlier.shape = NA, fill = "white",
               colour = "grey25", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_point(data = medians, aes(x = locality, y = med),
             shape = 18, size = 3, colour = "grey15", inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ sex) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal, guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(4, 8.5, 0.5)) +
  labs(
    title    = "The island condition penalty concentrates in females",
    subtitle = "Body condition index by locality and sex, Lake Prespa tortoises 2008–2023",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Body condition index",
    caption  = "Source: Tomović et al. 2026, Ecology Letters · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 9 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    strip.text    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank()
  )







The figure shows the distribution of the body condition index (BCI) for each sex–locality combination, with violin shapes giving the shape of the distribution and boxplots summarising the median and interquartile range. The male panel shows condition distributions that sit essentially on top of one another: Beach, Plateau, and (where observed) Konjsko males occupy the same range. Male condition is not noticeably worse on the island than on the mainland. The female panel tells a different story. The island distributions (Beach and Plateau) are shifted visibly downward relative to the Konjsko females, and the lower tail of the Beach distribution in particular extends further down than anything seen in the male panel. The data cannot prove that harassment is the causal mechanism, but the pattern matches the specific prediction of that mechanism: condition loss that is concentrated on one side of the sex divide rather than distributed across the island population as a whole.

title: A Sex-Specific Penalty
section: Visualization 2: Reproductive Output Across the Island–Mainland Gradient
text:
Visualization 2: Reproductive Output Across the Island–Mainland Gradient

Show codeggplot(clutch_clean,
       aes(x = body_mass_grams, y = eggs,
           colour = locality, fill = locality)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.12, linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_point(size = 2.6, alpha = 0.85, stroke = 0) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal, name = "Locality") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_number(big.mark = ","),
                     breaks = seq(500, 3000, 500)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = 1:12) +
  annotate("text", x = 2550, y = 10.7,
           label = "Mainland females:\nbigger, and more eggs",
           size = 3.2, colour = "#3c9fa3", hjust = 0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = 750, y = 1.4,
           label = "Island females:\nsmaller, and fewer eggs",
           size = 3.2, colour = "#e07b54", hjust = 0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  labs(
    title    = "Island females are smaller — and lay fewer eggs per clutch",
    subtitle = "Clutch size against body mass, Hermann's tortoises, Lake Prespa region",
    x        = "Body mass (grams)",
    y        = "Eggs per clutch",
    caption  = "Source: Tomović et al. 2026, Ecology Letters · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 9 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )







The second view takes the question from “what do females look like” to “what do they produce.” Each point is a single observed clutch. Two features emerge. The most obvious is position: Konjsko (mainland) females cluster in the upper right of the plot at 1,400–2,900 grams, producing 4–11 eggs per clutch, while the island females sit in the lower left at 500–1,700 grams, producing 1–5 eggs. The gap is not marginal — island clutches are on the order of half the size of mainland clutches, and island females themselves are roughly half the body mass of mainland females of the same species. The second, subtler feature is that within each locality the body-mass-to-eggs relationship has a similar positive slope. This suggests that the island penalty is not a different reproductive physiology so much as the result of island females being prevented, in the ordinary course of their lives, from ever reaching the body mass at which mainland females are reproducing.

title: A Sex-Specific Penalty
text:
Takeaway
The two panels together describe a single population-level pattern. Island males show condition distributions essentially indistinguishable from mainland males where both are observed; island females do not. Island females are lighter, in poorer condition, and produce clutches roughly half the size of mainland clutches of the same species. The cost of Golem Grad is real and it is falling, overwhelmingly, on one side of the sex divide.
None of this establishes causation on its own — capture-recapture data cannot, by construction, rule out confounders like differences in habitat productivity between the mainland and the island plateau. But a generic “harsh island” hypothesis would predict a penalty shared across both sexes, and that is not what the data show. The pattern in the open data matches the specific footprint of the paper’s proposed mechanism: a condition loss and a fecundity loss that are sex-specific rather than population-wide. Whether or not Tomović and colleagues are right that the last island female dies in 2083, the asymmetry they describe is already visible in the sixteen years of measurements they have left behind.

title: Words That Mean Nothing
text:
Background
In 1964, Sherman Kent, then chair of the CIA’s Board of National Estimates, circulated an internal memo titled “Words of Estimative Probability.” Kent had become alarmed by how wildly his own colleagues disagreed about what standard intelligence vocabulary meant. He had surveyed a group of senior analysts, asking each to put a percentage on phrases like “serious possibility” and “highly unlikely.” For “serious possibility,” one analyst reported 20 percent; another reported 80 percent. Kent’s worry was not that analysts were sloppy — it was that the vocabulary of estimation, which the intelligence community relied on to convey risk to policymakers, was systematically failing at the job.
The response, over decades, was to build calibrated language. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change now pegs “very likely” to 90–100 percent and “likely” to 66–100 percent in its reports. The US National Intelligence Council publishes its probability ladder alongside every major estimate. These institutional fixes assume the problem is real: that untethered probability phrases fail to coordinate interpretation across readers. Adam Kucharski’s online quiz (2024–2026, approximately 5,200 respondents) lets us check that assumption at scale. Each respondent assigned a 0–100 percent value to nineteen phrases in a random order. The question this dataset invites is whether, sixty years after Kent, ordinary usage actually conveys probability — or whether some phrases are so unmoored that saying them communicates essentially nothing.

title: Words That Mean Nothing
text:
Motivation & Research Question
There are two distinct ways a probability phrase can fail to communicate. It can be imprecise — people agree on roughly where it sits, but with wide disagreement on exactly how high or low. Or it can be redundant — people place it at a precise location, but several other phrases live in the same spot, so using one rather than another conveys no extra information about probability. A phrase that is both imprecise and redundant is, in Kent’s phrase, “a foot in each camp”: neither specific enough to pin a number to, nor distinct enough to justify its existence in the vocabulary.
Research question: Which probability phrases actually convey distinct information, and which are so imprecise or redundant that a reader learns nothing from them?

title: Words That Mean Nothing
text:
Data Preparation

Show codejudg_clean <- judgements |>
  filter(!is.na(probability), between(probability, 0, 100))

# Per-phrase summary: median and quartiles
phrase_summary <- judg_clean |>
  group_by(term) |>
  summarise(
    n      = n(),
    median = median(probability),
    q25    = quantile(probability, 0.25),
    q75    = quantile(probability, 0.75),
    iqr    = q75 - q25,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(median) |>
  mutate(term = factor(term, levels = term))

cat("Total judgements:    ", nrow(judg_clean), "\n")

Total judgements:     98306 

Show codecat("Distinct respondents:", n_distinct(judg_clean$response_id), "\n")

Distinct respondents: 5174 

Show codecat("Phrases evaluated:   ", n_distinct(judg_clean$term), "\n\n")

Phrases evaluated:    19 

Show codecat("Widest IQR (most imprecise phrases):\n")

Widest IQR (most imprecise phrases):

Show codephrase_summary |> slice_max(iqr, n = 5) |>
  mutate(across(c(median, q25, q75, iqr), round)) |>
  print()

# A tibble: 7 × 6
  term                      n median   q25   q75   iqr
  <fct>                 <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 Realistic Possibility  5174     60    40    75    35
2 Could Happen           5174     40    25    50    25
3 Might Happen           5174     40    25    50    25
4 May Happen             5174     40    30    51    21
5 Improbable             5174     10     5    20    15
6 Unlikely               5174     20    10    25    15
7 Probable               5174     75    65    80    15

title: Words That Mean Nothing
text:
Visualization 1: The Probability Spectrum

Show code# Join the ordering back to the raw judgements for plotting
judg_plot <- judg_clean |>
  mutate(term = factor(term, levels = levels(phrase_summary$term)))

# Colour IQR width continuously: tight = gold, wide = blue
ggplot(phrase_summary, aes(y = term)) +
  # horizontal reference grid at 0, 25, 50, 75, 100
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(0, 25, 50, 75, 100),
             colour = "grey88", linewidth = 0.3) +
  # individual responses as jittered dots behind
  geom_jitter(data = judg_plot,
              aes(x = probability, y = term),
              height = 0.22, alpha = 0.03, colour = "grey50", size = 0.4) +
  # IQR bar
  geom_linerange(aes(xmin = q25, xmax = q75, colour = iqr),
                 linewidth = 3.5) +
  # median point
  geom_point(aes(x = median), shape = 21, fill = "white",
             colour = "grey15", size = 2.6, stroke = 0.9) +
  scale_colour_gradient(low = "#d4af37", high = "#4a90d9",
                        name = "IQR width\n(disagreement)",
                        breaks = c(10, 25, 40)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 25),
                     labels = label_percent(scale = 1),
                     limits = c(0, 100),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0.01))) +
  labs(
    title    = "The edges of the probability scale are tight; the middle is anything goes",
    subtitle = "Each phrase ordered by median estimate, with interquartile range and individual responses",
    x        = "Assigned probability",
    y        = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: Kucharski CAPphrase quiz · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 10 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y  = element_text(size = 10.5),
    legend.position = "right"
  )







The plot orders the nineteen phrases by median rating, so the vertical axis reads as a rough probability ladder from “Almost No Chance” at the bottom to “Will Happen” at the top. Each phrase is represented by its interquartile range (the coloured bar) and its median (the white point), with individual responses shown as the faint grey cloud behind. Two patterns stand out. The ends of the ladder are tight: “Almost No Chance,” “Highly Unlikely,” “Will Happen,” and “Almost Certain” all have narrow interquartile bands concentrated near the edges of the scale, coloured gold. Respondents broadly agree on what these mean. The middle of the ladder is wide: “Could Happen,” “Might Happen,” “May Happen,” and “Realistic Possibility” all have interquartile bands spanning twenty-five to thirty-five percentage points, coloured deep blue. A reader who hears “might happen” cannot recover even a rough probability band from the phrase — its middle 50 percent of interpretations stretches from about 20 percent to about 55 percent.

title: Words That Mean Nothing
text:
Visualization 2: The Synonym Problem

Show code# Three "synonym clusters" of phrases at similar positions on the ladder.
# Defined by hand from viz 1: low / mid-high / high bands that sit in the same
# median neighbourhood.

synonym_groups <- tribble(
  ~term,                    ~cluster,
  "Improbable",             "Low end:\n~10%",
  "Highly Unlikely",        "Low end:\n~10%",
  "Remote Chance",          "Low end:\n~10%",
  "Realistic Possibility",  "Middle:\n~50%",
  "About Even",             "Middle:\n~50%",
  "Better than Even",       "Middle:\n~50%",
  "Likely",                 "High end:\n~75%",
  "Probable",               "High end:\n~75%",
  "Very Good Chance",       "High end:\n~75%"
)

overlap_df <- judg_clean |>
  inner_join(synonym_groups, by = "term") |>
  mutate(
    cluster = factor(cluster,
                     levels = c("Low end:\n~10%", "Middle:\n~50%", "High end:\n~75%"))
  )

# Per-cluster palette: three phrases share one hue family
cluster_colours <- c(
  "Improbable"            = "#bb7a3a",  "Highly Unlikely"       = "#d4af37",  "Remote Chance"         = "#e9cf73",
  "Realistic Possibility" = "#2a6b7a",  "About Even"            = "#4a90d9",  "Better than Even"      = "#7fb3e4",
  "Likely"                = "#7b5ea7",  "Probable"              = "#a078c8",  "Very Good Chance"      = "#c4a3e0"
)

ggplot(overlap_df, aes(x = probability, fill = term, colour = term)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.55, bw = 4) +
  facet_wrap(~ cluster, scales = "free_y", nrow = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = cluster_colours, name = NULL) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = cluster_colours, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, 25),
                     labels = label_percent(scale = 1),
                     limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Phrases at the same rung of the ladder are nearly interchangeable",
    subtitle = "Distribution of assigned probability for three 'synonym clusters' — low, middle, high",
    x        = "Assigned probability",
    y        = "Density of responses",
    caption  = "Source: Kucharski CAPphrase quiz · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 10 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    strip.text    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10.5),
    legend.position = "top",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  ) +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(nrow = 1, override.aes = list(alpha = 0.7)))







The second view answers the redundancy question. Within each panel, three phrases that landed at roughly the same position on the ladder are plotted against one another. If the phrases carried distinct information, the density curves would sit side by side with visible separation. They do not. In the low panel, “Improbable,” “Highly Unlikely,” and “Remote Chance” produce densities that peak in essentially the same neighbourhood near 10 percent and overlap almost entirely. In the middle panel, “Realistic Possibility” drifts slightly higher than “About Even” and “Better than Even,” but the three distributions share the bulk of their mass. The high panel is the starkest: “Likely,” “Probable,” and “Very Good Chance” produce densities that are close to identical. A reader who is told that an outcome is “likely” versus “probable” receives, from the two words alone, no recoverable information about which the speaker judged more plausible.

title: Words That Mean Nothing
text:
Takeaway
Sixty years after Sherman Kent warned that untethered probability vocabulary would fail to communicate, the pattern he documented internally at CIA is visible at scale in the open data. The edges of the scale work: phrases that anchor near certainty or near impossibility produce tight, consistent interpretations. The middle does not, and it fails in two distinct ways. First, the middle phrases are imprecise — “might happen” or “could happen” does not narrow the probability to anything a listener can act on. Second, at several positions on the ladder the phrases are redundant — “likely,” “probable,” and “very good chance” live in the same place and no listener can tell which a speaker meant by choosing one rather than another.
None of this says the words are meaningless. They clearly convey direction (high, medium, low), and some are tight enough to convey a useful band. But the claim implicit in ordinary usage — that picking “likely” rather than “probable,” or “may happen” rather than “might happen,” communicates a meaningful shade of probability — is not supported by how respondents actually interpret them. The institutional response of the IPCC and the National Intelligence Council, which is to bolt numerical ranges onto a small calibrated vocabulary rather than trust the phrases to do the work, looks, on this evidence, like the right response to a real problem rather than an overcautious one.

title: Below the Waterline
text:
Background
Salmon farming is one of Norway’s largest export industries. Roughly 400 million farmed Atlantic salmon are grown in open-net pens along the country’s western and northern coasts at any given time, and the sector’s health and welfare record has become a visible public issue. The Norwegian Veterinary Institute now publishes monthly mortality and loss statistics from every licensed farm through its Laksetap (literally “salmon loss”) portal, and the Institute’s annual Fish Health Report frames the governance question that gives this dataset its stakes: the industry aspires to a 5 percent annual mortality ceiling, a figure repeatedly cited in policy and industry communications, but recent annual averages have sat closer to 15 percent. The transparency regime is serious — every farm, every month, publicly — but the target itself remains aspirational rather than enforced.
Two features of the data make it more than a simple mortality tracker. First, mortality is reported with quartiles across farms within each sea area, not just a mean, which exposes how uneven outcomes are between sites. Second, the separate losses dataset breaks down each month’s total fish disappearance into four categories: dead, discarded, escaped, and other. The headline “mortality rate” tracks only the first of these. The other three are real losses — welfare-relevant, environmentally relevant, commercially relevant — that the headline figure quietly leaves out.

title: Below the Waterline
text:
Motivation & Research Question
A well-run regulatory regime delivers two things. It measures the outcome it claims to care about, and it measures it in a way that makes the full cost visible. Norwegian salmon mortality monitoring is a good test case. The industry has a publicly stated target (around 5 percent annual). The surveillance regime is dense and open. We can check, from the data alone, whether the target is being met, and whether the headline metric even captures what the public thinks it captures.
Research question: How close is the Norwegian salmon industry to its 5 percent annual mortality target, and what fraction of losses does the headline mortality figure fail to count?

title: Below the Waterline
text:
Data Preparation

Show code# Focus on salmon, at sea-area level (the finest geographic unit consistent
# across both datasets). Areas numbered 1-13 correspond to production zones
# along the Norwegian coast, running roughly south to north.
mort_salmon <- mortality |>
  filter(species == "salmon",
         geo_group == "area",
         !is.na(median)) |>
  mutate(
    year      = year(date),
    month     = month(date),
    # Put numeric areas in a sensible order; drop any multi-area aggregates
    area_num  = suppressWarnings(as.integer(region))
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(area_num)) |>
  mutate(area = factor(area_num, levels = 1:13, labels = paste("Area", 1:13)))

# The 5% annual target, expressed as the equivalent monthly rate.
# Monthly equivalent ≈ 1 - (1 - 0.05)^(1/12) ≈ 0.427%
target_monthly <- 1 - (1 - 0.05)^(1/12)

# National losses (all Norway) for the decomposition view
losses_norway <- losses |>
  filter(species == "salmon", region == "Norge") |>
  select(date, dead, discarded, escaped, other) |>
  pivot_longer(c(dead, discarded, escaped, other),
               names_to = "category", values_to = "fish") |>
  mutate(
    category = factor(category,
                      levels = c("dead", "other", "discarded", "escaped"),
                      labels = c("Dead", "Other", "Discarded", "Escaped"))
  )

cat("Mortality records (salmon, area-level):", nrow(mort_salmon), "\n")

Mortality records (salmon, area-level): 648 

Show codecat("Date range:", format(min(mort_salmon$date)), "–",
    format(max(mort_salmon$date)), "\n")

Date range: 2020-01-01 – 2025-12-01 

Show codecat("Areas covered:", n_distinct(mort_salmon$area), "\n")

Areas covered: 9 

Show codecat("Monthly 5% target equivalent:", percent(target_monthly, accuracy = 0.01), "\n\n")

Monthly 5% target equivalent: 0.43% 

Show code# How often is median monthly mortality above the 5% target equivalent?
cat("Share of area-months exceeding the 5% annual target: ",
    percent(mean(mort_salmon$median > target_monthly * 100), accuracy = 1), "\n")

Share of area-months exceeding the 5% annual target:  72%

title: Below the Waterline
section: Visualization 1: Six Years of Monthly Mortality, by Sea Area
text:
Visualization 1: Six Years of Monthly Mortality, by Sea Area

Show codeheat_df <- mort_salmon |>
  mutate(
    # Cap top of scale at 2.5% for readability — extreme outliers above
    # this are squashed to the top of the palette
    median_capped = pmin(median, 2.5),
    area = fct_rev(area)   # put Area 1 at top? actually flip so low numbers on top
  )

ggplot(heat_df, aes(x = date, y = area, fill = median_capped)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#4a90d9", mid = "#f5e6a8", high = "#b8412d",
    midpoint = target_monthly * 100,
    name = "Monthly\nmortality (%)",
    limits = c(0, 2.5),
    breaks = c(0, 0.42, 1, 2, 2.5),
    labels = c("0", "0.42\n(5%/yr\ntarget)", "1", "2", "≥2.5")
  ) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y",
               expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0.01))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Norway's 5% annual target is exceeded in most area-months",
    subtitle = "Median monthly salmon mortality by production area, 2020–2025 (red = above the 5%/yr target rate)",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: Norwegian Veterinary Institute (Laksetap) · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 11 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    panel.grid     = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.x  = element_line(colour = "grey70", linewidth = 0.3),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.key.height = unit(1.1, "cm")
  )







The heatmap reads each row as a production area, running from Area 1 (the southernmost, outside Agder) at the top down to Area 13 (Finnmark, at the Russian border) at the bottom.
