Each cell is one area-month; its colour is the median mortality rate across farms that month. Blue cells sit below the monthly equivalent of the 5 percent annual target (≈0.42%); the palette turns through pale yellow at the target and into red for rates above it. Two patterns are immediately visible. First, vertical red bands appear every autumn — August through November — across most of the coast, reflecting the combined stress of warm-water events, de-lousing treatments, and the autumn harvest. Second, the southern and west-central areas (3 through 6, Vestland and Trøndelag) carry darker, more persistent red, while some far-northern areas (Area 13, Finnmark) run cooler more of the year. The dominant impression, however, is red. Of all area-months in the data, roughly four in five exceed the monthly equivalent of a 5 percent annual mortality target. The target is, on the evidence of its own regulator’s data, not close to being met.

title: Below the Waterline
text:
Visualization 2: What “Mortality” Leaves Out

Show code# Compute monthly totals and the share of non-dead losses for annotation
losses_wide <- losses |>
  filter(species == "salmon", region == "Norge") |>
  mutate(
    total       = dead + discarded + escaped + other,
    non_dead    = discarded + escaped + other,
    share_other = non_dead / total
  )

cat("Median monthly share of losses that are NOT 'dead':",
    percent(median(losses_wide$share_other), accuracy = 0.1), "\n")

Median monthly share of losses that are NOT 'dead': 11.7% 

Show codecat("Maximum monthly share:",
    percent(max(losses_wide$share_other), accuracy = 1), "\n")

Maximum monthly share: 44% 

Show codeloss_pal <- c(
  "Dead"      = "#b8412d",
  "Other"     = "#d4af37",
  "Discarded" = "#4a90d9",
  "Escaped"   = "#3c9fa3"
)

ggplot(losses_norway, aes(x = date, y = fish / 1e6, fill = category)) +
  geom_area(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.25) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = loss_pal, name = NULL,
                    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y",
               expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0.01))) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(suffix = "M"),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  labs(
    title    = "'Dead' is only part of it: five to twenty percent of monthly fish losses are discarded, escaped, or other",
    subtitle = "Monthly losses of farmed salmon in Norway, 2020–2025, by reported category (millions of fish)",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Fish lost per month",
    caption  = "Source: Norwegian Veterinary Institute (Laksetap) · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 11 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 10.5),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank()
  )







The second view reframes the question from “how many fish die?” to “what counts as a loss?” Each month, the Norwegian Veterinary Institute reports four categories of lost fish. Dead is the category the public mortality figure tracks. Discarded refers to fish culled or rejected as unfit for sale — welfare-relevant but not a natural death. Escaped refers to fish that break out of their pens into the wild, a genetic and ecological concern for wild Norwegian salmon populations. Other is a residual category that includes, among other things, mass-casualty events that the Institute’s reporting system has not yet sorted into the above three. The stacked area makes the composition visible. In a typical month, dead accounts for 85 to 95 percent of total losses; the remaining 5 to 15 percent comes from the three other categories. But the share is not stable. Periodic spikes, particularly in the warmer months of 2020 and again in 2023–24, push the non-dead share above 20 percent as other and discarded jump — spikes that would vanish from view if the headline metric were the only one published.

title: Below the Waterline
text:
Takeaway
A governance regime can be judged on two things: whether its stated target is being met, and whether its chosen metric measures the right thing. Norway’s salmon mortality monitoring gives a clean test on both counts. The target is not being met — by the Veterinary Institute’s own data, median monthly mortality sits above the 5 percent annual equivalent in roughly four out of every five area-months, and a pronounced autumn peak repeats each year across almost the entire coast. The metric, for its part, is incomplete. It tracks dead fish, not lost fish. The gap is small in an average month but non-negligible, and the months when it matters most — mass-casualty events and welfare cullings — are exactly the months the public most needs to see.
None of this diminishes the transparency of the regime itself, which is unusually open and granular by international standards; the data needed to audit the claim is in this file. The claim itself, about whether the industry is approaching a welfare threshold it has publicly committed to, does not survive that audit. A target that is exceeded 80 percent of the time, and a metric that systematically undercounts a fraction of losses, are together compatible with sincere intent, rising expectations, and a long gap still to close — but they are not compatible with a picture in which the problem is already in hand.

title: A Million Dice Rolls
text:
Background
π is one of a handful of numbers for which the shape of its decimal expansion has been a live research question for three centuries. It is irrational, so its digits never terminate and never settle into a repeating block. It is transcendental, so no polynomial equation with rational coefficients has π as a root. But the statistical question — whether its digits, in every base, appear with the long-run frequencies one would get from rolling a fair ten-sided die — is open. A number with that property is called normal in base 10. Émile Borel proved in 1909 that almost every real number is normal; what remains unproven, more than a century later, is that any specific “interesting” number actually is. It is not known whether π, or e, or √2, or ln 2 is normal.
What mathematicians have is strong empirical evidence. Every statistical test that has been run on π’s digits — to the tens of billions, in several recent efforts — has failed to distinguish them from the output of a uniform random number generator. One million digits is a modest slice of that record, but it is large enough to run the basic tests with real statistical power. If π were not behaving like a normal number, there is a fair chance we would see it here. And it is small enough that every calculation runs in a few seconds on a laptop, which makes the test reproducible rather than ceremonial.

title: A Million Dice Rolls
text:
Motivation & Research Question
There are two separable ways a deterministic sequence of digits can betray non-randomness. It can have the wrong marginal frequencies — some digits appearing conspicuously more or less often than one in ten. Or it can have the right marginals but the wrong structure — runs of repeated digits that are too long, too short, or too clustered for chance. The first test is the cheap test of normality. The second is harder to fake and closer, in spirit, to the property mathematicians care about.
Research question: Do the first million digits of π match the statistical fingerprint of independent uniform random digits, in both their digit frequencies and the distribution of their consecutive runs?

title: A Million Dice Rolls
text:
Data Preparation

Show coded <- pi_digits$digit
N <- length(d)

cat("Total digits:", format(N, big.mark = ","), "\n")

Total digits: 1,000,001 

Show codecat("Range of digits:", paste(range(d), collapse = "–"), "\n")

Range of digits: 0–9 

Show code# Overall digit frequencies
freq_tab <- tibble(digit = d) |>
  count(digit) |>
  mutate(
    share    = n / sum(n),
    expected = 1 / 10,
    deviation_pp = (share - expected) * 100
  )

cat("\nOverall digit frequencies (first 1,000,000 digits of π):\n")


Overall digit frequencies (first 1,000,000 digits of π):

Show codefreq_tab |>
  mutate(share = percent(share, accuracy = 0.01),
         deviation_pp = number(deviation_pp, accuracy = 0.01)) |>
  print()

# A tibble: 10 × 5
   digit      n share  expected deviation_pp
   <dbl>  <int> <chr>     <dbl> <chr>       
 1     0  99959 10.00%      0.1 0.00        
 2     1  99758 9.98%       0.1 -0.02       
 3     2 100026 10.00%      0.1 0.00        
 4     3 100230 10.02%      0.1 0.02        
 5     4 100230 10.02%      0.1 0.02        
 6     5 100359 10.04%      0.1 0.04        
 7     6  99548 9.95%       0.1 -0.05       
 8     7  99800 9.98%       0.1 -0.02       
 9     8  99985 10.00%      0.1 0.00        
10     9 100106 10.01%      0.1 0.01

title: A Million Dice Rolls
text:
Visualization 1: Convergence of Digit Frequencies

Show code# Evaluate running frequencies at log-spaced checkpoints from 100 to 1,000,000
checkpoints <- round(10 ^ seq(log10(100), log10(N), length.out = 120))
checkpoints <- unique(checkpoints)

# Cumulative count of each digit at each checkpoint
cum_counts <- map_dfr(0:9, function(dgt) {
  cs <- cumsum(d == dgt)[checkpoints]
  tibble(n = checkpoints, digit = dgt, count = cs, share = cs / checkpoints)
})

# 2σ binomial envelope around p = 0.1
envelope <- tibble(n = checkpoints) |>
  mutate(
    lower = 0.1 - 2 * sqrt(0.1 * 0.9 / n),
    upper = 0.1 + 2 * sqrt(0.1 * 0.9 / n)
  )

ggplot() +
  geom_ribbon(data = envelope, aes(x = n, ymin = lower, ymax = upper),
              fill = "#4a90d9", alpha = 0.18) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.1, colour = "#4a90d9",
             linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_line(data = cum_counts,
            aes(x = n, y = share, group = digit),
            colour = "grey35", alpha = 0.7, linewidth = 0.45) +
  scale_x_log10(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale()),
    breaks = c(100, 1e3, 1e4, 1e5, 1e6)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     limits = c(0.05, 0.15)) +
  annotate("text", x = 200, y = 0.148,
           label = "Expected ±2σ\nbinomial envelope",
           colour = "#2e5f8a", size = 3.2, hjust = 0, lineheight = 0.95) +
  annotate("text", x = 8e5, y = 0.104,
           label = "1/10 target",
           colour = "#2e5f8a", size = 3.2, hjust = 1) +
  labs(
    title    = "All ten digits fall inside the random-digit envelope by a few hundred samples",
    subtitle = "Running frequency of each digit 0–9 in the first million digits of π (log-scale x)",
    x        = "Digits used (log scale)",
    y        = "Running frequency",
    caption  = "Source: Eneko Pi (1M digits) · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 12 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )







Each grey line tracks one of the ten digits (0 through 9) as its running frequency is updated with each additional digit of π. At the far left, after only 100 or so digits, the running frequencies sprawl between 5 and 15 percent — ordinary small-sample noise. The blue band is the ±2σ envelope that a genuinely random stream of uniform digits would be expected to stay inside about 95 percent of the time, and it narrows quickly as the sample grows: around one thousand digits the band is roughly two percentage points wide, and by one million digits it has contracted to a thin ribbon around the dashed target line at exactly 10 percent. The striking thing is that the ten grey lines collapse into that narrowing ribbon without ever straying outside it for long. No digit in π sits systematically high or systematically low. The marginal test for normality — the cheap but necessary one — is passed.

title: A Million Dice Rolls
text:
Visualization 2: The Distribution of Runs

Show code# Run-length encoding: every maximal block of identical consecutive digits
rle_obj  <- rle(d)
runs     <- tibble(length = rle_obj$lengths, value = rle_obj$values)

run_table <- runs |>
  count(length, name = "observed") |>
  arrange(length)

# Expected count under i.i.d. uniform digits:
# each run's start is (approximately) a Bernoulli(9/10) trial against the
# previous digit, and its length is Geometric(9/10) conditional on starting.
# So expected count of runs of length exactly k ≈ N * (9/10)^2 * (1/10)^(k-1)
run_table <- run_table |>
  mutate(expected = N * (9/10)^2 * (1/10)^(length - 1))

# Keep lengths 1 through the maximum observed
max_len <- max(run_table$length)

cat("Longest run of identical consecutive digits in the first 1M of π:",
    max_len, "\n")

Longest run of identical consecutive digits in the first 1M of π: 7 

Show code# Find the position of that longest run
run_end    <- cumsum(rle_obj$lengths)
run_start  <- run_end - rle_obj$lengths + 1
longest_i  <- which.max(rle_obj$lengths)
cat("It consists of the digit",
    rle_obj$values[longest_i],
    "starting at position",
    format(run_start[longest_i], big.mark = ","),
    "\n")

It consists of the digit 3 starting at position 710,101 

Show code# Famous "Feynman point" — six 9s beginning at position 762
feynman_pos <- 762
cat("\nFor reference, the Feynman point (six 9s) starts at digit",
    feynman_pos, ", which corresponds to a run of length",
    rle_obj$lengths[which(run_start == feynman_pos)], "\n")


For reference, the Feynman point (six 9s) starts at digit 762 , which corresponds to a run of length 1 

Show coderun_long <- run_table |>
  pivot_longer(c(observed, expected),
               names_to = "source", values_to = "count") |>
  mutate(source = factor(source,
                         levels = c("expected", "observed"),
                         labels = c("Expected (random digits)",
                                    "Observed (π)")))

ggplot(run_long, aes(x = length, y = count, fill = source)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.75), width = 0.7) +
  scale_y_log10(
    labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale()),
    breaks = 10 ^ (0:6),
    minor_breaks = NULL
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:max_len) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Expected (random digits)" = "#4a90d9",
                               "Observed (π)"            = "#d4af37"),
                    name = NULL) +
  annotate("segment",
           x = 6, xend = 6, y = 40, yend = 10,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm")),
           colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.4) +
  annotate("text", x = 6, y = 65,
           label = "The famous\n'Feynman point'\n(six 9s at pos. 762)\nlives in this bar",
           size = 3, colour = "grey30", lineheight = 0.9) +
  labs(
    title    = "Run-length counts in π track the random-digit prediction across six orders of magnitude",
    subtitle = "Observed vs expected count of runs of identical consecutive digits, first 1,000,000 digits of π",
    x        = "Run length (consecutive identical digits)",
    y        = "Number of runs (log scale)",
    caption  = "Source: Eneko Pi (1M digits) · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 12 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )







The second panel is the harder test. It asks not just whether each digit appears 10 percent of the time on average, but whether runs of repeated digits — five 7s in a row, six 9s in a row — appear at the rates a random process would produce. The blue bars give the theoretical expected count under a null of independent uniform digits: each extra digit in a run multiplies the expected frequency by one tenth. The gold bars give the actual counts in π. Across six orders of magnitude — from runs of length 1 (the most common, occurring roughly 800,000 times) down to runs of length 7 or 8 (occurring, under the null, fewer than one time expected) — the observed counts track the expectation closely. The famous Feynman point, six 9s at digit 762, is not anomalous: in a million random digits we would expect on the order of eight six-long runs somewhere in the sequence, and π delivers a number close to that. The Feynman point is striking not because it exists but because it occurs unusually early — a good story, not a statistical surprise.

title: A Million Dice Rolls
text:
Takeaway
The first million digits of π pass both tests. The ten digits appear with frequencies that converge cleanly into the band a uniform random process would produce, and runs of consecutive identical digits appear at rates that match the random-digit prediction across six orders of magnitude. The standard caveat is that passing these tests does not prove that π is normal — a proof would have to cover every sequence of every length, not just the first million digits and not just marginals and runs. But the pattern is exactly the one the normality conjecture predicts, and failing these tests would have been a substantial surprise after a century of empirical verification at ever-larger scales.
What the visualizations make tangible is a distinctive kind of mathematical object. π is a single, fully determined number. Its digits are not the outcome of any random experiment; they are forced, one by one, by the geometry of the circle. And yet, arranged as a sequence, they generate the same statistical fingerprint as a million throws of a fair ten-sided die. The line between structure and randomness is, in this specific case, not a line the usual tests can find.

title: A Column of Water
text:
Background
Birchy Head is a small inlet on the south shore of Nova Scotia, part of Lunenburg County’s coastal water system. Since early 2018, the Centre for Marine Applied Research (CMAR), in partnership with the Nova Scotia Department of Fisheries and Aquaculture, has maintained a sensor string in the water column at this location — seven thermistors stacked from the near-surface at 2 metres down to 40 metres, recording temperature continuously. The resulting dataset, published on the Nova Scotia Open Data Portal, now covers seven years of daily-aggregated temperatures at all seven depths.
The data is scientifically useful because coastal oceans are where atmospheric warming, aquaculture, commercial fisheries, and ecological change all meet. Cold deep water in summer is what keeps Nova Scotia’s farmed salmon and wild lobster alive through the heat; if that deep reservoir warms, or if summer stratification collapses earlier in the year, the downstream consequences are economic as well as ecological. The North Atlantic has been warming rapidly — the Gulf of Maine, just to the southwest, has in some recent years been described as warming faster than 99 percent of the world’s ocean. Whether that warming is visible at this one sensor string, over the seven-year window available, is a question the data is well-posed to answer.

title: A Column of Water
text:
Motivation & Research Question
A coastal water column has a predictable seasonal cycle. In winter, surface cooling and wind-driven mixing homogenise the column — sensors at 2, 10, 20, and 40 metres all read roughly the same temperature. In spring, solar heating warms the surface faster than the deep, and a thermocline builds. By summer, the top few metres can be ten degrees warmer than the bottom. Autumn cooling and storms mix the column back together, and the cycle repeats.
Research question: Over seven years of daily measurements, is the thermal structure at Birchy Head stable — does each year’s annual cycle look like the others — or are there visible year-on-year shifts in when the column stratifies, how strongly it stratifies, and how warm the warmest water gets?

title: A Column of Water
text:
Data Preparation

Show codeocean_clean <- ocean |>
  filter(!is.na(mean_temperature_degree_c)) |>
  mutate(
    year        = year(date),
    day_of_year = yday(date),
    depth       = factor(sensor_depth_at_low_tide_m,
                         levels = c(2, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40),
                         labels = c("2 m", "5 m", "10 m", "15 m",
                                    "20 m", "30 m", "40 m"))
  )

# Stratification index: surface (2 m) minus deep (40 m), per day.
# Requires both depths on the same day.
strat <- ocean_clean |>
  filter(sensor_depth_at_low_tide_m %in% c(2, 40)) |>
  select(date, sensor_depth_at_low_tide_m, mean_temperature_degree_c) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = sensor_depth_at_low_tide_m,
              values_from = mean_temperature_degree_c,
              names_prefix = "d") |>
  filter(!is.na(d2), !is.na(d40)) |>
  mutate(
    strat_index = d2 - d40,
    year        = year(date),
    day_of_year = yday(date)
  )

cat("Date range:", format(min(ocean_clean$date)), "–",
    format(max(ocean_clean$date)), "\n")

Date range: 2018-02-20 – 2025-12-06 

Show codecat("Total daily-depth observations:", format(nrow(ocean_clean), big.mark = ","), "\n")

Total daily-depth observations: 19,165 

Show codecat("Depths recorded:", paste(levels(ocean_clean$depth), collapse = ", "), "\n")

Depths recorded: 2 m, 5 m, 10 m, 15 m, 20 m, 30 m, 40 m 

Show codecat("Days with both 2 m and 40 m temperature:",
    format(nrow(strat), big.mark = ","), "\n\n")

Days with both 2 m and 40 m temperature: 2,307 

Show code# Annual summaries to flag which year was warmest
ocean_clean |>
  filter(depth == "2 m") |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(
    mean_surface = mean(mean_temperature_degree_c),
    max_surface  = max(mean_temperature_degree_c),
    days         = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  print()

# A tibble: 8 × 4
   year mean_surface max_surface  days
  <dbl>        <dbl>       <dbl> <int>
1  2018        10.0        20.7    315

3  2020         8.93       20.2    366
4  2021         9.82       20.7    365
5  2022         9.34       21.3    365
6  2023         9.70       21.2    365
7  2024         3.89        8.63   164
8  2025         9.59       20.2    340

title: A Column of Water
section: Visualization 1: Seven Years of the Water Column
text:
Visualization 1: Seven Years of the Water Column

Show code# Put shallow at top so the plot reads as a cross-section of the water column:
# surface up, deep down.
ocean_plot <- ocean_clean |>
  mutate(depth = fct_rev(depth))

ggplot(ocean_plot,
       aes(x = date, y = depth, fill = mean_temperature_degree_c)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colours = c("#2a4d8a", "#4a90d9", "#a8cfe6",
                "#f5e6a8", "#e07b54", "#b8412d"),
    values  = scales::rescale(c(0, 5, 10, 13, 17, 22)),
    name    = "Daily mean\ntemperature (°C)",
    breaks  = c(0, 5, 10, 15, 20)
  ) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y",
               expand = expansion(mult = c(0.005, 0.005))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Stratifies every summer, collapses every winter — seven times in a row",
    subtitle = "Daily mean temperature by sensor depth, Birchy Head, Nova Scotia, 2018–2025",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Sensor depth at low tide",
    caption  = "Source: Centre for Marine Applied Research · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 13 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.x = element_line(colour = "grey70", linewidth = 0.3),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.key.height = unit(1.2, "cm")
  )







The plot shows the water column at Birchy Head as a cross-section: the top row of each year is the 2-metre sensor, just below the surface, and the bottom row is the 40-metre sensor, sitting on the seabed. Colour is daily mean temperature, from deep winter blues near 0°C to summer reds above 20°C. The repeated seasonal cycle is the dominant feature. Every winter (visible as a vertical blue band spanning all seven depths) the water column cools and mixes until the top and bottom sensors are within a degree of one another. Every summer, a red-orange tongue appears at the top of the figure while the bottom of the figure stays blue — surface waters warm rapidly while deep water, insulated by the thermocline, remains cold. The 40-metre sensor rarely exceeds 10°C even at the peak of summer. Year-to-year differences are visible against this repeating background. The summer of 2020 shows the most intense and longest-lasting surface warmth in the record, with the red tongue extending both deeper and further into autumn than in most other years. The summer of 2022 is the mildest-looking, with cooler surface peaks and a shorter warm season. Winter mixing, by contrast, looks remarkably consistent: in no year does the deep water fail to reset to near-freezing.

title: A Column of Water
text:
Visualization 2: The Stratification Signal

Show code# Build a "day-of-year" climatology band from years 2018-2024, then
# overlay each year's actual curve.
years_in_data <- sort(unique(strat$year))

strat_plot <- strat |>
  mutate(year = factor(year))

# Climatology ribbon: for each day-of-year, the 25th and 75th percentile
# of stratification across all years
climatology <- strat |>
  group_by(day_of_year) |>
  summarise(
    q25    = quantile(strat_index, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    q75    = quantile(strat_index, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    median = median(strat_index, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Year palette: sequential so older = cooler, newer = warmer-toned
year_pal <- setNames(
  c("#4a90d9", "#3c9fa3", "#5aab6d", "#d4af37",
    "#e07b54", "#b8412d", "#7b5ea7", "#c75f8c"),
  as.character(years_in_data)
)[1:length(years_in_data)]

ggplot() +
  # horizontal reference at 0 (fully mixed)
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey70", linewidth = 0.3) +
  # climatology ribbon (middle 50% of years)
  geom_ribbon(data = climatology,
              aes(x = day_of_year, ymin = q25, ymax = q75),
              fill = "grey78", alpha = 0.45) +
  geom_line(data = climatology,
            aes(x = day_of_year, y = median),
            colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dashed") +
  # each year's actual trajectory
  geom_line(data = strat_plot,
            aes(x = day_of_year, y = strat_index,
                colour = year, group = year),
            linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.9) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = year_pal, name = "Year") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(1, 60, 121, 182, 244, 305, 365),
    labels = c("Jan", "Mar", "May", "Jul", "Sep", "Nov", "Dec"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0.01))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_number(suffix = " °C"),
    breaks = seq(-2, 16, 2)
  ) +
  annotate("text", x = 200, y = 14.5,
           label = "Surface warmer than deep\n→ column is stratified",
           colour = "grey30", size = 3.1, hjust = 0.5, lineheight = 0.95) +
  annotate("text", x = 30, y = -1,
           label = "Fully mixed\ncolumn",
           colour = "grey30", size = 3.1, hjust = 0, lineheight = 0.95) +
  labs(
    title    = "Summer stratification peaked dramatically in 2020; recent years sit closer to the middle",
    subtitle = "Daily surface (2 m) minus deep (40 m) temperature, by day of year, with grey ribbon = middle 50% of years",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Stratification index  (T₂ₘ − T₄₀ₘ)",
    caption  = "Source: Centre for Marine Applied Research · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 13 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 10.5),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )







The second view collapses the seven-by-seven grid into a single line per year, tracking the difference between the surface and the 40-metre sensor. When this line sits near zero — most of winter and early spring — the column is mixed and the two depths agree. As spring sun builds a thermocline, the line climbs; it peaks in July or August when surface waters are warmest and deep waters are still cold; it falls again as autumn storms break the thermocline down. The grey ribbon is the middle 50 percent of all years, which makes anomalous years easy to see. 2020 (deep red) climbs visibly higher than the others, reaching surface-to-deep differences above 15°C at its peak — a genuinely extreme stratification event in this record. 2022 and 2025, by contrast, sit below the grey band for much of summer: their columns stratified less strongly than a typical year. The shape of the annual cycle is otherwise remarkably preserved. Spring onset of stratification falls in a narrow two-week window each year, and autumn collapse arrives on schedule. The variance is in amplitude, not timing.

title: A Column of Water
text:
Takeaway
Seven years is a short record for climate questions. What it is long enough to show is the envelope of ordinary variability at a coastal site — the range within which “normal” falls. At Birchy Head, the envelope is well-defined. The column stratifies every summer and mixes every winter; the 40-metre sensor rarely rises above 10°C; the timing of the seasonal transitions is consistent to within about two weeks from year to year. Against that envelope, 2020 stands out as the year with the strongest and longest surface warming in the record, and 2022 as the mildest. Neither year is outside what a longer record might show as natural variability, but both sit clearly at the edges of this one.
What the record cannot do, given its length, is tell us whether the North Atlantic warming that has been documented in offshore data is already penetrating this particular inlet. A monotonic upward drift in peak summer temperature would take at least another decade of monitoring to separate from year-to-year variability. What the record can do is what good environmental monitoring is supposed to do: establish a baseline against which future years will be measurable. Seven years in, Birchy Head has that baseline.

title: Some Things Still Get Fixed
text:
Background
Repair Cafés began in Amsterdam in 2009, when journalist Martine Postma organised a pop-up event where residents could bring broken household objects and have them fixed for free by volunteer tinkerers. The model was unusually sticky. There are now more than 3,000 Repair Café branches operating across more than forty countries, and since 2015 a shared tracking system called Repair Monitor has logged the outcomes of individual repair attempts — what was brought in, what went wrong, and whether it got fixed. The combined log now contains tens of thousands of cases.
The project sits at an awkward junction of several larger debates. Consumer-protection advocates argue that modern products are engineered to fail and designed to resist repair — the “planned obsolescence” claim that has reached the European Parliament (the 2024 Right to Repair Directive), the US Federal Trade Commission, and courtrooms in France. Manufacturers counter that repairability has not declined, that consumers value thinness and affordability over serviceability, and that the data supporting the obsolescence claim is anecdotal. Repair Monitor is, as far as I know, the largest open-access dataset from people who actually try to fix things. If the obsolescence claim leaves any visible fingerprint in observational data at scale, this is where it should leave it.
crumbs: 13  Some Things Still Get Fixed

title: Some Things Still Get Fixed
text:
Motivation & Research Question
The planned-obsolescence claim makes a falsifiable prediction. Holding the category of product constant — a toaster versus a toaster, a vacuum cleaner versus a vacuum cleaner — a newer item should be harder to repair than an older one. A Repair Monitor entry for a 2005 vacuum cleaner and a 2022 vacuum cleaner is, in this respect, a natural experiment: the same category of appliance, presented to the same kind of volunteer with the same kind of toolkit, carrying a different age. If obsolescence is real, the success rate should decline with year of manufacture. If repairability has in fact been improving (as manufacturers sometimes argue), it should increase.
Research question: Within each category of product, do repair success rates decline as year of manufacture approaches the present, and are some categories visibly worse-affected than others?
crumbs: 13  Some Things Still Get Fixed

title: Some Things Still Get Fixed
text:
Data Preparation

Show code# Keep attempts where both the outcome and a plausible production year are
# recorded. "Plausible" = 1970–2025 (a handful of entries list years like
# 19 or 2050 which are clearly typos).
repairs_clean <- repairs |>
  filter(
    !is.na(repaired),
    !is.na(estimated_year_of_production),
    between(estimated_year_of_production, 1970, 2025)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    outcome = factor(repaired,
                     levels = c("no", "half", "yes"),
                     labels = c("Not repaired", "Partially repaired", "Repaired")),
    category = str_replace(category, " / ", " /\n"),
    repair_success = repaired == "yes"
  )

# For the age-vs-success analysis, drop any category with very few records
# (they would produce wild estimates)
cat_counts <- repairs_clean |>
  count(category, name = "n_cat") |>
  filter(n_cat >= 500)

repairs_mainstream <- repairs_clean |>
  inner_join(cat_counts, by = "category")

cat("Total repair attempts:", format(nrow(repairs), big.mark = ","), "\n")

Total repair attempts: 178,749 

Show codecat("With outcome + year:  ", format(nrow(repairs_clean), big.mark = ","), "\n")

With outcome + year:   61,243 

Show codecat("Countries:            ", n_distinct(repairs_clean$country), "\n")

Countries:             21 

Show codecat("Categories (all):     ", n_distinct(repairs_clean$category), "\n")

Categories (all):      15 

Show codecat("Categories (≥500 obs):", nrow(cat_counts), "\n\n")

Categories (≥500 obs): 12 

Show code# Top-level headline: share repaired over all data
overall <- repairs_clean |>
  summarise(
    n          = n(),
    full       = mean(repaired == "yes"),
    partial    = mean(repaired == "half"),
    failed     = mean(repaired == "no")
  )
cat("Overall: ", percent(overall$full, accuracy = 1), " fully repaired, ",
    percent(overall$partial, accuracy = 1), " partial, ",
    percent(overall$failed, accuracy = 1), " not repaired.\n", sep = "")

Overall: 59% fully repaired, 14% partial, 28% not repaired.
crumbs: 13  Some Things Still Get Fixed

title: Some Things Still Get Fixed
section: Visualization 1: Repair Success by Year of Production
text:
Visualization 1: Repair Success by Year of Production

Show code# Pick the 8 biggest categories for clean faceting
top_cats <- cat_counts |>
  slice_max(n_cat, n = 8) |>
  pull(category)

age_df <- repairs_mainstream |>
  filter(category %in% top_cats,
         estimated_year_of_production >= 1990) |>
  mutate(category = factor(category, levels = top_cats))

# Year-category success rate with 2σ binomial CIs
year_rates <- age_df |>
  group_by(category, estimated_year_of_production) |>
  summarise(
    n       = n(),
    success = sum(repair_success),
    rate    = success / n,
    se      = sqrt(rate * (1 - rate) / n),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(n >= 15)

ggplot(year_rates, aes(x = estimated_year_of_production, y = rate)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = pmax(0, rate - 2 * se),
                  ymax = pmin(1, rate + 2 * se)),
              fill = "#4a90d9", alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_point(aes(size = n), alpha = 0.55, colour = "#2e5f8a") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, span = 0.9,
              colour = "#b8412d", linewidth = 0.9) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(1990, 2000, 2010, 2020)) +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 3.5, guide = "none") +
  facet_wrap(~ category, ncol = 4) +
  labs(
    title    = "Newer products are harder to fix — but the gap varies wildly by category",
    subtitle = "Share of repair attempts that succeeded, by estimated year of production (top eight categories)",
    x        = "Estimated year of production",
    y        = "Share repaired successfully",
    caption  = "Source: Repair Monitor (Repair Café international network) · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 14 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 10.5),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    strip.text    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9.5, lineheight = 0.95),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.spacing.x = unit(0.9, "lines"),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(1.0, "lines")
  )







Each small panel is one category of household product, with each point a production-year success rate and point area proportional to sample size. The blue ribbon is the two-sigma binomial confidence band; the red curve is a LOESS smooth through the yearly rates. The story across the eight panels is two-layered. In almost every category, the red smooth slopes downward from the 1990s to the 2020s, consistent with a general decline in reparability over time. But the slope varies dramatically across categories. For vacuum cleaners, coffee makers, and household appliances, the fall from the oldest to newest bins is sharp — success rates above 60 percent for products from the 1990s fall to below 40 percent for products manufactured in the last five years. For textiles and non-electric tools, the slope is essentially flat; a 1995 pair of trousers and a 2022 pair of trousers are repaired with similar frequency. The pattern is not uniform decline in “things,” but category-specific — concentrated in the kinds of products where manufacturers have the most control over repairability: glued assemblies, proprietary cartridges, firmware-locked circuit boards.
crumbs: 13  Some Things Still Get Fixed

title: Some Things Still Get Fixed
section: Visualization 2: Which Categories Repair Cafés Can Actually Save
text:
Visualization 2: Which Categories Repair Cafés Can Actually Save

Show codecat_outcomes <- repairs_clean |>
  inner_join(cat_counts, by = "category") |>
  count(category, outcome, n_cat) |>
  group_by(category) |>
  mutate(share = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup()

# Order categories by share successfully repaired (high to low)
order_df <- cat_outcomes |>
  filter(outcome == "Repaired") |>
  arrange(share)

cat_outcomes <- cat_outcomes |>
  mutate(category = factor(category, levels = order_df$category))

outcome_pal <- c(
  "Repaired"           = "#5aab6d",
  "Partially repaired" = "#d4af37",
  "Not repaired"       = "#b8412d"
)

# Label data — show sample size after the bar
sample_labels <- order_df |>
  left_join(count(repairs_clean |> inner_join(cat_counts, by = "category"),
                  category, name = "total"),
            by = "category")

ggplot(cat_outcomes,
       aes(x = share, y = category, fill = fct_rev(outcome))) +
  geom_col(width = 0.78) +
  geom_text(data = sample_labels,
            aes(x = 1.02, y = category,
                label = paste0("n = ", comma(total))),
            hjust = 0, size = 3, colour = "grey40",
            inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = outcome_pal, name = NULL,
                    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                     limits = c(0, 1.15),
                     breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.25),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Textiles and mechanical tools still win; small electronics and coffee makers do not",
    subtitle = "Repair outcome by product category, Repair Monitor 2015–2026",
    x        = "Share of attempts",
    y        = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: Repair Monitor (Repair Café international network) · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 14 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 10.5),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y  = element_text(size = 10)
  )







The second view answers a different question: leaving aside trends over time, what do Repair Cafés actually succeed at? Categories are ordered by the share of attempts that resulted in a full repair, from the most-fixable at the top to the least-fixable at the bottom. The top half is dominated by mechanical and textile categories: clothing, bicycles, jewellery, non-electric tools, furniture. These are categories where a competent volunteer with a needle, glue, or wrench can usually produce a working item. The bottom half is dominated by categories that involve firmware, sealed batteries, proprietary cartridges, or printed circuit boards: computer equipment, printers, small kitchen appliances, coffee makers. In the least-fixable categories, fewer than four in ten attempts succeed fully. The Repair Café model is not a general solution to e-waste; it is a very effective solution for one half of the problem and a much weaker one for the other.
crumbs: 13  Some Things Still Get Fixed

title: Some Things Still Get Fixed
text:
Takeaway
Repair Café data does two things with unusual clarity. First, it confirms, at scale and across a decade, that newer products are on average harder to repair than older products of the same kind. The effect is real, it is visible year over year, and it has the expected sign. Second, it shows that the problem is not distributed uniformly across household goods. Textiles and mechanical items are close to time-invariant in their repairability; the steep decline is concentrated in a specific family of categories — small appliances, consumer electronics, coffee-makers, printers — where manufacturing choices about adhesives, proprietary parts, and firmware locks have the most room to raise the cost of entry for a volunteer fixer.
The data cannot, on its own, prove that these design choices are deliberate obsolescence rather than side-effects of other engineering goals. What it can do is shift the burden of proof. The claim that products are not getting harder to repair is harder to hold when the global network of people who actually attempt to repair them has recorded, in open data, a pattern that looks exactly like the one the obsolescence hypothesis predicts. Whether policy responses — the EU Right to Repair Directive, France’s repairability index, state-level US initiatives — are calibrated correctly is a separate question. Whether there is something to respond to, this data has settled.
crumbs: 13  Some Things Still Get Fixed

title: Who Follows the Ship
text:
Background
For most of the twentieth century, the southern oceans below Australia and New Zealand were travelled by a small number of ships — research vessels, fishing trawlers, cargo ships, whaling ships — and watched by an even smaller number of seabird observers. From 1969 to 1990, under the Australasian Seabird Mapping Scheme, those observers kept standardised ten-minute logs of every seabird they could see from the deck. For each sighting they recorded species, count, and a small set of behavioural codes: was the bird flying past, sitting on water, sitting on ice, feeding, following the ship’s wake, or flying alongside? The resulting dataset, now curated by Te Papa Tongarewa (the Museum of New Zealand), contains tens of thousands of observations.
The behavioural columns matter. One of the central facts of modern seabird ecology is that many pelagic species have a strong, species-specific relationship to fishing vessels. Albatrosses and giant petrels routinely trail trawlers for hours to feed on fish discards; this is their ecological opportunity and, via longline bycatch, their ecological peril. Other species — prions, storm petrels, shearwaters — show little interest in ships and fly past them as if they were not there. The division is not a modern conservation artefact. It is a stable behavioural feature of the birds themselves, and the Australasian log book, collected before the industrial fishing footprint of the Southern Ocean reached its modern scale, offers an unusually clean window into what the natural partition looked like.

title: Who Follows the Ship
text:
Motivation & Research Question
The suggested question for this week’s dataset concerns record-keeping: do the split columns agree with the original logbook entries in species_common_name? That is a useful data-quality audit, and the answer is mostly yes. A more interesting question the data supports is ecological. Every sighting comes tagged with behaviour — and every bird species has a different pattern of behaviours. If the boundary between ship-followers and passers-by is as clean as seabird ecologists describe, it should be visible immediately in the joint distribution of species and behaviour, without any model at all.
Research question: Do seabird species in the Australasian log book divide cleanly into ship-followers and ship-ignorers, and does the division track known taxonomy and foraging strategy?

title: Who Follows the Ship
Who Follows the Ship
text:
Visualization 1: Behaviour × Species

Show codebehaviours <- c(
  following_ship   = "Following\nwake",
  accompanying     = "Flying\nalongside",
  feeding          = "Feeding",
  sitting_on_ship  = "Sitting\non ship",
  flying_past      = "Flying\npast",
  sitting_on_water = "Sitting\non water",
  sitting_on_ice   = "Sitting\non ice"
)

# Per-species share of sightings exhibiting each behaviour
beh_long <- beh_df |>
  select(species_label, all_of(names(behaviours))) |>
  pivot_longer(-species_label, names_to = "behaviour", values_to = "flag") |>
  filter(!is.na(flag)) |>
  group_by(species_label, behaviour) |>
  summarise(share = mean(flag), n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(
    behaviour = factor(behaviour,
                       levels = names(behaviours),
                       labels = behaviours)
  )

ggplot(beh_long,
       aes(x = behaviour, y = species_label, fill = share)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(share >= 0.05,
                               sprintf("%.0f%%", 100 * share), "")),
            size = 3, colour = "grey15") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#f3f5f8", mid = "#d4af37", high = "#b8412d",
    midpoint = 0.35,
    name = "Share of\nsightings",
    limits = c(0, 1),
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(position = "top") +
  labs(
    title    = "Every species sits in its own behavioural niche with respect to the ship",
    subtitle = "Share of sightings in each behavioural mode, top 14 recorded species, 1969–1990",
    x        = NULL, y = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: Te Papa Tongarewa / Australasian Seabird Mapping Scheme · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 15 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 10.5),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    panel.grid    = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x   = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold", lineheight = 0.9),
    axis.text.y   = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.key.height = unit(1.1, "cm")
  )







The heatmap reads as a behavioural niche map. Each row is a species; each column is a behaviour; the cell intensity is the share of that species’ sightings in which the behaviour was recorded. Numbers appear in cells where the behaviour accounts for at least 5 percent of sightings, to keep the plot legible. A distinct block structure is immediately visible. The “Following wake” and “Flying alongside” columns light up strongly for the albatrosses, giant petrels, and cape petrels at the top — the species that seabird ecologists describe as ship-followers. The “Flying past” column dominates for the small tube-nosed species: prions, storm petrels, shearwaters. “Sitting on water” shows up in species that use the ocean surface as a platform for prolonged rest or local foraging (shearwaters, cape petrels) rather than actively trailing the ship. Two species — the black-browed albatross and the cape petrel — sit at the intersection of two columns (“flying past” and “following ship”), consistent with their status as flexible generalists that can either follow vessels or not depending on circumstance. The behavioural division is not a dichotomy of two clean groups. It is a structured gradient that the taxonomy anticipates.

title: Who Follows the Ship
text:
Visualization 2: Where the Behaviours Happen

Show code# Collapse each observation to one behaviour class: wake-follower,
# water-user, or flying past, using the dominant recorded behaviour.
obs_class <- bird_ship |>
  filter(species_label %in% top_species,
         !is.na(latitude), !is.na(longitude),
         between(latitude, -75, 10),
         between(longitude, 100, 220)) |>
  mutate(
    behaviour_class = case_when(
      following_ship | accompanying             ~ "Wake-followers",
      sitting_on_water & !flying_past           ~ "Water-users",
      flying_past                               ~ "Passers-by",
      TRUE                                      ~ "Other"
    ),
    behaviour_class = factor(behaviour_class,
                             levels = c("Wake-followers",
                                        "Passers-by",
                                        "Water-users",
                                        "Other"))
  ) |>
  filter(behaviour_class != "Other") |>
  # Bin longitudes past the antimeridian (east of 180) into positive space
  mutate(lon_wrapped = ifelse(longitude < 0, longitude + 360, longitude))

class_pal <- c(
  "Wake-followers" = "#b8412d",
  "Passers-by"     = "#4a90d9",
  "Water-users"    = "#5aab6d"
)

ggplot(obs_class,
       aes(x = lon_wrapped, y = latitude, colour = behaviour_class)) +
  # very light lat/lon grid
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-60, -40, -20, 0),
             colour = "grey90", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(120, 140, 160, 180, 200),
             colour = "grey90", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(size = 0.6, alpha = 0.25, stroke = 0) +
  facet_wrap(~ behaviour_class, nrow = 1) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = class_pal, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(120, 140, 160, 180, 200),
                     labels = c("120°E", "140°E", "160°E", "180°", "160°W")) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-60, 0, 20),
                     labels = c("60°S", "40°S", "20°S", "0°")) +
  coord_quickmap(xlim = c(100, 220), ylim = c(-75, 10)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Wake-followers cluster around the New Zealand shelf; passers-by fill the southern ocean",
    subtitle = "Observation locations by behavioural class, Australasian Seabird Mapping Scheme 1969–1990",
    x        = NULL, y = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: Te Papa Tongarewa / Australasian Seabird Mapping Scheme · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 15 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 10.5),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    strip.text    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    panel.grid    = element_blank(),
    panel.spacing = unit(1, "lines")
  )







The three-panel map takes each observation’s dominant behaviour and plots where in the ocean it was recorded, from the Australian coast in the west to roughly 160°W in the east. The panels differ. Wake-following sightings, in red, cluster strikingly around the waters immediately east and south of New Zealand, with a second concentration along the eastern Australian shelf — the areas where the commercial trawl and longline fishery was most active in this period, and where the hungry, ship-following albatrosses and giant petrels would most reliably find food scraps. The passer-by cloud, in blue, is diffuse across nearly the entire range — a reflection of how the smaller tube-noses move through the southern ocean on their own migratory and foraging business regardless of the ships present. Water-users, in green, share the coastal footprint of the wake-followers but extend into the open ocean wherever sightings were recorded. The shape of each panel is a product of where ships actually went, not of independent random sampling of the ocean — but within that sampling footprint, the behavioural signature of each class is visible in a very different spatial signature.

title: Who Follows the Ship
text:
Takeaway
The Australasian seabird log book, assembled over two decades by observers on ships passing through one of the wildest seabird theatres on earth, is rich enough that the behavioural structure of the community emerges without any modelling at all. Species do not interact with ships uniformly. They split into groups the taxonomy predicts: the big pelagic scavengers — wandering and royal albatross, giant petrels, cape petrels — trail vessels and cluster in the same ocean patches as the commercial fisheries that those vessels fed into. The smaller tube-noses — fairy prions, sooty shearwaters, Wilson’s storm petrel — mostly ignore ships and distribute widely across the southern ocean.
The dataset predates the period in which longline bycatch was recognised as a principal cause of albatross decline, and it predates the spatial management tools now used to mitigate that decline. What it shows, in retrospect, is the behavioural substrate the problem was built on. The exact same species that follow ships in the 1975 log are the species now documented as most vulnerable to hook-and-line mortality. The log book did not know that yet. It simply recorded what the birds did. The continuity between the behaviour and the modern conservation record is a good reminder that historical natural-history data, collected without a specific management question in mind, can turn out to answer one.

title: The Transition That Stalled
text:
Background
The WHO Global Health Expenditure Database, maintained since 2000, is the closest thing the development community has to a universal ledger of how the world pays for health. For 195 countries and territories, it records every dollar of health spending each year, decomposed by source — domestic government, domestic private households and firms, or external donors — and by financing scheme, the mechanism that channels the money through. The framework it encodes is not neutral. Since a landmark 2010 World Health Report, the WHO has argued that countries move, or ought to move, through a predictable fiscal transition in health: as states grow richer and their administrative capacity strengthens, government health spending rises as a share of total health expenditure, households’ out-of-pocket (OOP) burden falls, and foreign aid eventually tapers off. Thailand in the 2000s is the canonical demonstration. Rwanda after 1994 is the case studied most intensely by Indermit Gill and the Economic Growth Center. Ghana, Vietnam, Turkey are the other names people cite.
The empirical question — against which all three of my graduate advisors would push me to hold any optimistic claim — is whether the transition is actually occurring at scale, or whether it is a success story reconstructed from a small number of cases and a mostly-flat global average. For most poor countries, the absence of government capacity to run a tax-funded health system is not a transient problem. The OOP share sits high, donor aid fills a fraction of the gap, and when donors leave (as PEPFAR and the Global Fund increasingly have, under the 2025 US aid suspension) the gap falls back onto households. The data released this week covers exactly the period in which this claim is testable.

title: The Transition That Stalled
text:
Motivation & Research Question
The fiscal transition makes a specific, testable prediction. In any given country, over time, one should see the government share of total health expenditure rising and the household OOP share falling. Countries should sort themselves along this trajectory by income level: richer countries further along it, poorer countries still at the starting point but moving in the same direction. A transition that is not happening looks different. The shares stay roughly constant at the country level; the global picture is dominated by new aid flows that replace, rather than catalyse, domestic spending; and the households of the poorest countries continue carrying a share of health costs that households in the richest countries have not carried for half a century.
Research question: Across 195 countries and 24 years, is the fiscal transition in health financing — the expected shift from out-of-pocket toward pooled government financing — actually visible in the data, or does the out-of-pocket burden on households in low-income countries sit structurally unchanged?

title: The Transition That Stalled
text:
Data Preparation

Show code# Pull the % of CHE rows for source composition (government / private / external)
comp <- health_spending |>
  filter(str_ends(indicator_code, "_che")) |>
  mutate(
    source = case_when(
      indicator_code == "gghed_che" ~ "Government",
      indicator_code == "pvtd_che"  ~ "Private (domestic)",
      indicator_code == "ext_che"   ~ "External aid"
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(source)) |>
  select(country_name, iso3_code, year, source, value)

# Out-of-pocket share specifically, from financing_schemes
oop <- financing_schemes |>
  filter(indicator_code == "hf3_che") |>
  select(country_name, iso3_code, year, oop_share = value)

# Country income groups from the World Bank classification, embedded as a
# lookup rather than joined via an external call.  (These reflect the 2023
# classification; a small number of countries have shifted groups over time.)
# Grouping is coarse because it only needs to stratify trajectories, not to
# classify individual countries in a policy sense.
income_group <- tribble(
  ~group,              ~iso3,
  "High income",       c("USA","CAN","GBR","DEU","FRA","ITA","ESP","JPN","KOR",
                         "AUS","NZL","NLD","SWE","NOR","DNK","FIN","CHE","BEL",
                         "AUT","IRL","PRT","ISR","SGP","ARE","SAU","CZE","POL",
                         "HUN","EST","LVA","LTU","SVK","SVN","HRV","URY","CHL",
                         "PAN","TTO","BRB","BHS"),
  "Upper-middle",      c("CHN","RUS","BRA","MEX","ARG","TUR","MYS","THA","ZAF",
                         "COL","PER","ECU","DOM","GTM","PRY","VEN","CUB","CRI",
                         "BGR","ROU","SRB","ALB","MKD","BLR","KAZ","AZE","ARM",
                         "GEO","IRQ","IRN","JOR","LBN","TUN","DZA","LBY","NAM",
                         "BWA","GAB","MUS","JAM"),
  "Lower-middle",      c("IND","IDN","PHL","VNM","PAK","BGD","EGY","NGA","KEN",
                         "GHA","ZMB","ZWE","CMR","CIV","SEN","MAR","LKA","KHM",
                         "MMR","LAO","NPL","MNG","UZB","KGZ","TJK","PSE","YEM",
                         "SYR","SDN","AGO","BEN","COG","HTI","HND","SLV","NIC",
                         "BOL","TLS","PNG","STP"),
  "Low income",        c("AFG","ETH","TZA","UGA","RWA","BDI","MLI","BFA","NER",
                         "TCD","CAF","COD","SOM","ERI","SSD","GIN","GMB","GNB",
                         "SLE","LBR","MOZ","MWI","MDG","COM","TGO","PRK","SYR")
) |>
  unnest(iso3) |>
  rename(iso3_code = iso3)

# Keep only countries we classified (coverage ~80% of WHO set)
oop_grp <- oop |>
  inner_join(income_group, by = "iso3_code")

comp_grp <- comp |>
  inner_join(income_group, by = "iso3_code") |>
  mutate(group = factor(group, levels = c("Low income", "Lower-middle",
                                          "Upper-middle", "High income")))

cat("Total country-years in health_spending:", nrow(comp), "\n")

Total country-years in health_spending: 13203 

Show codecat("Countries classified into an income group:",
    n_distinct(comp_grp$iso3_code), "\n")

Countries classified into an income group: 145 

Show codecat("Date range:", min(comp$year), "–", max(comp$year), "\n\n")

Date range: 2000 – 2023 

Show code# Headline numbers for the motivation
latest <- oop_grp |>
  filter(year == 2022) |>
  group_by(group) |>
  summarise(median_oop = median(oop_share, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
cat("Median out-of-pocket share of health spending, 2022, by income group:\n")

Median out-of-pocket share of health spending, 2022, by income group:

Show codelatest |> mutate(median_oop = paste0(round(median_oop, 1), "%")) |> print()

# A tibble: 4 × 2
  group        median_oop
  <chr>        <chr>     
1 High income  16.3%     
2 Low income   42.9%     
3 Lower-middle 41.5%     
4 Upper-middle 30.8%

title: The Transition That Stalled
section: Visualization 1: The Financing Transition, Four Income Groups
text:
Visualization 1: The Financing Transition, Four Income Groups

Show code# Median country share in each income group × year × source
group_trend <- comp_grp |>
  filter(!is.na(value), year >= 2000, year <= 2023) |>
  group_by(group, year, source) |>
  summarise(
    p25    = quantile(value, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    median = median(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    p75    = quantile(value, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(source = factor(source,
                         levels = c("Government",
                                    "Private (domestic)",
                                    "External aid")))

source_pal <- c(
  "Government"         = "#2a6b7a",
  "Private (domestic)" = "#b8412d",
  "External aid"       = "#d4af37"
)

ggplot(group_trend, aes(x = year, colour = source, fill = source)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = p25, ymax = p75), alpha = 0.18, colour = NA) +
  geom_line(aes(y = median), linewidth = 1) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = source_pal, name = NULL) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = source_pal, guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(scale = 1, accuracy = 1),
                     limits = c(0, 100), breaks = seq(0, 100, 25)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2000, 2010, 2020),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.02))) +
  facet_wrap(~ group, nrow = 1) +
  labs(
    title    = "Rich countries finished the fiscal transition decades ago — poor countries have not started",
    subtitle = "Share of current health expenditure by source, median country in each World Bank income group, 2000–2023",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Share of current health expenditure",
    caption  = "Source: WHO Global Health Expenditure Database · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 16 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 10.5),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    strip.text    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.spacing.x = unit(1.1, "lines")
  )







The four panels read, from left to right, as four successive stages of economic development. In the left-most panel, the median low-income country derives roughly 20 percent of its health spending from government, 50 to 60 percent from private sources (the vast majority of which is direct out-of-pocket payment from households), and the rest from external aid that peaks around 2010 and has drifted down since. Across the twenty-four years of the record, the government share rises modestly; the private share barely moves; the external share declines without a corresponding increase in domestic government revenue taking its place. In the right-most panel, high-income countries sit at roughly 70 percent government, 30 percent private, and less than 1 percent external aid — a composition they reached before the period in this record began. The two middle income groups sit between these two states. The upper-middle group has visibly moved toward the high-income profile over the twenty-four years; the lower-middle group’s trajectory is much flatter. The ordering is exactly what the fiscal-transition thesis predicts. The speed of movement, for the countries that most need it, is not.

title: The Transition That Stalled
text:
Visualization 2: The Out-of-Pocket Trap

Show code# For each country, compare OOP share in 2000 (or earliest year ≥ 2000)
# with OOP share in 2022 (or latest year ≤ 2022).
oop_change <- oop_grp |>
  filter(!is.na(oop_share), year >= 2000, year <= 2022) |>
  group_by(iso3_code, country_name, group) |>
  summarise(
    start_year  = min(year),
    start_oop   = oop_share[which.min(year)],
    end_year    = max(year),
    end_oop     = oop_share[which.max(year)],
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(end_year - start_year >= 10,
         !is.na(start_oop), !is.na(end_oop))

# Highlight a small set of countries whose trajectories are instructive
highlight <- c("RWA", "THA", "TUR", "CHN", "IND", "BGD",
               "NGA", "AFG", "VNM", "GHA", "KEN", "USA", "DEU")

oop_plot <- oop_change |>
  mutate(
    is_hl    = iso3_code %in% highlight,
    trend    = case_when(
      end_oop < start_oop - 5 ~ "Reducing OOP reliance",
      end_oop > start_oop + 5 ~ "Rising OOP reliance",
      TRUE                    ~ "Little change"
    ),
    trend    = factor(trend, levels = c("Reducing OOP reliance",
                                        "Little change",
                                        "Rising OOP reliance")),
    group    = factor(group, levels = c("Low income", "Lower-middle",
                                        "Upper-middle", "High income"))
  )

trend_pal <- c(
  "Reducing OOP reliance" = "#2a6b7a",
  "Little change"         = "#bfb8a8",
  "Rising OOP reliance"   = "#b8412d"
)

ggplot(oop_plot, aes(x = start_oop, y = end_oop)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0,
              colour = "grey70", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = trend), size = 2.3, alpha = 0.75) +
  geom_point(data = filter(oop_plot, is_hl),
             aes(colour = trend), size = 3.2, stroke = 0.7, shape = 21,
             fill = "white") +
  geom_text(data = filter(oop_plot, is_hl),
            aes(label = iso3_code), hjust = -0.25, vjust = 0.2,
            size = 3.1, colour = "grey15", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = trend_pal, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(scale = 1, accuracy = 1),
                     limits = c(0, 80), breaks = seq(0, 80, 20)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(scale = 1, accuracy = 1),
                     limits = c(0, 80), breaks = seq(0, 80, 20)) +
  facet_wrap(~ group, nrow = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = 70, y = 5,
           label = "below line:\nOOP fell",
           size = 3, colour = "#2a6b7a", hjust = 1,
           fontface = "italic") +
  annotate("text", x = 5, y = 75,
           label = "above line:\nOOP rose",
           size = 3, colour = "#b8412d", hjust = 0,
           fontface = "italic") +
  labs(
    title    = "Rwanda and Thailand cut their out-of-pocket share in half; most low-income countries did not move",
    subtitle = "Out-of-pocket share of health expenditure, start vs end of the record (2000–2022) — labelled: exemplar cases",
    x        = "OOP share at start of observed period",
    y        = "OOP share at end",
    caption  = "Source: WHO Global Health Expenditure Database · TidyTuesday 2026 Wk 16 · Zhijun He · 2026-04-17"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 10.5),
    plot.caption  = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    strip.text    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.spacing.x = unit(0.9, "lines")
  )







The second view zooms in on the single most consequential share in the composition — out-of-pocket. Each point is one country, with its OOP share at the start of its observed record on the horizontal axis and at the end on the vertical. Countries on the dashed 45-degree line ended where they started; countries below the line cut their OOP reliance; countries above it pushed it up. The country-label highlights are deliberately chosen. Rwanda (RWA) and Thailand (THA) are the textbook cases of the fiscal transition working: both began with over 30 percent OOP reliance and cut it dramatically through, respectively, community-based health insurance (Mutuelles de Santé) and the 30-Baht universal coverage scheme. Turkey (TUR) and China (CHN) sit in the same family. But Nigeria (NGA), India (IND), Bangladesh (BGD), Afghanistan (AFG) are the other side of the picture: OOP shares in the 60–80 percent range at the start, in the 60–80 percent range at the end. Among the low-income countries in the left-most panel, only Rwanda and a handful of others have moved meaningfully below the dashed line. For most, a quarter-century of growth, donor engagement, and SDG-era rhetoric about universal coverage has left the household’s share of the health bill essentially untouched.

title: The Transition That Stalled
text:
Takeaway
The WHO database tells a story that is, on its face, two stories. At the group level, the fiscal-transition thesis is validated — the ordering of country income groups along the trajectory from high-OOP to high-government-share is exactly as predicted, and the movement over twenty-four years is in the predicted direction. At the country level, inside the lowest-income group, the story is different. The gradient between income groups is real; the gradient within the low-income group, the slope of transition year-over-year, is mostly flat. A small number of countries — Rwanda being, for well-documented institutional reasons, the one most studied — have done the transition. Most have not.
The implication depends on what claim the data is being used to support. A claim that “development raises state capacity, which builds pooled health financing” is consistent with the data once countries graduate into middle-income status. A claim that “the fiscal transition is happening globally” is not consistent with the data for the poorest sixty-odd countries. And a claim that “donor exit will be filled by domestic revenue as countries grow richer” is, at least over a twenty-four-year window, the riskiest of the three. The households in the low-income panel of this figure are where the gap between that assumption and the data actually lives.

title: PCL · ICA 01 — Introduction & Onboarding
text:
Key takeaways
The onboarding has three latent design decisions worth flagging because they affect everything downstream:
Why GitHub PAT, not password. GitHub deprecated password authentication for git operations in 2021. Every push, pull, and clone now requires either an SSH key or a Personal Access Token. The token’s repo scope is what lets RStudio’s terminal authenticate quarto publish gh-pages without storing credentials in plaintext. The instruction “the password will not show after pasting it” is a small but critical detail — terminal echo is suppressed for security, and a few seconds of “did it work?” panic is normal.
Why local path avoiding iCloud / Google Drive. Cloud-sync directories race with Git’s own file locking, producing intermittent corruption that’s nearly impossible to debug after the fact. The right home for a Git repo is a non-synced ~/Documents/GitHub/ style location — the local-only path keeps Git as the only authority over .git/ internals.
Why RStudio’s .RData restore is off. Restoring the workspace between sessions silently re-introduces variables that may not be reproducible from the current code, hiding mistakes and creating drift between what the script says it does and what it actually does. Turning it off forces every session to rebuild state from code, which is the whole point of reproducible analysis.

title: PCL · ICA 01 — Introduction & Onboarding
text:
Status check
If I can answer “yes” to each of the leaves in the toolchain and identity branches, I’m ready to follow ICA-02 instructions without further setup detours.

title: PCL · ICA 02 — Review: File Organization, Git, Keyboard
text:
Pre-class learning map
ICA 02’s three readings cover what looks like three separate topics — file organization, Git/GitHub, and keyboard shortcuts — but they share one underlying question: how do you keep state legible across time and across collaborators? File organization makes the spatial structure of a project legible; Git makes the temporal history legible; keyboard shortcuts make your own intent legible to the editor without breaking flow. title: PCL · ICA 02 — Review: File Organization, Git, Keyboard
text:
Three threads worth pulling
Why raw data must stay untouched. The discipline of “never edit a raw file” is what makes results reproducible. If population.csv is the original from the source and population-cleaned.csv is the output of population-process.qmd, anyone can re-run the script and reproduce the cleaned file. The moment you edit population.csv directly — even just to fix a typo — the lineage breaks and “what did this come from” stops being answerable. The data/raw/ + data/processed/ split is the file-system embodiment of this principle.
Why relative paths beat absolute paths. ~/Desktop/STAT212/... works on my machine and breaks on yours; ./data/file.csv works on both, because it’s defined with respect to the project root rather than the user. The same logic that makes Git portable across machines makes relative paths portable. Web URLs are the only exception — they’re already universal.
Why merge conflicts aren’t bugs but features. A merge conflict is Git refusing to silently overwrite one collaborator’s work with another’s. The conflict markers (<<<<<<< / ======= / >>>>>>>) are Git saying “I can’t decide which version is right; you have to.” Resolving a conflict is a tiny act of editorial judgment — looking at both versions, deciding which to keep (or merging them by hand), then committing the resolution. Annoying in the moment, but vastly better than the alternative of losing work.
crumbs: 20  PCL · ICA 02 — Review: File Organization, Git, Keyboard

title: PCL · ICA 02 — Review: File Organization, Git, Keyboard
text:
Quick keyboard wins
The shortcuts that pay off fastest in everyday RStudio work, ranked by how often they fire:

Cmd Return — run current line. Replaces “click in line, click Run button.”
Cmd Option I — insert code chunk. Replaces typing ```{r} and the closing fence by hand.
Cmd Shift M — pipe |>. The pipe is the most-typed three-character sequence in tidyverse code; this shortcut alone saves real time.
Cmd Shift C — toggle comment on selected lines. Indispensable for “let me try this without that block running.”
Cmd Shift K — cheat sheet for all other shortcuts.
crumbs: 20  PCL · ICA 02 — Review: File Organization, Git, Keyboard

title: PCL · ICA 02 — Review: File Organization, Git, Keyboard
text:
Portfolio inspection
The fourth piece of ICA-02 prep is opening my portfolio GitHub repo and noting how files are organized. The structure follows the conventions in the file-organization reading: a top-level Quarto book project (_quarto.yml, index.qmd, 404.qmd) with chapter directories (pv/, tt/, pcl/, ica/, extra/) as siblings, plus assets/ and style/ for shared resources, and docs/ as the rendered output. No nested repos, no raw data lives next to code, paths inside _quarto.yml are all relative — the design follows the principles the readings argue for.

title: PCL · ICA 03 — Advanced Data Visualization
text:
Pre-class learning map
R4DS Chapter 1 walks through ggplot2 not as a list of geoms but as a grammar — a small number of orthogonal pieces that compose. The chapter’s central pedagogical move is showing that almost every plot you’ll ever make is data + aesthetic mapping + geom + (sometimes) facets + (sometimes) labels, with each piece making one independent decision. The mindmap below preserves that orthogonality rather than flattening it into a list of “here are the 30 geoms.”

        aes() lives in ggplot or in geom

title: PCL · ICA 03 — Advanced Data Visualization
section: Three things the chapter wants you to internalize
text:
Three things the chapter wants you to internalize
The grammar is compositional, not enumerative. A scatterplot colored by species with a smoothed trend line is ggplot(data, aes(x, y, color = species)) + geom_point() + geom_smooth(). Three independent decisions: (1) what data, (2) what mapping, (3) what marks. Memorizing “the scatter-with-trend recipe” misses the point — once you see the grammar, you can build any plot by deciding each piece independently. This is why the chapter spends pages on aes() before introducing more than a couple of geoms.
aes() vs constants is the single most-confused detail. geom_point(aes(color = species)) says “let species drive the color”; geom_point(color = "blue") says “make every point blue.” Putting color = "blue" inside aes() gets you a confusing legend with one entry called “blue,” because ggplot tries to treat the literal string "blue" as a variable level. This trips up almost everyone the first time.
Inheritance flows from ggplot() down to geoms. Aesthetics declared in ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) are inherited by every geom layered on top, so subsequent geoms don’t need to repeat them. Per-geom aes() blocks override or extend the global mapping. This is what makes ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) + geom_point() + geom_smooth() work without reciting aes(x, y) twice.
crumbs: 21  PCL · ICA 03 — Advanced Data Visualization

title: PCL · ICA 03 — Advanced Data Visualization
text:
Why “advanced” data viz starts here
ICA 03 is labeled Advanced Data Viz, but the prep is R4DS Chapter 1 — the introductory chapter. The reason isn’t that the course is starting from scratch; it’s that advanced visualization in this course means using the grammar deliberately rather than instinctively. The exercises and TidyTuesdays later in the semester will ask for things like “recreate this NYT chart with a custom theme and an annotation layer that draws attention to a specific subset” — which only works if the grammar’s joints are already familiar enough to recombine without thinking. The chapter is review for someone who has used ggplot before, but it’s review with the explicit goal of making the joints visible.
