title: PCL · ICA 04 — Advanced Spatial Visualization
text:
Pre-class learning map
A spatial dataset is a regular tibble plus one extra column — a geometry column — and a piece of metadata that says how those numbers correspond to real places on Earth. The metadata is the Coordinate Reference System (CRS), and almost every spatial-data bug traces back to either not knowing the CRS or mixing two CRSs in the same operation. The mindmap below organizes the prep around that single pivot: data has geometry, geometry needs a CRS, and the CRS dictates everything else.

title: PCL · ICA 04 — Advanced Spatial Visualization
section: Three concepts the video assumes you internalize
text:
Three concepts the video assumes you internalize
Datum vs projection are not the same thing. A datum is a model of Earth’s shape (typically an ellipsoid plus a reference origin); a projection is a transformation from that ellipsoid to a flat plane. WGS84 is a datum; Mercator is a projection. Talking about “the WGS84 projection” is technically wrong even though everyone does it. The distinction matters when you’re debugging — st_set_crs(data, 4326) declares the datum/coordinate system; if the underlying coordinates are actually in Web Mercator meters, that declaration silently corrupts the data.
Every projection distorts something. Mercator preserves shape and angle but inflates polar areas to absurd sizes (Greenland looks bigger than Africa even though Africa is ~14× larger). Albers Equal Area preserves area but distorts shape. Lambert Conformal preserves shape locally but distorts area. There is no projection that preserves both — this is a mathematical fact (Gauss’s Theorema Egregium). When choosing a CRS for a map, the question is “which distortion is least harmful for what this map is trying to show?”
st_set_crs() and st_transform() look similar but do opposite things. st_set_crs() labels coordinates with a CRS without changing them — used when the CRS metadata was lost or never set. st_transform() actually reprojects the coordinates from one CRS to another — the numerical values change. Confusing them produces silent wrong answers: st_set_crs() on data already labeled with a CRS overwrites the label without recomputing, putting your polygons in completely wrong locations on a map.
crumbs: 22  PCL · ICA 04 — Advanced Spatial Visualization

title: PCL · ICA 04 — Advanced Spatial Visualization
text:
EPSG codes worth memorizing
The five codes that come up constantly in a US-centered spatial workflow:

4326 — WGS84 lon/lat. The default for almost everything that gets shared globally; what GPS uses; what Leaflet expects.
3857 — Web Mercator. The CRS Google Maps and most slippy-tile web maps use for display.
4269 — NAD83 lon/lat. The CRS that most US Census data ships in.
2163 — US National Atlas Equal Area. A reasonable choice for projecting the Lower 48 when you want equal-area distortion.
5070 — NAD83 / Conus Albers. Another equal-area projection optimized for the contiguous US, increasingly the recommended default.
crumbs: 22  PCL · ICA 04 — Advanced Spatial Visualization

title: PCL · ICA 04 — Advanced Spatial Visualization
text:
sf in one sentence
A sf object is a regular tibble where one column holds geometry (POINTs, LINESTRINGs, POLYGONs, MULTIPOLYGONs) and the whole tibble carries one CRS attribute — which means every dplyr verb you already know works on it, plus a layer of st_* functions that do the spatial operations (intersect, buffer, distance, area). The Moodle checkpoint will likely test exactly this collapse: spatial data in R is not a special data structure, it is regular data plus one careful piece of metadata.
crumbs: 22  PCL · ICA 04 — Advanced Spatial Visualization

title: PCL · ICA 06 — Advanced Data Wrangling I

Pre-class learning map
R4DS Chapters 12, 13, 16, and 17 don’t introduce new dplyr verbs — they introduce type-specific toolkits for the four atomic vector classes you’ll spend most of your wrangling life on: logicals, numbers, factors, dates. The grammar from Ch1 (mutate, filter, summarize) stays the same; what changes is the function library you reach for inside each verb depending on what type the column is. crumbs: 23  PCL · ICA 06 — Advanced Data Wrangling I

title: PCL · ICA 06 — Advanced Data Wrangling I
section: Three concepts these chapters quietly hinge on
text:
Three concepts these chapters quietly hinge on
== is treacherous on floats; near() is what you actually want. The classic gotcha: sqrt(2)^2 == 2 returns FALSE in R because floating-point arithmetic introduces tiny rounding errors. The right pattern is near(sqrt(2)^2, 2) which checks equality within tolerance. R4DS Ch12 mentions this in passing but it’s the source of countless silent filter bugs — filter(df, value == 0.1) may drop rows where the value is 0.09999999999999998. The general rule: use == only on integers, characters, and factors; use near() on doubles.
Factors aren’t just labeled strings — they’re strings with a predetermined level set. This matters in two places: (1) ggplot uses factor levels as the x-axis order, so fct_reorder(species, body_mass) is how you sort a bar chart by something other than alphabetical; (2) case_when() with factor outputs needs all branches to return the same factor with the same levels, or you get NA for unmatched levels. The forcats package exists because manipulating levels (reorder, rename, collapse, lump) is much more common than people expect, and base R’s tools for it are awkward.
NA in logical operations follows three-valued logic, and the rules aren’t intuitive. NA & FALSE is FALSE (because regardless of what NA actually is, ANDing with FALSE gives FALSE). But NA & TRUE is NA (because if NA is FALSE, the answer is FALSE; if NA is TRUE, the answer is TRUE — we can’t decide). Same logic for OR: NA | TRUE is TRUE (always true), but NA | FALSE is NA. This is Kleene logic, not a bug. The takeaway: filtering with is.na() is explicit and safe; comparisons that produce NA get silently dropped by filter() because filter() treats NA as “not TRUE.”
crumbs: 23  PCL · ICA 06 — Advanced Data Wrangling I

title: PCL · ICA 06 — Advanced Data Wrangling I
text:
The unifying move
All four chapters teach the same shape: a mutate() or filter() or summarize() call where the value of the verb’s argument is a type-specific function. The skeleton is always:
df |>
  mutate(new_col = TYPE_SPECIFIC_FN(old_col)) |>
  filter(LOGICAL_FN(some_col)) |>
  summarize(stat = NUMERIC_SUMMARY(some_col), .by = group_col)
What changes between chapters is which TYPE_SPECIFIC_FN you reach for:



Column type
Common toolkit




Logical
if_else, case_when, any, all


Numeric
round, lag, cumsum, min_rank


Factor
fct_reorder, fct_lump, fct_recode


Date/time
year, wday, month, floor_date



The dplyr verbs are the spine; the type-specific functions are the muscle.
crumbs: 23  PCL · ICA 06 — Advanced Data Wrangling I

title: PCL · ICA 06 — Advanced Data Wrangling I
section: Why this comes before joins and pivots
text:
Why this comes before joins and pivots
ICA 06 is “Adv Wrangling I” — and by week 6 of STAT 212, the grammar from Ch1 + the basic wrangling verbs from Ch3-5 are already in muscle memory. What was missing was the toolkit for handling specific column types cleanly: how to bin a numeric variable, how to reorder a factor for plotting, how to extract day-of-week from a date column to detect weekly patterns. These four chapters fill in that gap, which makes the next steps (joins, pivots, missing data) workable on real-world data — where columns aren’t always nicely numeric and dates don’t parse themselves.
crumbs: 23  PCL · ICA 06 — Advanced Data Wrangling I

title: PCL · ICA 07 — Advanced Data Wrangling II
text:
Pre-class learning map
R4DS Chapters 14, 15, and 19 extend ICA 06’s “type-specific toolkit” framing in two directions. Ch14 (strings) and Ch15 (regex) add the toolkit for text columns — the ones that don’t parse cleanly into numbers, factors, or dates and require pattern-based extraction. Ch19 (joins) introduces a different kind of operation entirely: how to bring two tables together via a shared key. Single-table wrangling has limits — most real datasets live across multiple tables connected by IDs, and the moment you need information from both, you need joins. The mindmap organizes these three chapters as two toolkits and one paradigm shift.

title: PCL · ICA 07 — Advanced Data Wrangling II
section: Three concepts these chapters quietly hinge on
text:
Three concepts these chapters quietly hinge on
Backslashes in regex are doubly escaped in R strings. To match a literal period in regex, the regex engine wants \. — but R strings interpret \. first and pass . to regex, which means “any character.” The right pattern in R is "\\.": R string parser reduces \\ to \, then regex engine sees \. and matches a literal period. This is the source of more wrangling bugs than any other regex issue. Use raw strings (r"(\.)", R 4.0+) when you have many backslashes — it suspends R-level escaping. Or test patterns in str_view() first, which prints what regex actually receives.
left_join() is almost always what you want; inner_join() is what you sometimes want. New users often default to inner_join() because it sounds tidier (“only matched rows”), but in practice it silently drops rows you wanted to keep. The diagnostic question is: which table is your “main” table? The main table goes on the left, you left_join() lookup tables onto it, and you check NA in the new columns to see what didn’t match. inner_join() is appropriate only when every row of both tables must have a match, which is rarer than it sounds.
Many-to-many joins multiply rows, and dplyr now warns you for a reason. If table A has 3 rows for id = 1 and table B has 4 rows for id = 1, joining them produces 12 rows for id = 1 — every combination. This is sometimes what you want (combining time-varying attributes), but most of the time it’s a sign that one of your “keys” isn’t actually unique. Check with count(df, key) |> filter(n > 1) before joining. Since dplyr 1.1.0 (2023), many-to-many joins emit a warning unless you pass relationship = "many-to-many" explicitly, which forces you to acknowledge the choice.
crumbs: 24  PCL · ICA 07 — Advanced Data Wrangling II

title: PCL · ICA 07 — Advanced Data Wrangling II
section: Strings + regex + joins, in one sentence
text:
Strings + regex + joins, in one sentence
If the previous PCL was “what do you do with each column type,” this one is “what do you do when columns are messy strings and what do you do when the data you need is in another table.” Together with Ch12-13 (logical, numeric) and Ch16-17 (factor, date), the toolkit is now complete for almost any single-row-per-observation dataset that arrives in tabular form.
crumbs: 24  PCL · ICA 07 — Advanced Data Wrangling II

title: PCL · ICA 07 — Advanced Data Wrangling II
text:
Practical heuristic for joins
A small decision tree that captures most wrangling-time reasoning:







Question
Answer




Adding columns to your main table?
mutating join (left_join, inner_join, …)


Filtering rows of your main table by another table’s contents?
filtering join (semi_join, anti_join)


Want to keep rows that didn’t match?
left_join (then NA will appear in new columns)


Want to drop rows that didn’t match?
inner_join


Want to find rows that should match but don’t?
anti_join


Both tables have a column with the same name as the key?
by = "key"


Different names?
by = c("a" = "b")


Worried about duplicate keys?
count() first, then join with relationship = "..." set explicitly



Joins are the gateway from “single-table dplyr” to “real-world relational data.” The rest of STAT 212 — APIs returning nested JSON, web scraping that produces multiple coordinated tables, SQL — all assume you’re fluent in this paradigm.

title: PCL · ICA 08 — Missing Data
text:
Pre-class learning map
R4DS Chapter 18 makes one fundamental distinction: missing data comes in two flavors that need different tools. Explicit missingness is a row that exists with NA in some column — visible, countable, easy to handle. Implicit missingness is a row that should exist but doesn’t — invisible to anything that doesn’t already know what’s expected. The chapter’s core skill is learning to see the second kind, because once you see it, you can decide how to handle it; until you see it, your analysis is silently wrong on the cases that didn’t make it into the data. The mindmap below organizes the chapter around this two-flavor framing plus the operational toolkit for each.

title: PCL · ICA 08 — Missing Data
section: Three concepts the chapter quietly hinges on
text:
Three concepts the chapter quietly hinges on
x == NA doesn’t work; is.na(x) does. This is the most-bitten gotcha in all of R. NA == NA evaluates to NA, not TRUE, because R’s logic is “we don’t know what either is, so we don’t know if they’re equal.” Same for x == NA. The only way to test for missingness is is.na(x), which is a special predicate that returns TRUE/FALSE (never NA). Every wrangling beginner writes filter(df, x == NA) once, gets an empty result, and is confused; this is why.
filter() silently drops rows where the condition is NA. filter(df, x > 0) keeps rows where x > 0 is TRUE, drops rows where it’s FALSE, and also drops rows where it’s NA (because NA > 0 is NA, which filter() treats as “not TRUE”). This means filtering on a column with missing values silently shrinks your dataset in ways you might not notice. The defensive pattern: if you suspect missing values matter, use filter(df, x > 0 | is.na(x)) to keep NA rows explicitly, or check count(df, is.na(x)) first to see how many would be dropped.
Implicit missingness is the invisible kind that bites the hardest. If you have monthly sales data and February’s row is just missing — not NA, but absent — every plot, every summary, every mean() will silently treat the dataset as 11-month instead of 12-month, with no warning. The detection move: build an expected universe (expand(df, year, month) or full_seq() for numeric ranges), then anti_join() your data against it to find what’s absent. Or use complete(df, year, month) to fill in the missing rows with NA so they become explicit and countable. After surfacing, you have to decide why — was Feb skipped? Did the system go down? Was the product not yet launched? — and that decision determines whether to drop, impute, or flag.

title: PCL · ICA 08 — Missing Data
text:
A small decision tree
For most missing-data wrangling, this captures the reasoning:







Symptom
Move




Want to count missingness in each column
colSums(is.na(df)) or naniar::miss_var_summary(df)


Want to keep only complete rows
drop_na()


Want to keep rows where specific cols are non-NA
drop_na(col1, col2)


Want to replace NA with a constant
replace_na(list(col = 0)) inside mutate()


Want to carry a value forward (e.g., last observed)
fill(col, .direction = "down")


Want NA to be a visible category in a plot
fct_na_value_to_level() on the factor


Want to find rows that should exist but don’t
complete() or anti_join() against template


Computing a mean and getting NA back
add na.rm = TRUE to the summary

title: PCL · ICA 08 — Missing Data
text:
Why this comes after joins
ICA 08 follows ICA 07 (joins) for a reason: the moment you start joining tables, missingness becomes structural rather than incidental. A left_join produces NA in the new columns whenever the lookup fails — and those NAs are diagnostic of mismatches between key sets, not just sensor failures. Knowing how to handle missing data is therefore part of knowing how to debug a join. The two skills compose: surface the implicit missingness, decide its meaning, then drop or impute or flag accordingly.

title: PCL · ICA 08 — Missing Data
section: A note on the deeper question
text:
A note on the deeper question
Ch18 is operational — how to detect and handle missingness. It deliberately doesn’t get into the statistical question of why it’s missing (MCAR vs MAR vs MNAR — missing completely at random, missing at random, missing not at random) or what imputation strategy is justified for each. That’s downstream territory, picked up in mice and other imputation packages. The R4DS chapter is the prerequisite layer: you need to see the missingness clearly before you can reason about its mechanism. ICA 08 builds that layer.

title: PCL · ICA 09 — Functions
text:
Pre-class learning map
R4DS Chapter 25 and Peng’s Section 13.1 together answer one question: when you find yourself copy-pasting code with small variations, how do you turn that pattern into a reusable function? R4DS gives the design philosophy (when to write one, how to name and structure it, what the three function flavors are); Peng’s 13.1 supplies the missing piece — the if/else control logic that almost every function body needs to branch on its inputs. The mindmap below organizes both readings around the lifecycle of a function: recognize the pattern → name it → write the signature → write the body with control flow → test it on edge cases.

title: PCL · ICA 09 — Functions
section: Three concepts these readings quietly hinge on
text:
Three concepts these readings quietly hinge on
A function’s last expression is its return value, automatically. R doesn’t require return(x) at the end of a function — whatever expression is last in the body is what gets returned. return() is reserved for early exit, when you want to bail out of the function before reaching the end. Idiomatic R style uses implicit return for the normal path and explicit return() only for guard clauses (if (is.na(x)) return(NA) at the top). Newcomers from Python or JavaScript often write return(result) on the last line out of habit; it works, but it’s noise.
if takes a single TRUE/FALSE; if_else() (or case_when()) takes a vector. This is the most common control-flow confusion in R. Inside a function with scalar logic — “if the input is missing, do X” — use if (is.na(x)) .... Inside a mutate() where you’re branching on a column — “for each row, if the value is positive, do Y” — use if_else(col > 0, ...). Mixing them up gives one of two errors: if complains about a vector condition, or if_else() silently treats your scalar as a length-1 vector and surprises you with the result. Peng 13.1 covers if; the dplyr equivalents are the vector versions.
Variables inside a function don’t leak out, but variables outside do leak in. This is lexical scoping. If a function references a name it didn’t define, R looks for that name in the enclosing scope (typically your global workspace). This is convenient — you can use packages without passing them in — but it’s also dangerous: a function that accidentally relies on a global variable will work in your console and silently break for someone else. Defensive practice: a function should take everything it depends on as an argument (data, columns, parameters), not reach into the global environment for them.

title: PCL · ICA 09 — Functions
section: A small decision tree for function design
text:
A small decision tree for function design







Question
Default move




Does this code repeat with small variations?
Refactor into a function


What goes in front of the parens?
A verb in snake_case, distinct from base R names


First argument?
The data — so the function pipes well


What kind of function is this?
Vector (returns vector), data frame (returns tibble), plot (returns ggplot)


Does it take column names from a tibble?
Use { col } for tidy-eval embracing


Should it have side effects?
Avoid if possible — pure functions compose better


What edge cases to test?
Empty input, single element, all NA, wrong type


Branching on scalar input?
if/else if/else


Branching on a vector inside mutate()?
if_else() or case_when()

title: PCL · ICA 09 — Functions
text:
Why this comes after wrangling
ICA 09 sits at the inflection point of STAT 212. Up to ICA 08, everything has been use of existing tools — call mutate(), call geom_point(), call left_join(). From ICA 09 onward, you’re making tools. The skills compose: a wrangling pipeline you’d otherwise repeat across 5 datasets becomes a single function called 5 times. A plot specification you’d otherwise duplicate across 10 facets becomes a function returning a ggplot. This is the move from “user of dplyr” to “extender of dplyr” — and it’s also the prerequisite for ICAs 11-12 (loops and iteration), where you’ll apply functions across collections.

title: PCL · ICA 09 — Functions
section: Why if-else gets its own reading
text:
Why if-else gets its own reading
Peng 13.1 is short but central. Almost every nontrivial function has at least one if — to handle a missing argument, to branch on data type, to short-circuit on edge cases. R4DS Ch25 assumes you know if/else and focuses on function design; Peng fills in the syntax and scoping rules. Worth flagging: R’s if is an expression, not just a statement — meaning y <- if (cond) 10 else 0 is valid and idiomatic, where languages like Python require a separate ternary syntax (y = 10 if cond else 0). Treating if as an expression makes function bodies cleaner: assignments at the top, conditional logic in the middle, the result naturally falling out at the bottom.

title: PCL · ICA 10 — A Field Guide to Base R
text:
Pre-class learning map
R4DS Chapter 27 is the only chapter in the entire book deliberately about base R rather than the tidyverse. The framing matters: it’s not “use base R instead of tidyverse,” it’s “you need to read base R because the rest of the R world is written in it.” Stack Overflow answers, package source code, legacy analyses, and almost every helper function inside the tidyverse itself all use [, [[, $, apply, and for — so fluency in these is what lets you debug, extend, and learn from code outside R4DS. The mindmap below organizes the chapter around the four big topics it identifies — single-bracket subsetting, double-bracket extraction, the apply family, and for loops — plus a fifth on base plotting, with each node noting its tidyverse counterpart so the mapping is visible.
Code
mindmap
  root((ICA 10<br/>Base R))
    Subsetting with [ · multiple elements
      Five things you can put inside [ ]
        Positive integers
          x[c(3, 2, 5)]
          can repeat to grow
        Negative integers
          x[-c(1, 3)]
          drop those positions
        Logical vectors
          x[x > 0]
          like filter() but keeps NA
        Character vectors
          x[c("name1", "name2")]
          for named vectors
        Nothing
          df[, ] returns whole df
          useful for 2D dimension keep
      Data frames · two dimensions
        df[rows, cols]
        df[rows, ]
        df[, cols]
        df[df$x > 1, ]
      Tibble vs data.frame
        tibble · always returns tibble
        data.frame · single col returns vector
        drop = FALSE to force df
      dplyr equivalents
        filter() ≈ df[!is.na(x) & x > 1, ]
        arrange() ≈ df[order(x, y), ]
        select() ≈ df[, c("x", "z")]
        subset() · base hybrid
    Subsetting with [[ and $ · single element
      Data frames
        tb[[1]] · by position
        tb[["x"]] · by name
        tb$x · by name shortcut
        tb$z <- tb$x + tb$y
          base equivalent of mutate()
      Tibble strictness
        partial match · data.frame OK
        partial match · tibble warns
        unknown col · tibble warns
          surly and lazy
      Lists · the critical case
        l[1] · returns sub-list
        l[[1]] · drills into element
        l$name · same as l[[name]]
      Pepper shaker mental model
        pepper[1] · shaker with one packet
        pepper[[1]] · the packet itself
      pull() · tidyverse equivalent
        diamonds |> pull(carat) |> max()

title: PCL · ICA 10 — A Field Guide to Base R
section: Three concepts the chapter quietly hinges on
text:
Three concepts the chapter quietly hinges on
[ returns the same type; [[ drills down a level. The single most-confusing thing about R lists. l[1] returns a list of length 1 — the same type, smaller. l[[1]] returns whatever was at position 1 — a level deeper. The pepper-shaker analogy R4DS uses is exactly right: pepper[1] is a one-packet shaker, pepper[[1]] is the packet’s contents. The reason this matters: most operations you actually want to do (call a function on the contents, print the value, do arithmetic) need [[, not [. [ is for slicing collections; [[ is for unpacking a single item. New users who reach for [ everywhere end up with mysteriously nested results.
Tibbles are “lazy and surly”; data.frames are silently helpful. R4DS’s joke captures a real safety property. data.frame does partial matching on $: df$x works even when the actual column is x_value, returning the matching column without warning. tibble refuses partial matches and warns when you ask for an unknown column. This means switching from data.frame to tibble will surface bugs that were silently working — typos in column names that happened to match a prefix of a real column. This is a feature, not a regression.
Iteratively growing a vector is what makes “for loops slow” — not the loop itself. The myth that R’s for is slow is mostly wrong. What’s slow is the anti-pattern out <- c(out, new_value) inside a loop, because each iteration allocates a new vector and copies the old one. The fix: pre-allocate the output once (vector("list", n) or numeric(n)) then assign by index (out[[i]] <- ...). With pre-allocation, for is comparable in speed to lapply() and purrr::map(). The base-R apply family and tidyverse map family aren’t faster because of some clever trick — they’re faster because they pre-allocate for you.

text:

A small lookup for reading other people’s code:








What you want

df \|> mutate(z = x + y)

lapply(xs, f)

df \|> group_by(g) \|> summarize(m = mean(x))

title: PCL · ICA 10 — A Field Guide to Base R
section: Why this comes after functions and before iteration
text:
Why this comes after functions and before iteration
ICA 10 sits between Functions (ICA 09) and Loops/Iteration (ICA 11-12). The placement is deliberate: the apply family and for loops are base R’s iteration primitives, and ICA 11-12 will introduce purrr’s map_* family as the tidyverse alternative. By covering base subsetting and apply first, ICA 10 ensures you can read the iterating-with-base-R style you’ll inevitably encounter — and crucially, you’ll understand that purrr’s map functions are not magic: under the hood they’re doing what lapply() does, just with consistent return types and progress bars.
crumbs: 27  PCL · ICA 10 — A Field Guide to Base R

title: PCL · ICA 10 — A Field Guide to Base R
text:
A note on “use what works”
The chapter’s most important meta-message is the line “It’s 100% okay to write code that uses a mix of approaches, and don’t let anyone tell you otherwise!” The tidyverse-vs-base-R debate is mostly a community culture artifact, not a technical one. Production R code regularly mixes both — using map() for the iteration shape and [[ for fast extraction, using filter() for the readable chain and df$col for a quick interactive check. Fluency means knowing both vocabularies and choosing the clearer one for each piece of code. ICA 10 is what makes the second vocabulary legible.
crumbs: 27  PCL · ICA 10 — A Field Guide to Base R

title: PCL · ICA 11 — Loops & Iteration I
text:
Pre-class learning map
R4DS Chapter 26 is where ICA 09 (writing functions) and ICA 10 (base R primitives) fuse into a workflow. Once you can write a function, the next question is: how do I apply it to a hundred things? The chapter answers this with three concrete tasks rather than abstract theory: modifying multiple columns of one data frame (across), reading multiple files into one data frame (map + list_rbind), and writing one R object out to multiple files (walk / walk2). Each task is a slightly different shape of the same idea — functional programming means passing functions around so a higher-order function can do the looping for you — and each unlocks a class of analysis that copy-paste cannot reach. The mindmap below organizes the chapter around those three tasks.

title: PCL · ICA 11 — Loops & Iteration I
section: Three concepts the chapter quietly hinges on
text:
Three concepts the chapter quietly hinges on
Pass the function, don’t call it. The single most common error inside across() and map(): writing across(a:d, median()) instead of across(a:d, median). The first calls median() immediately with no input, which errors. The second passes the function median itself to across, which then calls it on each column. The mental shift: in functional programming, function names are values you can hand around. The parentheses are how you invoke a function; without them, you’re referring to the function as a thing. This is the same logic as the difference between a recipe and cooking — you give across the recipe, it does the cooking.
map() returns a list; the typed variants return vectors. map(xs, f) always returns a list, even if every result is a single number. To get a numeric vector, use map_dbl(xs, f). To get a character vector, use map_chr. To get logical, map_lgl. The choice matters because lists are slow to work with downstream — purrr::map_dbl(xs, mean) gives you a vector you can plot and summarize directly, while map(xs, mean) gives you a list you’ll have to unlist() first. The base R analogue is lapply() (list) vs vapply() (typed); the design intent is the same.
walk() is map() with the result thrown away — and that’s a feature. When iteration is for side effects (writing files, appending to a database, printing progress), the return value is uninteresting noise. walk(paths, append_file) does the same thing as map(paths, append_file) but returns the input invisibly so you can keep piping. Using map for side effects works but produces a list of NULLs or whatever the side-effecting function returned, which clutters your workspace and obscures intent. The walk family signals: “I’m doing this for the effect, not the value.”
crumbs: 28  PCL · ICA 11 — Loops & Iteration I

title: PCL · ICA 11 — Loops & Iteration I
section: Two iteration design choices worth flagging
text:
Two iteration design choices worth flagging
One complex step vs many simple steps. When you need to read 12 files and clean each one, R4DS recommends the many simple steps approach: map(read_csv) |> map(filter) |> map(mutate) |> list_rbind() rather than map(process_file) where process_file does everything. The reasoning: writing one big function tempts you to fixate on getting file 1 right before looking at the others, when in reality you should be looking at all of them holistically. Many simple steps lets you see the full collection at every stage. Even better when possible: bind the data frames before cleaning and let regular dplyr handle the rest.
The set_names() move for path data. When the filename itself contains data (year, region, version, …), set_names(basename) carries that filename forward as list-element names, then list_rbind(names_to = "year") materializes it into a column. This is the cleanest way to recover information from the directory structure without writing a custom parser. For more complex paths (multiple data fields embedded), set_names() with no argument keeps the full path, then separate_wider_delim() parses it.
crumbs: 28  PCL · ICA 11 — Loops & Iteration I

title: PCL · ICA 11 — Loops & Iteration I
section: Why this comes after functions and base R
text:
Why this comes after functions and base R
ICA 11 is the synthesis week. ICA 09 taught how to write a function; ICA 10 taught how lapply and for iterate in base R; ICA 11 introduces purrr’s map_* family as the more consistent, more pipe-friendly version of the same idea. The progression is intentional: by the time you meet map(), you know that under the hood it’s doing what lapply() does, that the function you pass is one you might have just written yourself, and that the value of doing this — over a for loop — is composability with the rest of your tidyverse pipeline. Functional programming becomes legible because all three pieces (functions, base iteration, tidyverse iteration) sit in one connected mental model.
crumbs: 28  PCL · ICA 11 — Loops & Iteration I

title: PCL · ICA 11 — Loops & Iteration I
section: A note on what this enables
text:
A note on what this enables
The skills in this chapter are what make HW07 and HW08 possible. HW07 P1 reads ~196k JSON files and binds them — that’s list.files() |> map(read_json) |> list_rbind() at scale. HW07 P2 hits the Census API repeatedly across years — that’s map(years, fetch_year) |> list_rbind(names_to = "year"). HW08 P1 scrapes 21 alphabetical pages from cheese.com — that’s map(1:21, scrape_page) |> list_rbind(). None of these are realistic without iteration. The pattern, once internalized, is the same shape every time: list the inputs, map the work function over them, bind the results.
crumbs: 28  PCL · ICA 11 — Loops & Iteration I

title: PCL · ICA 12 — Loops & Iteration II
text:
Pre-class learning map
Rebecca Barter’s Learn to purrr tutorial picks up where R4DS Ch26 stops. R4DS taught the three big tasks (across for columns, map for files, walk for outputs); Barter teaches the rest of the purrr toolkit — the map_* typed variants, map2/pmap for multi-input iteration, keep / discard / reduce / every / some for list manipulation, and the conceptual move that unlocks the most leverage: list-columns inside nested data frames, where each row of a tibble carries a whole sub-tibble (or a model object, or a plot) and dplyr verbs apply to those sub-objects via map. Once nested tibbles click, “fit a separate model for each continent and extract its coefficients into one tidy table” is one pipeline, not a script. The mindmap below organizes the tutorial around this trajectory: typed variants → multi-input → list-columns → list utilities.
Code

        common · reduce(list_of_dfs, left_join, by = "id")
      accumulate
        like reduce · returns intermediates
      every(.x, .p)
        all() for lists
        do all elements satisfy?
      some(.x, .p)
        any() for lists
        does at least one?
      has_element
        %in% for lists
    Mental model
      Vector
        many elements · same type
      List
        many elements · any type
      Data frame
        many vectors · same length
      Tibble with list-col
        each cell · arbitrary object
        unlocks per-row computation
          on plots · models · sub-tibbles
crumbs: 29  PCL · ICA 12 — Loops & Iteration II

title: PCL · ICA 12 — Loops & Iteration II
section: Three concepts the tutorial quietly hinges on
text:
Three concepts the tutorial quietly hinges on
Tibble columns can be lists, and that’s where the power lives. A regular column is a vector — every cell is a single value. A list-column is a list — every cell can be a whole object: a sub-tibble, a fitted model, a ggplot, a list of polygon coordinates. Once you have a list-column, you can use mutate() with map() to apply any function to each cell, as if it were a row-level operation on objects more complex than scalars. This is the move that makes “fit a separate model for each group” into a single pipeline rather than a for loop with manual list management. The tutorial spends a lot of time on this because it is the purrr leverage point: everything else is supporting infrastructure.
map_* choice is about output type, not capability. map(xs, mean) returns a list of length-1 numbers — you’d need to unlist() it to plot or summarize. map_dbl(xs, mean) returns a numeric vector directly. The tutorial keeps using map_df to build summary tables row-by-row, with .id to preserve the iteration name. The base R analogue is the difference between lapply (always list) and vapply (typed and length-checked) — same design intent, friendlier syntax. Picking the right variant saves a step downstream every time.
map2 is paired iteration, not Cartesian product. This catches almost everyone once. If continents = c("Asia", "Americas") and years = c(1952, 2007), map2(continents, years, plot_fn) produces two plots: Asia 1952 and Americas 2007. It does not produce four plots covering all combinations. To get the Cartesian product you’d build the combinations first (expand_grid() or crossing()) and then pmap over the resulting tibble. The pattern data |> distinct(group_a, group_b) followed by pmap() is how you safely iterate over real combinations that exist in your data.
crumbs: 29  PCL · ICA 12 — Loops & Iteration II

title: PCL · ICA 12 — Loops & Iteration II
crumbs: 29  PCL · ICA 12 — Loops & Iteration II

title: PCL · ICA 12 — Loops & Iteration II
section: Why this is the synthesis week
text:
Why this is the synthesis week
ICA 11 introduced purrr through the three R4DS use cases. ICA 12 generalizes: purrr is not a set of recipes, it’s a small algebra. map_* chooses your output type. map2 / pmap extend to multiple inputs. keep / discard / every / some answer logical questions of a list. reduce collapses a list to a single object. List-columns turn dplyr into a tool that operates on arbitrary objects, not just scalars. Once these compose, you stop thinking in loops and start thinking in transformations — what shape do I have, what shape do I want, what’s the function that gets me there?
crumbs: 29  PCL · ICA 12 — Loops & Iteration II

title: PCL · ICA 12 — Loops & Iteration II
section: A note on what this enables in practice
text:
A note on what this enables in practice
This is the layer that made several pieces of my STAT 212 work tractable. HW07 P1’s 196k JSON files: map(paths, jsonlite::read_json) |> list_rbind(). HW07 P2’s per-year ACS pulls: map(years, ~ get_acs(year = .x, ...)) |> list_rbind(names_to = "year"). HW08 P1’s pagination through cheese.com: map(1:21, scrape_page) |> list_rbind(). The TidyTuesday CNPJ analysis nests by legal_nature and computes Lorenz curves per nested sub-tibble — exact instance of the model-per-group pattern, but with a custom function instead of lm. None of these were “I wrote a loop and waited” — they were map, with the right output type, and a binary reduce or list_rbind at the end. The tutorial gives you the vocabulary; the work is recognizing the pattern.
crumbs: 29  PCL · ICA 12 — Loops & Iteration II

title: PCL · ICA 12 — Loops & Iteration II
text:
Closing thought
The tutorial’s last section title is Now go forth and purrr. Worth taking literally: every time you find yourself reaching for a for loop in R, ask whether the operation has the shape “do the same thing to every element of a collection” — and if so, what the output type is. If the answer is “list,” use map; if “numeric vector,” use map_dbl; if “data frame stacked from each step,” use map_df or map(...) |> list_rbind(). Functional programming in R becomes muscle memory after maybe 20 deliberate uses; the loop instinct fades.

title: PCL · ICA 13 — APIs I: JSON, URLs, urltools
text:
Pre-class learning map
ICA 13’s three readings introduce the infrastructure layer beneath any API call. Before you write a single line of httr2 or jsonlite, you need to understand three things: (1) JSON — the format the API will return data in, with its name/value pairs and arrays; (2) URL anatomy — the structured way to address the API endpoint, with scheme + host + path + query parameters; (3) urltools — the package that lets you manipulate URLs programmatically, encoding, parsing, and modifying query parameters at scale. These three together are what make iteration over many API requests possible; without them, you’re stuck building URLs by string concatenation and parsing JSON by hand. The mindmap below organizes the prep around these three axes plus the way they compose.

title: PCL · ICA 13 — APIs I: JSON, URLs, urltools
section: Three concepts these readings quietly hinge on
text:
Three concepts these readings quietly hinge on
JSON is just nested name-value pairs and arrays — but the nesting matters. A flat JSON response (single name-value pairs at top level) parses straight into a one-row tibble. A list of objects (an array where each element is itself a name-value object — like the results array in the Scott Lowe example) parses into a tibble with one row per element, after unnest_wider. A nested object inside another object requires another unnest_wider. Most real-world API responses combine both shapes, and reading JSON well is mostly about recognizing which shape you have at each level. The Mia Museum data from HW07 is the canonical case: each artwork is an object, and inside it creator is itself an array of nested objects (one per artist).
A URL is a structured address, not a string — and treating it like a string is the source of API bugs. When you concatenate "https://api.example.com?year=" + year, you’re treating the URL as a string. This works until the year is "2020 (revised)" or the API needs additional parameters that may or may not be present. The structured view is: a URL has six fields (scheme, domain, port, path, query, fragment), and any one of them can change independently. urltools::url_parse() materializes that structure as a data frame; param_set() and param_remove() modify the query without rebuilding the whole string. Once you think of URLs as objects rather than strings, building requests dynamically becomes safe.
Encoding is what keeps your URL parsable when values contain reserved characters. A query parameter value containing &, ?, /, =, or a space will break the URL if not encoded. The reserved-character problem is silent: the request often appears to succeed but returns wrong or empty data because the API parsed the URL differently than you intended. The fix: percent-encode the value with url_encode() (or let httr2 handle it automatically — it does). The principle: encode values, never the separators. Base R’s URLencode(reserved = TRUE) over-encodes by default and is the wrong tool here. Worth memorizing: space is %20, slash is %2F, ampersand is %26.
crumbs: 30  PCL · ICA 13 — APIs I: JSON, URLs, urltools

title: PCL · ICA 13 — APIs I: JSON, URLs, urltools
section: Why this comes before the actual API call
text:
Why this comes before the actual API call
ICA 14 will introduce httr2 and the request/response cycle. ICA 13 builds the foundation: by then you should already know what shape the data will arrive in (JSON), what form the request takes (URL with query parameters), and how to manipulate either programmatically. Without these, you’d be debugging string concatenation and re-learning JSON anatomy at the same time as learning HTTP verbs and authentication. Splitting the prep this way keeps each ICA’s cognitive load reasonable.
crumbs: 30  PCL · ICA 13 — APIs I: JSON, URLs, urltools

title: PCL · ICA 13 — APIs I: JSON, URLs, urltools
section: A note on what this enables
text:
A note on what this enables
The three building blocks here are exactly what HW07 P2 needs. The Census ACS endpoint is a URL with query parameters — param_set() is how you swap years across calls. The response is JSON arrays — fromJSON() plus unnest_wider() flattens them. And the API key is just one more parameter to set. The pattern, once seen, is portable: every JSON-over-HTTP API you’ll meet (OpenWeather, Spotify, GitHub, Twitter, NYT) follows this shape. ICA 13 gives you the vocabulary; ICA 14 gives you the verb.
crumbs: 30  PCL · ICA 13 — APIs I: JSON, URLs, urltools

title: PCL · ICA 14 — APIs II: jsonlite & Real-World Endpoints
text:
Pre-class learning map
ICA 14 picks up where ICA 13 stopped: ICA 13 explained the infrastructure (JSON shape, URL anatomy, programmatic URL manipulation); ICA 14 introduces the call (jsonlite doing the round trip from URL to R object) and the patterns you’ll encounter in the wild (auth via key, pagination via loop, nested responses requiring unnesting). The two vignettes split the cognitive load: the “Getting started” piece teaches the type-mapping machinery — how JSON’s three structures (array of primitives, array of objects, array of arrays) collapse into R’s three corresponding types (vector, data frame, matrix) via fromJSON()’s simplification arguments; the “Fetching JSON” piece is six real-world examples (GitHub, CitiBike, Ergast, ProPublica, NYT, Twitter), each illustrating a different API design choice — public vs key-required, single endpoint vs paginated, flat vs deeply nested. The mindmap organizes the prep around mechanism + patterns + the auth/pagination realities.
Code
mindmap
  root((ICA 14<br/>APIs II))
    The jsonlite round trip
      fromJSON
        URL · file · string
        returns R object
        type depends on input
      toJSON
        R object → JSON string
        for sending or storing
      Round-trip identity
        all.equal(mtcars, fromJSON(toJSON(mtcars)))
        TRUE
        proves bidirectional mapping
    Simplification · the type machinery
      Three JSON structures
        Array of primitives
          ["a", "b", "c"]
          → atomic vector
          arg · simplifyVector
        Array of objects

          → data frame
          arg · simplifyDataFrame
        Array of arrays
          [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
          → matrix
          arg · simplifyMatrix

        most APIs work zero-config
      Disable for raw structure
        simplifyVector = FALSE
        returns nested list
        useful for irregular data
      NULL handling
        missing keys → NA
        empty objects → row of NAs

      Public · no auth

          /users/USERNAME/repos
          /repos/OWNER/REPO/issues

          single endpoint
          all stations as array

          consumer key + secret
          POST to oauth2/token
          receive bearer token
          add as header in subsequent calls
        more complex · ICA 14 won't dwell here
    Pagination · the iteration shape
      Why
        APIs cap rows per response
          common · 100 · 500 · 1000
        more rows · multiple requests
      Common pagination styles
        page number
          ?page=1 · ?page=2 · ...
        offset
          ?offset=0&limit=100
        cursor / token
          response includes next-token
          next call passes it

        or list_rbind from purrr

      Inspect first
        str() · or names() of result

      Pluck deeper
        result %>% pluck("data", "items")
        navigate hierarchy

      What never to do

        commit key to git
        share repo with key inside
      What to do
        store in .Renviron

        gitignore .Renviron

      5 · flatten if nested
        unnest_wider · unnest_longer
      6 · paginate if needed
        loop · accumulate
      7 · bind into one tibble

title: PCL · ICA 14 — APIs II: jsonlite & Real-World Endpoints
section: Three concepts these vignettes quietly hinge on
text:
Three concepts these vignettes quietly hinge on
Simplification is what makes fromJSON() ergonomic — and it’s what occasionally bites. When you call fromJSON(url), it tries to coerce the response into the most R-friendly type: a JSON array of primitives becomes an atomic vector; an array of objects becomes a data frame; an array of arrays becomes a matrix. This works beautifully for the 80% of APIs that return regular structures, but it can surprise you when the response is almost regular — say, an array of objects where one record is missing a field, or a “string or array” union type that varies per element. The escape hatch is fromJSON(url, simplifyVector = FALSE), which returns a raw nested list, letting you handle the shape yourself with purrr/tidyr. Worth knowing that the default exists and that turning it off is the move when you hit weird structure.
Most APIs paginate, and paginating well is half the work. A single API call returning 1100 records (like the ProPublica example) is already split across 11 pages of 100 each — and most APIs are similar. The ProPublica vignette shows the canonical pattern: a for loop over page numbers, accumulating into a list, then rbind_pages (or list_rbind from purrr) at the end. The choice points: how does the API page (page number / offset / cursor)? How do you know when to stop (response count / empty page / explicit “has_more” flag)? Get those two right and the iteration becomes mechanical. Get them wrong and you either miss data or loop forever.
Auth credentials never live in your code, and .Renviron is the right place. Every example in the “Fetching JSON” vignette that requires a key shows it inline — that’s pedagogical, not safe. In real work, the API key goes in ~/.Renviron as NYT_API_KEY=abc123, accessed via Sys.getenv("NYT_API_KEY"), with .Renviron itself listed in .gitignore. The reason is asymmetric: writing api-key=hard_coded_string once costs nothing, but if you push that to GitHub, your key is now public and you have hours of revocation/rotation work ahead. Worth treating as a habit even when prototyping — the cost of doing it right is one extra line, the cost of doing it wrong is real.
crumbs: 31  PCL · ICA 14 — APIs II: jsonlite & Real-World Endpoints

title: PCL · ICA 14 — APIs II: jsonlite & Real-World Endpoints
section: A small reference for jsonlite’s type mapping
text:
A small reference for jsonlite’s type mapping
The four-cell table that the Getting Started vignette is essentially organized around:









JSON structure
Example
Simplifies to
Argument




Array of primitives
["a", "b", "c"]
atomic vector
simplifyVector = TRUE


Array of objects
[{"x": 1}, {"x": 2}]
data frame
simplifyDataFrame = TRUE


Array of arrays (same length)
[[1, 2], [3, 4]]
matrix
simplifyMatrix = TRUE


Anything irregular
[{"x": 1}, {"y": 2}] or mixed
nested list
use simplifyVector = FALSE



All three simplification arguments default to TRUE. Disabling them gives you raw structure to work with manually.
crumbs: 31  PCL · ICA 14 — APIs II: jsonlite & Real-World Endpoints

title: PCL · ICA 14 — APIs II: jsonlite & Real-World Endpoints
text:
Two patterns worth memorizing
The paginated-list pattern. Generalized from the ProPublica example:
base <- "https://api.example.com/v1/items"

pages <- list()
for (i in 0:max_page) {
  url <- paste0(base, "?page=", i, "&", auth)
  resp <- fromJSON(url, flatten = TRUE)
  pages[[i + 1]] <- resp$items
  message("Retrieved page ", i)
  if (length(resp$items) == 0) break    # stop on empty
}

all_items <- jsonlite::rbind_pages(pages)
The purrr version is shorter and composable but functionally equivalent:
0:max_page |>
  map(\(i) fromJSON(paste0(base, "?page=", i, "&", auth), flatten = TRUE)$items) |>
  list_rbind()
For unknown total page count, replace 0:max_page with a while loop that breaks when length(items) == 0.
The store-the-key-safely pattern. Once per machine, in ~/.Renviron:
CENSUS_API_KEY=abc123def456
NYT_API_KEY=xyz789
Restart R (so the file is re-read on session start), then in any analysis:
key <- Sys.getenv("CENSUS_API_KEY")
url <- urltools::param_set(base_url, "key", key)
data <- fromJSON(url)
The .Renviron file is per-user; on a Mac it lives in ~/.Renviron and isn’t tracked by any project’s .git. If you accidentally commit a key, rotate it immediately — assume it’s compromised the moment it hits a public repo.
crumbs: 31  PCL · ICA 14 — APIs II: jsonlite & Real-World Endpoints

title: PCL · ICA 14 — APIs II: jsonlite & Real-World Endpoints
section: Why this comes after URLs and JSON
text:
Why this comes after URLs and JSON
ICA 13 gave you URL structure and JSON anatomy in the abstract. ICA 14 puts both into motion: a URL is what you build to make a request, JSON is what comes back. The vignettes are deliberately concrete — six different APIs, each illustrating a different design choice — because no abstract description of “an API” can prepare you for the variety you’ll meet in the wild. By the time you finish ICA 14, the goal is that hitting a new API for the first time feels like applying a familiar template: build URL, fetch, inspect shape, flatten if needed, paginate if needed, bind. The httr2 package layered on top of all this (status codes, retries, OAuth flows, rate limiting) is incremental — once the JSON round trip is muscle memory, the request layer is just bookkeeping.
crumbs: 31  PCL · ICA 14 — APIs II: jsonlite & Real-World Endpoints

title: PCL · ICA 14 — APIs II: jsonlite & Real-World Endpoints
section: A note on what this enables
text:
A note on what this enables
HW07 P2 is a paginated API call: one Census ACS endpoint, called once per year from 2005 to 2022, results bound into one long tibble. That’s the paginated-list pattern with years instead of page numbers. The nested response (variables and geographies in separate JSON arrays) needs unnest_wider to flatten. The API key sits in .Renviron, accessed via Sys.getenv(), never visible in the qmd. Every piece of the prep maps to a piece of the homework — and the same template will work for every JSON API you’ll encounter outside this course (OpenWeather, GitHub, Spotify, FRED, World Bank, OECD).

title: PCL · ICA 15 — Web Scraping
text:
Pre-class learning map
ICA 15 is the mirror image of ICAs 13–14. APIs work when the website’s owner has built one and made it accessible; web scraping works when they haven’t, or when the data is only available through the rendered page itself. The mechanics are different — instead of a clean JSON response keyed by parameter, you get a hierarchical HTML document and have to address into it using CSS selectors, the same mini-language CSS uses to apply styles. The rvest vignette teaches three things in sequence: (1) HTML structure as a tree of nested tags with attributes; (2) CSS selectors as the addressing language for picking nodes out of that tree; (3) rvest’s extraction functions for getting text, attributes, or tabular data out of selected nodes. The mindmap below organizes the prep around these three layers plus the practical observation pattern (html_elements() outer / html_element() inner) that keeps your variables aligned.
Code
mindmap
  root((ICA 15<br/>Web Scraping))
    HTML as a tree
      Document structure
        html
          head · metadata · title
          body · visible content
      Element anatomy
        start tag · <tag>
        attributes · name=value
        contents · text or more elements
        end tag · </tag>
      Block tags
        h1 · h2 · h3 · headings
        p · paragraph
        div · generic block
        ol · ul · li · lists
        table · tr · th · td
      Inline tags
        a · link
          href attribute
        b · strong · bold
        i · em · italic
        span · generic inline
        img · self-closing
          src · alt · width · height
      Attributes that matter for scraping
        id · unique per page
        class · group of elements
        href · link target
        src · image source
        data-* · custom data
    CSS selectors · addressing the tree
      Four primary forms
        tag · p
          all p elements
        class · .important
          dot · matches class attribute
        id · #title
          hash · matches id attribute · unique
        tag.class · p.special
          tag with that class
      Combinators
        descendant · ul li
          li anywhere inside ul
        child · ul > li
          li direct child of ul
        adjacent · h2 + p
          first p right after h2
        sibling · h2 ~ p
          all p following h2
      Attribute selectors
        [data-modal=open]
        [href^=https]
        [class*=card]
      Pseudo-classes
        :first-child
        :nth-child(2n+1)
        :not(.hidden)
      Discovery tools
        SelectorGadget · point and click
        browser DevTools · inspect element
        Right-click · Inspect · copy selector
    rvest functions
      Reading
        read_html(url)
          fetches and parses
          returns xml_document
        minimal_html(string)
          for testing on literal HTML
      Selecting
        html_element(x, css)
          first match · always single
          NA if no match
        html_elements(x, css)
          all matches · vector of nodes
      Extracting
        html_text2()
          plain text · respects whitespace
          like browser display
        html_text()
          raw text · keeps junk whitespace
          rarely what you want
        html_attr(x, "name")
          attribute value as string
          may need parse_number etc.
        html_table()
          HTML table → data frame
          one call · done
    The observation pattern
      Wrong way · directly extract each variable
        names <- html_elements("b") · text2
        species <- html_elements("i") · text2
        weights <- html_elements(".weight") · text2
        problem · different lengths · misaligned
      Right way · select rows then variables
        rows <- html_elements(x, "li")
        names <- rows %>% html_element("b") %>% text2
        species <- rows %>% html_element("i") %>% text2
        weights <- rows %>% html_element(".weight") %>% text2
        property · same length · NA fills missing
      Why
        html_elements outside · selects observation rows
        html_element inside · returns one or NA per row
        guarantees alignment
    Iteration over pages
      Pagination patterns
        URL with ?page=N · increment
        URL with /page/N/ · path increment
        next link in HTML · follow
      Map shape
        urls <- str_glue base ?page= 1:N
        urls %>% map(read_html) %>% map(scrape_fn) %>% list_rbind
        ICA 11 territory
      Politeness
        rate limiting · Sys.sleep(1)
        identify yourself · User-Agent
        respect robots.txt
        cache locally · don't re-scrape
    Edge cases
      Dynamic content · JS rendering
        rvest reads server-side HTML only
        for JS-rendered pages · use chromote or RSelenium
      Login walls
        rvest_session · maintain cookies
        post form data
      Inconsistent structure
        some pages have field · some don't
        html_element returns NA · keeps alignment
        post-process to clean
      Encoding · whitespace
        html_text2 vs html_text
        str_squish · collapse whitespace
        str_trim · drop leading/trailing

title: PCL · ICA 15 — Web Scraping
section: Three concepts the vignette quietly hinges on
text:
Three concepts the vignette quietly hinges on
The observation pattern is what keeps your data frame aligned. This is the single most important pattern in rvest, and the one new scrapers most commonly miss. If you call html_elements("b"), html_elements("i"), html_elements(".weight") directly on the page, you’ll get vectors of different lengths whenever any observation is missing one of those fields — and there’s no safe way to align them back. The fix: first select the observation rows with html_elements() (e.g., the <li> for each Star Wars character), then extract each variable from each row with html_element() (singular) — which returns NA when no match is found, preserving alignment. The whole rvest API is designed around this two-level pattern. Internalize it and most scraping bugs disappear.
html_text2() mimics what the browser shows; html_text() returns raw character soup. HTML ignores whitespace in source code and uses element structure to render text. html_text() returns the source with all its newlines, tabs, and indentation preserved — ugly and rarely what you want. html_text2() reconstructs what the rendered page would look like: paragraphs separated by blank lines, no leading whitespace, sentences joined sensibly. The default reach should always be html_text2(). The only time html_text() is correct is when you specifically want to preserve raw whitespace (which is almost never).
Attributes are how the data not visible on screen gets carried. When scraping links from a list, the visible text might be the article title — but the URL you actually want is in the href attribute of the <a> tag. html_attr(node, "href") is how you get it. Same with images (src), data attributes (data-id, data-price), and form inputs (value). Always check what’s in the attribute as well as what’s in the text — half of the useful data on a typical page is in attributes, not in visible content.

title: PCL · ICA 15 — Web Scraping
section: A small reference for CSS selectors
text:
A small reference for CSS selectors
The selector forms ranked by frequency in real scraping work:








Selector
Matches
Example use




tag
All elements with that tag
p, a, td


.class
Elements with that class
.product-name, .price


#id
The unique element with that id
#main-content


tag.class
Tag with that class
a.external, div.card


parent child
Descendant (any depth)
ul li, .card a


parent > child
Direct child only
ul > li, nav > a


[attr] / [attr=value]
Attribute presence / equality
[href], [data-type=card]


[attr^=value]
Attribute starts with
[href^=https]


:first-child / :nth-child(n)
Positional
li:nth-child(odd)


selector1, selector2
Either of these (union)
h2, h3



For most scraping tasks, the first four are sufficient. The rest are tools you reach for when the page’s structure is hostile.

title: PCL · ICA 15 — Web Scraping
text:
Two patterns worth memorizing
The complete scrape-one-page function. Generalized from the rvest Star Wars example:
scrape_page <- function(url) {
  page <- read_html(url)
  rows <- page |> html_elements("li.character")  # observation selector

  tibble(
    name    = rows |> html_element("b")        |> html_text2(),
    species = rows |> html_element("i")        |> html_text2(),
    weight  = rows |> html_element(".weight")  |> html_text2() |> parse_number(),
    profile = rows |> html_element("a")        |> html_attr("href")
  )
}
The shape: read → select rows → tibble of per-variable extractions. Every variable is one column-line; every line is the same length because html_element (singular) returns NA on miss.
The paginated scrape. Same shape as the API pagination from ICA 14, but with read_html instead of fromJSON:
urls <- str_glue("https://example.com/items?page={1:21}")

all_items <- urls |>
  map(scrape_page) |>          # one tibble per page
  list_rbind()                 # bind into one
Add Sys.sleep(0.5) inside scrape_page if the site rate-limits or if politeness matters.

title: PCL · ICA 15 — Web Scraping
text:
Why this comes after APIs
ICAs 13–14 taught the structured case: a documented endpoint, a JSON contract, parameters that the maintainer has agreed to support. ICA 15 teaches the unstructured case: no contract, you read the HTML the page rendered for human eyes and address into it via styling hooks the developers happened to use. The skill ordering matters because once you’ve internalized JSON paths and URL parameters, CSS selectors feel familiar — they’re just a different addressing language for a different data shape. The iteration patterns (map over pages, bind with list_rbind) are identical to the API case; only the per-page extraction logic changes.

title: PCL · ICA 15 — Web Scraping
section: A note on what this enables
text:
A note on what this enables
HW08 P1 is exactly this pattern. cheese.com has 21 alphabetical pages, each listing 100 cheeses (the last has 46) — map(1:21, scrape_page) |> list_rbind() produced the 2046-row index. The detail-page version takes each cheese’s URL (extracted from <a href>), follows it, and pulls the milk / country / family / type / flavor fields by .summary-points class — same observation pattern, just one row per page instead of many. The same template will scrape essentially any catalog-style website: paginated index → detail pages → bind. The vocabulary rvest gives you is the toolkit; recognizing the catalog-detail pattern is what makes a scraping task tractable in 30 lines instead of 300.

title: PCL · ICA 15 — Web Scraping
section: A note on legality and politeness
text:
A note on legality and politeness
Scraping is legal in the US for publicly accessible pages (Van Buren v. United States, 2021), but ethical scraping is its own discipline. Three rules of thumb: (1) check robots.txt and respect Disallow rules; (2) rate-limit yourself even when not asked — a Sys.sleep(0.5) between requests costs nothing and keeps you off the maintainer’s radar; (3) cache locally and never re-scrape what you already have. Most scraping problems with hosting providers come not from scraping itself but from scraping aggressively — hammering a small site at full speed will get your IP banned and may break their server. The rvest vignette doesn’t dwell on this, but it’s part of the practice.

title: PCL · ICA 16 — Databases & SQL
text:
Pre-class learning map
R4DS Chapter 21 takes an unusual pedagogical route: it teaches SQL backwards through dbplyr. Rather than introduce SQL from scratch (SELECT col FROM table WHERE ...), it shows that the dplyr verbs you already know (select, filter, arrange, group_by, summarize, *_join) translate one-to-one into SQL clauses, and that show_query() will print the SQL dbplyr generates. The benefit is that SQL stops feeling like a foreign language: it’s the output of a dplyr pipeline, just written in a different syntax. The mindmap below organizes the chapter around that mapping — DBI for connection, dbplyr for translation, and SQL clauses arranged in the order they’re typed (which deliberately differs from the order they’re evaluated).
Code
mindmap
  root((ICA 16<br/>Databases & SQL))
    Why databases
      Stored on disk · arbitrarily large
      Indexed for fast lookup
      Multi-user · concurrent access
      Persist between R sessions
      Three DBMS shapes
        Client-server · Postgres · MySQL · Oracle
        Cloud · Snowflake · BigQuery · Redshift
        In-process · SQLite · duckdb
    The two-package architecture
      DBI · low-level interface
        dbConnect() · open connection
        dbListTables() · enumerate
        dbReadTable() · read whole table
        dbGetQuery() · run raw SQL
        dbWriteTable() · upload data
        dbDisconnect() · close
      Driver package · DBMS-specific
        RPostgres · for Postgres
        RMariaDB · for MySQL
        RSQLite · for SQLite
        duckdb · for duckdb
        odbc · generic fallback
      dbplyr · high-level translation
        tbl() · table reference
        dplyr verbs work normally
        show_query() · see SQL
        collect() · pull into R
    Lazy evaluation
      tbl(con, "name") · creates reference
      dplyr ops · build query · don't execute
      Query runs only at
        print to console · LIMIT
        collect() · pull all rows
        compute() · materialize on server
      Why it matters
        operate on huge tables without loading
        row count often unknown
        push computation to server
    The five SQL clauses
      SELECT · which columns
        select() · rename() · relocate() · mutate()
        AS for renaming · aliasing
        column expressions
          arithmetic
          if_else / case_when
          → CASE WHEN
      FROM · which table
        single table · or join · or subquery
      WHERE · which rows
        filter() before grouping
        comparison · = not ==
        boolean · AND · OR · NOT
        IN · ('a', 'b', 'c') for %in%
        IS NULL · IS NOT NULL
      GROUP BY · how to aggregate
        group_by() · summarize()
        aggregate fns · COUNT · SUM · AVG · MAX · MIN
      ORDER BY · how to sort
        arrange() · always at end
        DESC for descending
      Plus HAVING · for post-aggregation filter
        filter() after summarize() in dplyr
    SQL idiosyncrasies
      Order of writing
        SELECT · FROM · WHERE · GROUP BY · ORDER BY
      Order of evaluation
        FROM · WHERE · GROUP BY · SELECT · ORDER BY
        SELECT comes near end · WHERE can't use SELECT aliases
        creates need for subqueries
      Case · doesn't matter
        SELECT · select · SeLeCt · all valid
        convention · keywords UPPERCASE
      Strings vs identifiers
        '...' single quotes · string literal
        "..." double quotes · column or table name
        backticks · alternate identifier · MySQL
      NULL not NA
        infectious in arithmetic · comparison
        silently dropped in aggregation
        IS NULL not = NULL
        three-valued logic
    Joins in SQL
      Mutating joins
        LEFT JOIN · ON tableA.key = tableB.key
        INNER JOIN · matches both
        RIGHT JOIN · keep all of right
        FULL JOIN · keep all of both
      Filtering joins
        less natural in SQL
        WHERE EXISTS · WHERE IN
      Multi-join databases
        normalized · facts in one place
        primary keys · foreign keys
        dm package · navigate complex schemas
    Subqueries
      Why
        WHERE evaluated before SELECT
        can't filter on a column you just made
      Pattern
        outer query · uses inner query as FROM
        SELECT * FROM (inner_query) AS q1
      dbplyr generates them automatically
        when needed
    Window functions
      Why
        aggregate INSIDE mutate · not summarize
        no GROUP BY · use OVER instead
      Syntax
        AVG(x) OVER (PARTITION BY group)
        LEAD(x) OVER (ORDER BY time)
        LAG(x) OVER (PARTITION BY g ORDER BY t)
      Need ORDER BY for windowed
        SQL tables have no inherent order
    Function translation
      Easy
        mean → AVG
        sum → SUM
        n() → COUNT(*)
        + - * / · same
      Surgery required
        median → MEDIAN · syntax varies by DBMS
        if_else → CASE WHEN
        case_when → CASE WHEN ... WHEN ... END
        cut() → CASE WHEN with breakpoints
      String / date functions
        translated per DBMS
        vignette("translation-function", package = "dbplyr")

      dbplyr generates them automatically
        when needed
    Window functions
      Why
        aggregate INSIDE mutate · not summarize
        no GROUP BY · use OVER instead
      Syntax
        AVG(x) OVER (PARTITION BY group)
        LEAD(x) OVER (ORDER BY time)
        LAG(x) OVER (PARTITION BY g ORDER BY t)
      Need ORDER BY for windowed
        SQL tables have no inherent order
    Function translation
      Easy
        mean → AVG
        sum → SUM
        n() → COUNT(*)
        + - * / · same
      Surgery required
        median → MEDIAN · syntax varies by DBMS
        if_else → CASE WHEN
        case_when → CASE WHEN ... WHEN ... END
        cut() → CASE WHEN with breakpoints
      String / date functions
        translated per DBMS
        vignette("translation-function", package = "dbplyr")

title: PCL · ICA 16 — Databases & SQL
section: Three concepts the chapter quietly hinges on
text:
Three concepts the chapter quietly hinges on
SQL writes in one order, evaluates in another. The clauses are written SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE ... GROUP BY ... ORDER BY, but they’re evaluated in order FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → SELECT → ORDER BY. This mismatch has a real consequence: you can’t reference a column you create in SELECT from inside WHERE, because WHERE runs first. dplyr hides this — mutate(x2 = x + 1) |> filter(x2 > 5) looks straightforward — but dbplyr handles it by generating a subquery: an inner SELECT that creates the new column, wrapped by an outer SELECT that filters on it. Reading dbplyr’s show_query() output for such cases is the fastest way to internalize why subqueries exist.
Lazy evaluation is what makes dbplyr useful at scale. When you call tbl(con, "flights") |> filter(...) |> select(...), nothing actually runs. dbplyr just records the operations as a query plan. The plan executes only when you print it (with a LIMIT for the preview), call collect() (pull all rows into R), or call compute() (materialize as a temporary table on the server). The win: you can compose a 12-step pipeline against a 100-million-row table, and only when you collect() does the database actually run anything — and even then it’s the result that comes back, not the underlying table. Without laziness, every intermediate step would round-trip through R, defeating the whole point of using a database.
NULL is not NA — and it’s silently dropped in aggregations. R’s NA is “infectious”: NA + 1 is NA, mean(c(1, 2, NA)) is NA. SQL’s NULL follows three-valued logic in expressions (also infectious there) but is silently removed from aggregations: AVG(), COUNT(), SUM() all skip NULLs by default. dbplyr issues a one-time warning when you first hit this — “Missing values are always removed in SQL aggregation functions” — but it’s worth knowing in advance. Practical consequence: a mean(arr_delay) in dplyr-on-database returns the average of non-missing values automatically, whereas the same in dplyr-on-tibble returns NA unless you write na.rm = TRUE. If you need to know how many were missing, count them separately with sum(is.na(x)).

title: PCL · ICA 16 — Databases & SQL
PCL · ICA 16 — Databases & SQL
section: Why this comes after iteration and APIs
text:
Why this comes after iteration and APIs
ICA 16 sits at the end of the data-acquisition arc. ICAs 13-14 taught APIs (request data over HTTP), ICA 15 taught scraping (extract data from HTML), ICA 16 teaches databases (query data from a structured store). The progression is from external + ad-hoc to external + structured to internal + structured. By this point, you have three orthogonal ways to acquire data, each with a different cost-benefit profile: APIs are clean but rate-limited and may not exist; scraping is universal but fragile; databases are powerful but require infrastructure. Knowing all three means you can usually find a path to whatever data you need.

title: PCL · ICA 16 — Databases & SQL
section: A note on what this enables
text:
A note on what this enables
HW08 P2 is exactly this pattern, except the database is run by someone else (Stack Exchange Data Explorer). Each of the four queries — users, badges, posts, comments — is a SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE ... [GROUP BY ...] written by hand in SEDE’s web interface, downloaded as CSV, then loaded into R. The structure is identical to dbplyr’s output: pick columns, filter rows, group and aggregate where needed, and SEDE returns the result. The reason that pattern works at billion-row scale (the Stack Overflow dump is genuinely massive) is exactly what this chapter is about: the database engine does the heavy lifting before any data crosses the wire.

title: PCL · ICA 16 — Databases & SQL
text:
A small philosophical note
The chapter ends with: “it’s important to learn some SQL because it’s the most commonly used language for working with data and knowing some will make it easier for you to communicate with other data folks who don’t use R.” This is true and worth taking seriously. SQL was designed in the 1970s and has outlasted nearly every other data-tool fashion. The dialect drift between Postgres, MySQL, BigQuery, and Snowflake is real but the core 80% — SELECT / FROM / WHERE / GROUP BY / JOIN / window functions — is portable, durable, and present in every data infrastructure stack you’ll meet. dbplyr is a wonderful learning tool and a real production tool, but the underlying skill it teaches — thinking in queries — is what carries forward.

title: PCL · ICA 17 — Effective Visualization
text:
Pre-class learning map
ICA 17 marks the moment in STAT 212 when viz stops being about how to draw and starts being about how to communicate. ICA 03 introduced the grammar of ggplot2 — the syntax of layered plots. ICA 17 introduces the principles that decide whether the plot succeeds — whether someone reading it leaves with the insight you intended. Two readings split the cognitive load: Brent Dykes’s Data Storytelling makes the why argument — that data alone, however accurate, doesn’t change minds; only data + visuals + narrative does, because decisions are mediated by emotion as well as logic. Peng’s Customizing ggplot2 Plots gives the how — six concrete design guidelines (data density, labels, references, highlighting, small multiples, meaningful order) that translate Tufte/Few/Wainer’s writing into ggplot2 idioms you can apply directly. The mindmap organizes the prep around storytelling structure, the six design rules, and the ggplot2 mechanisms that implement each rule.
