title: PCL · ICA 17 — Effective Visualization
section: Three concepts these readings quietly hinge on
text:
Three concepts these readings quietly hinge on
A statistic informs; a story persuades. Dykes’s most cited point is that brain scans of statistics versus stories show different patterns of activation: statistics light up Broca’s and Wernicke’s (language processing only), while stories light up additional motor, sensory, and emotional regions. The implication for data work: a finding that is statistically valid but narratively flat will be remembered, recalled, and acted on far less than one that is identical in numbers but shaped into “a small farmer in rural Mali…” rather than “12.3% of children…” This isn’t manipulation — it’s recognition that human cognition is dual-process, and a chart that engages only the analytical channel leaves the persuasive channel on the table. Every plot you make has a narrative dimension whether you intend one or not; the question is whether you’ve chosen what it says.
Data-to-ink ratio is the meta-rule that contains most others. Tufte’s principle — every drop of ink should be carrying information — is the geometric core of Peng’s six guidelines. Lower data-to-ink: gridded backgrounds, 3D effects, redundant legends, gradient bars where a dot would do. Higher data-to-ink: minimal theme, single annotated reference line, faceted small multiples without per-panel scaffolding. Peng demonstrates this with the side-by-side Chicago mortality plots: same data, but the right-hand version (line + minimalist theme) communicates the heat-wave spike instantly while the left-hand version (filled area + colored background + gridlines) buries the signal under decoration. When you’re making a plot and uncertain what to add, the right move is usually subtraction.
Color is the aesthetic with the most communicative power and the most common misuse. Three failure modes recur in student work: (1) using a qualitative palette for ordered data — Set1 for income brackets — which destroys the ordering by mapping it to perceptually unordered hues; (2) using a sequential palette for unordered data — viridis for political party — which suggests an ordering that doesn’t exist; (3) confusing color with fill — color is the edge of an element, fill is the interior, and geom_point mostly responds to color while geom_bar mostly responds to fill, so a misapplied scale function silently does nothing. The recovery: pick palette family by data type (qualitative / sequential / diverging), prefer viridis for sequential (it’s perceptually uniform and colorblind-safe), and double-encode (color + shape, color + label) for accessibility.

title: PCL · ICA 17 — Effective Visualization
section: A small reference for the six guidelines
text:
A small reference for the six guidelines
Each guideline paired with the ggplot2 mechanisms that implement it:







Guideline
Primary mechanisms




1. High data density
theme_minimal / theme_few / theme_tufte, drop gridlines, prefer dots over bars where appropriate


2. Clear labels
labs(), xlab() / ylab(), coord_flip(), scales::comma, fct_recode


3. Useful references
geom_smooth, geom_hline / geom_vline, geom_ribbon for normal range, alpha + gray


4. Highlighting
geom_text / geom_label with subsetted data, aes(color = flag), named subsets


5. Small multiples
facet_wrap (one var), facet_grid (two vars), scales = "free_y", ordered factor levels


6. Meaningful order
forcats::fct_reorder, fct_infreq, arrange + factor(levels = unique(.)) pattern

title: PCL · ICA 17 — Effective Visualization
text:
Two patterns worth memorizing
The “highlight a subset” pattern. Generalized from Peng’s noteworthy-players example:
noteworthy <- data |>
  filter(value > threshold) |>
  mutate(label = paste(name, value, sep = ": "))

ggplot(data, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "gray60") +              # background
  geom_point(data = noteworthy, color = "firebrick",        # highlight
             size = 2.5) +
  geom_text(data = noteworthy, aes(label = label),          # label
            vjust = "inward", hjust = "inward",
            color = "firebrick", size = 3) +
  theme_few()
The structural move: two layers, two datasets. The full data forms a quiet background; the subset of interest gets stronger color, larger size, and a text label. The vjust = "inward" and hjust = "inward" keep labels inside the plot bounds without manual positioning.
The “order by another variable” pattern. Useful any time the categorical axis is alphabetically meaningless:
data |>
  group_by(category) |>
  summarize(metric = mean(value)) |>
  arrange(metric) |>
  mutate(category = factor(category, levels = unique(category))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = metric, y = category)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_few()
The arrange |> mutate(factor(levels = unique(.))) idiom forces ggplot’s natural axis order to follow the metric. forcats::fct_reorder(category, metric) does the same thing in one line and is the more idiomatic choice when the data is already in the form you need.

title: PCL · ICA 17 — Effective Visualization
section: The lift from grammar to design
text:
The lift from grammar to design
ICA 03 (R4DS Ch1) was about being able to make any plot at all — knowing which geom_ to call, where aes() goes, how facets work. ICA 17 is about making plots that communicate. The difference is the difference between writing a grammatical sentence and writing a sentence that lands. The same data, the same geom_, the same facet — applied with attention to data-to-ink, references, and ordering — turns a chart that’s technically correct into one that’s actually read. Peng’s chapter is essentially a translation manual between the two layers: each design rule has a small set of ggplot2 mechanisms that implement it, so the gap between “I know what I want” and “I know what to type” is small.

title: PCL · ICA 17 — Effective Visualization
section: Why this comes after wrangling, iteration, and acquisition
text:
Why this comes after wrangling, iteration, and acquisition
ICA 17 is where the course’s threads converge. By this point you can wrangle (ICAs 06-08), iterate (ICAs 11-12), acquire from APIs / scrape / SQL (ICAs 13-16). The natural next question is: given that you can produce any chart you want from any source, how do you produce one that’s worth showing? The answer is design discipline — not artistry, but a small set of rules consistently applied. Tufte / Few / Wainer have written entire books on this; ICA 17’s prep distills the actionable subset.

title: PCL · ICA 17 — Effective Visualization
section: A note on the larger argument
text:
A note on the larger argument
Dykes’s article opens with Hal Varian’s 2009 prediction — “the ability to take data, to be able to understand it, to process it, to extract value from it, to visualize it, to communicate it, that’s going to be a hugely important skill in the next decades.” Reading this 17 years later, the prediction is uncontroversial: every data-related job posting now lists communication. What’s still contested is how to teach it. ICA 17 takes a position: communication isn’t a soft skill bolted onto technical work, it’s a craft with rules — data-to-ink ratio, reference framing, ordered comparison, visual hierarchy, narrative arc — that can be learned the same way function design or SQL queries can. The Forbes article is the philosophy; Peng’s chapter is the syllabus.

title: PCL · ICA 18 — Interactive Visualization
text:
Pre-class learning map
ICA 18’s two readings are deliberately in tension with each other. Cole Nussbaumer Knaflic’s Storytelling with Data podcast argues for the storytelling tradition: a curated narrative, a focused chart, annotations that point the reader to the takeaway. Dominikus Baur’s Medium post The death of interactive infographics? presents a sobering empirical fact from the New York Times: only 10–15% of readers click on essential buttons, and 85% never see anything hidden behind interaction. The pair forces the question that ICA 18 is really about: when does interactivity earn its complexity, and when is a static, well-annotated chart actually better? The answer hinges on three audience variables — time, goals, and care — that determine whether interaction is a feature or a barrier. The mindmap organizes the prep around the storytelling argument, the empirical critique, and the three-axis decision framework that reconciles them.
Code
mindmap
  root((ICA 18<br/>Interactive Viz))
    The storytelling tradition · Knaflic
      Story arc
        setup · context the reader needs
        conflict · what changed · why care
        resolution · the takeaway
      Focus
        one chart · one message
        avoid kitchen-sink dashboards
        every element earns its place
      Annotation
        text on the chart · not just legends
        arrows to anomalies
        title that states the conclusion
      Color as attention
        gray for context
        bold color for the point
      Why it works
        reader has limited attention
        you've already done the work
          you know what matters
          you should say so
    The empirical critique · Baur
      The NYT numbers
        Gregor Aisch · 10-15% click rates
        Archie Tse · "assume nobody sees it"
        85% miss what is behind interaction
      Costs of interactive
        development time · weeks for one piece
        cross-device support
          mouse · trackpad · touch
          desktop · tablet · phone
        error surface · larger and more fragile
        maintenance · ongoing
      What this means
        most interactivity is invisible
        it is a tax on attention
        many readers are mid-commute
    Three audience variables
      TIME
        do they have 30 seconds or 15 minutes
        commute reader · quick scan
          static + annotation wins
        museum or installation visitor · slow
          interaction adds value
        researcher with a question · open-ended
          interaction essential
      GOALS
        learn about a topic

        find a specific answer
          interaction wins · "where can I get cell signal"
        explore freely
          interaction wins · with onboarding
        decide between options
          comparison view · static or interactive
      CARE
        why should this reader care
        personalization hooks
          location-based default
          ask one entry question
          show "your" data first
        narcissism is a feature
          OECD wellbeing started in user's region
          NYT street name visualization
        if they don't care · they won't click
    When interaction earns its keep
      Personal answers from large data
        too much to show statically
        question is "what about me"
        reader has specific data they want
      Exploration of complex space
        no single message
        meaningful patterns vary by viewer
        shaped exploration with sensible defaults
      Installations and dashboards
        captive audience
        time available
        no rush to next article
      Visualization as interface
        access to a tool · not a story
        Subspotting for cell signal
        bus or transit app
    When interaction is overhead
      Single message stories
        you know the takeaway
        annotated static chart is faster
      News graphics on social
        reader arrived from feed · seconds of patience
        will scroll past anything that loads
      Mobile-first contexts
        small screen · imprecise touch
        tooltips and hover states fail
      Just because you can
        novelty is not value
        cleverness without payoff alienates
    Onboarding · the click-rate fix
      Why it matters
        Baur · "blank stare" if no intro
        readers don't know how to start
      Patterns that work
        explicit "How to read this" panel
          Accurat for La Lettura
        guided tour · stepped exploration
          Nicky Case's Parable of the Polygons
        sensible defaults · then enable change
          OECD started at user's region
        text + interactivity interspersed
          read · do · read · do
      What fails
        plain canvas · no instructions
        hover-only without affordance
        too many controls visible at once
    The R interactive toolkit
      plotly · ggplotly() drop-in
        lift static ggplot to hoverable
        zoom · pan · click · brush
        retains ggplot grammar
      leaflet
        interactive maps
        tile layers · markers · popups
        WGS84 input only
      DT
        sortable · filterable tables
        for data inspection more than narrative
      htmlwidgets · the wrapper layer
        D3 · Highcharts · ECharts
        when ggplot can't do it
      shiny
        full reactive UI
        too heavy for static portfolio
        right tool when reactivity is the point
      Quarto extras
        crosstalk · linked filtering
        OJS chunks · Observable in Quarto
        embed-resources for portable HTML
    A small framework
      Three questions before adding interaction
        Q1 · How long will reader spend?
          short → static
          long → interactive viable
        Q2 · Is there a single takeaway?
          yes → static + annotation
          no → interactive may help
        Q3 · Will the reader know what to do?
          yes → proceed
          no → onboarding required
      If all three favor interaction · build it
      If any one says no · default to static

        gray for context
        bold color for the point
      Why it works
        reader has limited attention
        you've already done the work
          you know what matters
          you should say so
    The empirical critique · Baur
      The NYT numbers
        Gregor Aisch · 10-15% click rates
        Archie Tse · "assume nobody sees it"
        85% miss what is behind interaction
      Costs of interactive
        development time · weeks for one piece
        cross-device support
          mouse · trackpad · touch
          desktop · tablet · phone
        error surface · larger and more fragile
        maintenance · ongoing
      What this means
        most interactivity is invisible
        it is a tax on attention
        many readers are mid-commute
    Three audience variables
      TIME
        do they have 30 seconds or 15 minutes
        commute reader · quick scan
          static + annotation wins
        museum or installation visitor · slow
          interaction adds value
        researcher with a question · open-ended
          interaction essential
      GOALS
        learn about a topic
          guided narrative wins
        find a specific answer
          interaction wins · "where can I get cell signal"
        explore freely
          interaction wins · with onboarding
        decide between options
          comparison view · static or interactive
      CARE
        why should this reader care
        personalization hooks
          location-based default
          ask one entry question
          show "your" data first
        narcissism is a feature
          OECD wellbeing started in user's region
          NYT street name visualization
        if they don't care · they won't click
    When interaction earns its keep
      Personal answers from large data
        too much to show statically
        question is "what about me"
        reader has specific data they want
      Exploration of complex space
        no single message
        meaningful patterns vary by viewer
        shaped exploration with sensible defaults
      Installations and dashboards
        captive audience
        time available
        no rush to next article
      Visualization as interface
        access to a tool · not a story
        Subspotting for cell signal
        bus or transit app
    When interaction is overhead
      Single message stories
        you know the takeaway
        annotated static chart is faster
      News graphics on social
        reader arrived from feed · seconds of patience
        will scroll past anything that loads
      Mobile-first contexts
        small screen · imprecise touch
        tooltips and hover states fail
      Just because you can
        novelty is not value
        cleverness without payoff alienates
    Onboarding · the click-rate fix
      Why it matters
        Baur · "blank stare" if no intro
        readers don't know how to start
      Patterns that work
        explicit "How to read this" panel
          Accurat for La Lettura
        guided tour · stepped exploration
          Nicky Case's Parable of the Polygons
        sensible defaults · then enable change
          OECD started at user's region
        text + interactivity interspersed
          read · do · read · do
      What fails
        plain canvas · no instructions
        hover-only without affordance
        too many controls visible at once
    The R interactive toolkit
      plotly · ggplotly() drop-in
        lift static ggplot to hoverable
        zoom · pan · click · brush
        retains ggplot grammar
      leaflet
        interactive maps
        tile layers · markers · popups
        WGS84 input only
      DT
        sortable · filterable tables
        for data inspection more than narrative
      htmlwidgets · the wrapper layer
        D3 · Highcharts · ECharts
        when ggplot can't do it
      shiny
        full reactive UI
        too heavy for static portfolio
        right tool when reactivity is the point
      Quarto extras
        crosstalk · linked filtering
        OJS chunks · Observable in Quarto
        embed-resources for portable HTML
    A small framework
      Three questions before adding interaction
        Q1 · How long will reader spend?
          short → static
          long → interactive viable
        Q2 · Is there a single takeaway?
          yes → static + annotation
          no → interactive may help
        Q3 · Will the reader know what to do?
          yes → proceed
          no → onboarding required
      If all three favor interaction · build it
      If any one says no · default to static

title: PCL · ICA 18 — Interactive Visualization
section: Three concepts these readings quietly hinge on
text:
Three concepts these readings quietly hinge on
The 85% statistic should change how you design. Aisch’s number — that only 10–15% of NYT readers click on interactive elements — is not just sobering; it should reshape decision priors. A reasonable mental model: whatever you put behind a click, 85% of your audience will never see. This means the default state of an interactive piece must already convey the core message; interaction is only ever for more, not for the point. Baur’s quote of Tse — “If you make a tooltip or rollover, assume no one will ever see it” — is the operationalization. Tooltips can hold supplementary detail (data values, sources, footnotes), but never the headline. The headline goes in the title, the annotation, the static layer.
Time + Goals + Care is the three-axis filter for interactivity. Baur’s framework names the variables that actually determine whether interaction works. Time: a commute reader has 30 seconds and will not click; a museum visitor at an installation has 30 minutes and may. Goals: a reader learning about a topic wants a guided narrative; a reader looking for a specific answer (where on my line can I get cell signal?) needs a tool. Care: a reader’s interest must be activated before they engage — through personalization (defaulting to their location), through narrative hook (why this matters to you), or through onboarding (showing them how to read it). When all three favor interaction, build it; when any one fails, default to static. This collapses years of data-vis design debate into a tractable triage.
Storytelling and exploration are different jobs, not opposing philosophies. Knaflic and Baur appear to disagree, but they’re optimizing for different contexts. Knaflic’s audience: a manager presenting a quarterly result to executives, a journalist with a single point to communicate, an analyst justifying a recommendation. The job is persuasion — one message, well-told. Baur’s audience: a researcher exploring an unfamiliar dataset, a museum-goer at an installation, a transit rider answering “where can I get signal.” The job is discovery — many possible messages, the reader picks. The bug is treating these as the same job. A persuasive chart with too many interactions dilutes its message; an exploratory tool with a forced narrative removes the agency that justified building it. Match the form to the job.

title: PCL · ICA 18 — Interactive Visualization
section: A small reference for the decision
text:
A small reference for the decision
Before adding any interactivity, three questions in order:








Question
If “static”
If “interactive”




How long will the reader spend?
Brief / scrolling
Sustained / focused


Is there one core takeaway?
Yes — annotate it
No — multiple meaningful views


Will the reader know what to do?
Reading is the action
Needs onboarding or sensible defaults


Is the goal persuasion?
Yes — story arc
Goal is exploration or tool-use


Will the chart appear in feed / mobile?
Yes — assume scroll
No — captive context



If two or more rows lean static, default to static + annotation. If two or more lean interactive, build interaction with onboarding.

title: PCL · ICA 18 — Interactive Visualization
text:
Two patterns worth memorizing
The “default state must carry the message” pattern. Even when building an interactive, design as if interaction will not occur:
# Bad: the headline is hidden behind a tooltip
plot_ly(data, ...) |>
  add_markers() |>
  add_text(text = "")          # nothing visible by default

# Good: headline annotated on the static layer; interaction adds detail
ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = important_flag)) +
  geom_text(data = highlights,
            aes(label = label),
            color = "firebrick") +                 # visible without click
  labs(
    title = "Headline conclusion in plain text",
    subtitle = "Source data and detail available on hover"
  ) |>
  plotly::ggplotly()                                # interactivity layered on
The static version of an interactive should still tell the story. Interaction adds depth, never the substance.
The personalize-first pattern. When interactivity is justified, hook the reader by starting from their data:
# OECD pattern: detect or ask, default to user's region
user_region <- detect_or_default_region()  # via geolocation or dropdown

oecd_data |>
  filter(region == user_region) |>
  plot_initial_view()                       # this is what they see first

# Then make exploration available
ui_for_changing_region(default = user_region)
The reader arrives in a chart that’s already about them; agency to change the view is offered, not required. This converts the “why should I care” question before the reader can ask it.

title: PCL · ICA 18 — Interactive Visualization
section: Why this comes after effective viz
text:
Why this comes after effective viz
ICA 17 taught the design discipline of static charts — data-to-ink, references, highlighting, ordering, narrative arc. ICA 18 takes those principles and asks: which of them change when you add interaction? Some carry over directly (high data density, meaningful order), some get inverted (tooltips can violate “highlight one thing” because the user picks), and some become harder (small multiples in interactive context need careful onboarding). The progression reflects how the tools are actually built: plotly::ggplotly() adds interaction on top of a well-designed ggplot, so the static design discipline is the foundation, not the alternative.

title: PCL · ICA 18 — Interactive Visualization
section: A note on what this enables
text:
A note on what this enables
The decision framework here directly shapes how I’ll build the final TidyTuesday entries and any portfolio dashboards. For most TidyTuesday posts — single dataset, single weekly question, scrolling reader — static + annotation wins; the time investment in interactivity isn’t repaid by the audience. For an exploratory piece (the CNPJ Lorenz analysis is a candidate) the interactive version might be justified — but only if the static version still carries the headline finding. The quiet rule both readings converge on: interaction is for the second question, not the first. Make sure the first question has a static answer.

title: PCL · ICA 18 — Interactive Visualization
text:
Closing thought
The Baur essay’s title — The death of interactive infographics? — is intentionally provocative. The honest answer is the one he gives: it depends. The maturation of the field means we’re past the period when interaction was a virtue in itself, and into a period when it’s a tool to be deployed only when its complexity is repaid by audience benefit. Knaflic’s storytelling tradition isn’t an alternative to interaction; it’s the discipline that lets interaction serve a purpose when you do choose to add it. ICA 18’s job is to internalize the question — what does interaction earn here? — and only build it when there’s an answer.

title: PCL · ICA 19 — Quality Control: Critiquing ‘Counting Happiness’
text:
Pre-class learning task
ICA 19’s prep is structurally different from every prior PCL: instead of readings to synthesize, it’s a critique task. Nathan Yau’s Counting Happiness and Where It Comes From (FlowingData, July 2021) is a published data story analyzing the HappyDB corpus — 100,000 happy moments from 10,000 Mechanical Turk participants, parsed into subject-verb-object triples. The job is to read it as a designer, not as a consumer: identify the story, identify what works, identify what could be stronger. The mindmap below organizes the piece itself plus the three critique axes the assignment asks for, ending with what this exercise teaches about quality control as the closing skill of STAT 212.
Code

          V · watched
          O · movie

        moments with other subjects
          relational happiness
      Visual form
        flowing Sankey-like diagrams
        verbs flowing into objects
        weighted by frequency
      Three sub-views
        I-centered top 5
        Others-centered top 5
        Pets-centered breakdown
    What is the data story
      Headline
        happiness comes in many forms · many places
        but with structure visible in language
      Two-channel claim
        self-driven · daily mundane joys
          dinner · movie · time
        other-driven · relational moments
          team won · husband surprised
      Implied insight
        most relational happiness

      Restraint in interpretation

    What could be improved

      No magnitudes shown

crumbs: 36  PCL · ICA 19 — Quality Control: Critiquing 'Counting Happiness'

title: PCL · ICA 19 — Quality Control: Critiquing ‘Counting Happiness’
text:
What is the data story
The piece argues — with structure rather than overt claim — that happiness in daily life splits into two grammatical channels: things people do for themselves (I watched a movie, I had dinner) and things others do that arrive as relational gifts (husband surprised me, son gave hug). The Sankey-style flow diagrams are not the argument; they’re the evidence display. The argument is that when you parse 100,000 self-reports of happy moments by subject-verb-object, the verbs and objects in the relational column trend small and ordinary — meals, gifts, hugs — rather than grand gestures. The closing line — “sometimes you have to refocus your attention to see the moments more clearly” — is the persuasive payload: happiness is structurally available, you just need to see it.
The pets sub-view operates as a third channel that confirms the same logic at finer resolution — when the relational counterpart is a pet, the verbs become specific and physical (snuggled, jumped, curled). Same story, sharper instance.
crumbs: 36  PCL · ICA 19 — Quality Control: Critiquing 'Counting Happiness'

title: PCL · ICA 19 — Quality Control: Critiquing ‘Counting Happiness’
text:
What is effective
Method transparency at the right granularity. Yau explains the SVO parsing in two sentences with one concrete example before showing any results. The reader can immediately replicate the mental move — “I watched a movie” → S=I, V=watched, O=movie — and trust what follows. Many published data stories skip this; the absence shows up later as confusion about what the chart is actually counting.
Honest sample disclosure. The Mechanical Turk caveat — that participants skew 20–40 years old, which inflates spouse and child mentions and depresses workplace mentions — is not buried in a footnote. It explicitly tells the reader what would change with a different sample. This converts a methodological weakness into a strength: the reader leaves trusting the analyst, not the dataset.
Form fits content. SVO triples have a natural left-to-right flow, and the Sankey-like rendering makes that flow legible. The gray-and-accent palette directs attention without overpowering. The top-5 lists in plain text before the diagrams give readers an anchor — they know what they’re about to see in graphical form.
Restraint in interpretation. Yau narrates the patterns (“Generally speaking, it’s about someone making time for another”) without claiming causal mechanisms. Given that this is observational self-report data, this restraint is appropriate; many less careful data stories would have over-claimed.
crumbs: 36  PCL · ICA 19 — Quality Control: Critiquing 'Counting Happiness'

title: PCL · ICA 19 — Quality Control: Critiquing ‘Counting Happiness’
text:
What could be improved
Magnitude information is missing from the rankings. The top-5 lists give ordinal information (rank 1, rank 2, …) but no counts or proportions. Without that, the reader can’t tell whether dinner (rank 1) is 5× or 1.05× more frequent than lunch (rank 5). For a story about what makes people happy, the size of the gap between #1 and #5 is itself part of the answer. A small inline bar with each rank — even just visual length — would carry magnitude without disrupting the layout.
The bias caveat appears after the main argument. The note that the MTurk sample skews 20–40 years old, increasing spouse/child mentions and depressing workplace ones, is a real interpretive constraint — but it’s placed below the analysis, after the reader has already formed conclusions. One sentence at the top — “the sample skews 20–40, which shapes what relational categories appear” — would frame the reading without undercutting it. Knaflic’s storytelling principle would put the constraint in the setup, not the resolution.
Long-tail data is invisible. Top-5 lists by definition show 5 of however-many distinct triples exist. With 100k moments and SVO parsing, the long tail almost certainly contains interesting structure — rare but coherent clusters of moments that the top-5 framing erases. A static piece is constrained here; ICA 18’s lessons about when interactivity earns its keep apply: this is exactly the case where a search/filter affordance would add real value, because the reader’s question (“does my kind of happiness appear?”) isn’t answered by aggregate ranks.
No comparative null. What does the same SVO parsing look like on a sadness corpus, or a frustration corpus? Without that comparison, it’s hard to tell what’s specifically about happiness versus what’s about how humans attribute attention to events generally. The piece is strong on its own terms; a comparative lens would have made it stronger.
Time is absent. When were these moments collected? Was it pre- or post-pandemic? Any seasonality? Aggregate-over-time analysis can hide drift, and for an emotion corpus that’s a substantive concern.
crumbs: 36  PCL · ICA 19 — Quality Control: Critiquing 'Counting Happiness'

title: PCL · ICA 19 — Quality Control: Critiquing ‘Counting Happiness’
section: Three takeaways for my own work
text:
Three takeaways for my own work
Put method transparency before findings, not after. Yau does this and it’s the cleanest move in the piece. One paragraph naming what was done and how — concretely, with an example — earns the rest of the reader’s attention.
Frame sample bias in the opening, not the notes. Bias caveats placed after the analysis read as defensive footnotes; placed before, they read as honest framing. The same sentence does very different work depending on where it lives.
Show magnitude, not just rank. Ordinal lists are easy and accessible, but they hide gradient. Even a minimal visual encoding of count or proportion — a tiny inline bar — converts a list into a chart that respects the reader’s intelligence.
crumbs: 36  PCL · ICA 19 — Quality Control: Critiquing 'Counting Happiness'

title: PCL · ICA 19 — Quality Control: Critiquing ‘Counting Happiness’
section: Why quality control is the closing skill
text:
Why quality control is the closing skill
ICA 19 is the last ICA of STAT 212 deliberately. By this point you can wrangle, iterate, acquire, visualize statically, and add interaction. The remaining skill — the one that’s hardest to teach because it requires slow reading of finished work — is critique. The course’s earlier ICAs taught what should be in a chart; ICA 19 trains the muscle of asking what shouldn’t be there, what’s missing, what could be clearer. This is the skill that distinguishes a data scientist who produces work from one whose work others trust.
Critique is also reciprocal: every good chart you’ll ever make benefits from someone else applying this same lens before it goes public. ICA 19 is rehearsal for that role. The Yau piece was chosen well — it’s published, it’s by a respected practitioner, and it’s good enough that the criticisms have to be substantive rather than corrective. Critiquing weak work teaches little; critiquing strong work teaches what the next level of strength looks like.
crumbs: 36  PCL · ICA 19 — Quality Control: Critiquing 'Counting Happiness'

title: PCL · ICA 19 — Quality Control: Critiquing ‘Counting Happiness’
text:
Closing thought
The piece’s own closing line — “sometimes you have to refocus your attention to see the moments more clearly” — applies recursively to data design. The chart that looks finished often has structural choices the maker has stopped seeing. Quality control is the practice of refocusing — looking at the chart as if you’d never seen it, asking whether the magnitudes are visible, whether the framing is honest, whether the reader can answer their own questions from what’s there. STAT 212 has spent a semester teaching how to make charts; ICA 19 closes by teaching how to read them. The two are the same skill seen from opposite sides.
crumbs: 36  PCL · ICA 19 — Quality Control: Critiquing 'Counting Happiness'

Setup
Codelibrary(tidyverse)

title: 01 — Introduction: Sources, Levels of Understanding, and Wrangling Review
text:
🧩 Learning Goals
By the end of this lesson I should be able to:

Use and evaluate different sources of information to find potential answers
Distinguish between high- and low-level understanding
Decide which source of information is suitable for each level of understanding
Decide which level of understanding is suitable for this class
Review the data-wrangling verbs from COMP/STAT 112
crumbs: 37  01 — Introduction: Sources, Levels of Understanding, and Wrangling Review

title: 01 — Introduction: Sources, Levels of Understanding, and Wrangling Review
section: Sources of Information — What is a Data Frame in R?
text:
Sources of Information — What is a Data Frame in R?
The exercise is to ask the same question of seven different sources and watch how the answers differ. What follows is a compressed synthesis of what each source actually says, not a retyping of its text.
(a) What each source says
Search engines (Google). The top results are tutorial sites (W3Schools, GeeksforGeeks, DataCamp). They converge on the framing “a data frame is a table-like structure with rows and columns, where each column can have a different type.” Optimized for findability; thin on mechanism.
LLMs (Gemini). Returns the tutorial-site framing with an extra sentence or two of context — “similar to a spreadsheet or SQL table” — and, if prompted, offers a data.frame() example. Fluent and fast; confidently silent about what’s underneath the abstraction.
Intro textbooks (R4DS). Wickham & Grolemund introduce the tibble first (tbl_df) rather than data.frame, and frame it as “the modern reimagining of the data frame.” The pedagogical move is to let the learner use tidy data without worrying about legacy base-R footguns (stringsAsFactors, partial matching, drop = TRUE in [).
R Manuals (official). An Introduction to R is terser and more precise: a data frame is a list of variables of the same number of rows with unique row names, given class "data.frame". This definition is the one that tells you what the object is, not what it looks like.
Advanced textbooks (Advanced R). Wickham is explicit: a data frame is an S3 object built on top of a named list of equal-length vectors, with a row.names attribute and class = "data.frame". Everything else — the rectangular printout, [ behavior, $ access — is convention layered on top of the list substrate. This is the view that generalizes.
data.frame() documentation. The function signature tells you what the constructor actually does: recycles shorter columns, mangles or checks names, and (since R 4.0) no longer stringifies-to-factor by default. Read this when something surprising happens at construction time.
tibble class documentation. Specifies exactly how tbl_df differs from data.frame: [ always returns a tibble (never drops to a vector), no partial name matching, no row names by default, lazier coercion, nicer printing. Read this when something surprising happens downstream of a mutate() or a bracket.
(b) How the sources differ
Two axes separate them cleanly:


High-level (what it looks like) vs. low-level (what it is). Search engines, LLMs, and intro textbooks stay on the first side; manuals, Advanced R, and the class documentation cross to the second.

Happy-path vs. edge-case. Tutorials describe the happy path; documentation describes the behavior under unusual inputs — which is where bugs actually live.

The pattern that matters: a source that looks authoritative (a confident LLM answer, a polished tutorial site) can be shallower than a source that looks dry (the R manual page). Authority of tone is not authority of depth.
Our definition of a data frame in R






NoteOur definition



A data frame in R is an S3 object built on a named list of equal-length vectors, with a row.names attribute supplying an implicit row index and class = "data.frame" supplying method dispatch. The rectangular “table with rows and columns” is the user-facing abstraction; the list of vectors is the implementation. A tibble (tbl_df) is a refined subclass that changes defaults (no partial name matching, non-dropping [, no row names, no silent string-to-factor coercion) while preserving the list-of-vectors substrate.


A small verification pass makes this concrete:

Codedf <- data.frame(x = 1:3, y = c("a", "b", "c"))

class(df)          # "data.frame"

[1] "data.frame"

Codetypeof(df)         # "list" — this is the move

[1] "list"

Codeis.list(df)        # TRUE

[1] TRUE

Codelength(df)         # 3 columns — same as length of the underlying list

[1] 2

Codeattributes(df)     # names, class, row.names

$names
[1] "x" "y"

$class
[1] "data.frame"

$row.names
[1] 1 2 3


typeof(df) == "list" is the sentence that turns “data frame” from a black box into a navigable object. Once you know the substrate is a list, every list operation — [[, $, lapply(), purrr::map() — is available, and the rectangular shape is just a display convention the class enforces on top.

Code# A tibble behaves like a data frame but tightens the defaults
tb <- tibble(x = 1:3, y = c("a", "b", "c"))

class(tb)          # "tbl_df" "tbl" "data.frame" — inheritance chain

[1] "tbl_df"     "tbl"        "data.frame"

Codeidentical(as.list(tb), as.list(df))   # same substrate, different defaults

[1] TRUE
crumbs: 37  01 — Introduction: Sources, Levels of Understanding, and Wrangling Review

title: 01 — Introduction: Sources, Levels of Understanding, and Wrangling Review
text:
Levels of Understanding
Two levels:


High-level (intuitive): enough to implement common tasks. “A data frame is a table with columns of possibly different types.”

Low-level (foundational): enough to tackle new problems and invent new solutions. “A data frame is an S3 object over a named list of equal-length vectors; [ dispatches through the data.frame method; tibbles are a subclass with tightened defaults.”

The class standard is low-level. The practical workflow is to start high-level (LLM, tutorial, R4DS) to get oriented, then dig into documentation and Advanced R to actually understand why the thing behaves the way it does. The high-level pass tells you which questions to ask; the low-level pass gives answers that generalize.
A compact test for whether I have reached the low-level understanding: can I predict what an unfamiliar operation will do to the object before I run it, by reasoning about its structure? If yes, low-level. If I have to run it to see, still high-level.
crumbs: 37  01 — Introduction: Sources, Levels of Understanding, and Wrangling Review

title: 01 — Introduction: Sources, Levels of Understanding, and Wrangling Review
section: Data Wrangling Verbs — Review from COMP/STAT 112
text:
Data Wrangling Verbs — Review from COMP/STAT 112
Organized by what they do to the shape of the data, not in alphabetical order — grouping by effect is how I actually recall them under pressure.
Column operations


mutate() — creates or modifies columns.

select() — keeps a subset of columns.
Row operations


filter() — keeps a subset of rows by condition.

arrange() — sorts rows.
Group-and-collapse


group_by() — internally groups rows by one or more columns (no visible change until a downstream verb consumes the grouping).

summarize() — collapses each group to a single row using n(), sum(), mean(), min(), max(), median(), sd(), etc.

count() — shortcut for group_by(...) |> summarize(n = n()).
Joins — combining two tables
Mutating joins (can add columns):


left_join() — keep all rows in the left table.

full_join() — keep all rows in both.

inner_join() — keep only rows with matches in both.

Filtering joins (never add columns, only filter the left table):


semi_join() — keep left-table rows that have a match on the right.

anti_join() — keep left-table rows that have no match on the right.
Reshape — change the rectangle itself


pivot_wider() — one column’s values become new column names; another column’s values populate them. Used when the tidy long form needs to be read by a human or by a wide-format model.

pivot_longer() — the inverse: many columns collapse into a name column and a value column. Used to get data into tidy form from spreadsheet-shaped inputs.
A compact demonstration
One chained pipeline exercising most of the verbs, for muscle memory:

Codemtcars |>
    rownames_to_column("car") |>
    mutate(power_to_weight = hp / wt) |>
    filter(cyl %in% c(4, 6, 8)) |>
    group_by(cyl) |>
    summarize(
        n          = n(),
        mean_mpg   = mean(mpg),
        mean_ptw   = mean(power_to_weight),
        .groups    = "drop"
    ) |>
    arrange(desc(mean_mpg))

# A tibble: 3 × 4
    cyl     n mean_mpg mean_ptw
  <dbl> <int>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1     4    11     26.7     37.9
2     6     7     19.7     39.9
3     8    14     15.1     53.9


One sentence about each verb in the pipeline: rownames_to_column() moves the implicit row-name index into a real column (because row names are a legacy feature that tibbles drop); mutate() adds a derived column; filter() subsets rows; group_by() sets the grouping for the next verb; summarize() collapses each group to one row; arrange(desc(...)) sorts descending. The pipeline is the mental model: shape the columns, shape the rows, group, collapse, sort.
crumbs: 37  01 — Introduction: Sources, Levels of Understanding, and Wrangling Review

title: 01 — Introduction: Sources, Levels of Understanding, and Wrangling Review
text:
Reflection
Two things I want to carry forward from this first lesson.
Source selection is a skill, not a preference. The naïve model is “use the best source.” The real model is which source answers which kind of question. A tutorial site answers how do I do X right now?; an LLM answers give me a fluent overview of X; the R manual answers what does X actually do under unusual inputs?; Advanced R answers why does X behave the way it does structurally?. A workflow that always reaches for the same source is a workflow that will be wrong in predictable ways — too shallow when precision matters, too dense when orientation is what’s needed. The cost of the wrong source is time: shallow sources fail silently on edge cases, dense sources eat hours on questions that had a one-sentence answer.
Low-level understanding is what lets code generalize. The typeof(df) == "list" line is the entire game. If I know a data frame is a list of vectors, purrr::map_dfc(), [[, bracket subsetting, and every coercion surprise become one system rather than five disconnected tricks. The high-level “table with columns” mental model is sufficient for mutate() and filter(); it’s insufficient the moment I need to write a function that takes any tabular object and does something sensible with it. This is the same instinct that sits behind the str() pass in later lessons — the object is a list underneath, so open it and look.

title: 02 — Review: File Paths, Git, and Data Wrangling
section: 🔥 Data Story Critique — The Rhythm of Food
text:
🔥 Data Story Critique — The Rhythm of Food

Source: https://rhythm-of-food.net/#explore-foods
Creators: Moritz Stefaner / Truth & Beauty (creative direction, design, viz); Yuri Vishnevsky (development); Google News Lab (Simon Rogers, Alberto Cairo); Stefanie Weigele (illustration); Destry Sibley (text). 12–15 years of weekly Google Trends data, US-focused, disambiguated through Google Knowledge Graph topics. 195 topics, 129,660 data points.
What is the data story?
The piece argues that food has measurable signatures of seasonality visible in how people search for it. Each food gets a radial “year clock”: the 52 angular segments are weeks of the year, distance from center encodes relative search interest, and color encodes which year the reading came from. Stacked over 12+ years, the charts surface two layers of pattern simultaneously — the shape of a food’s seasonality (does interest peak in November? in July? in no month at all?) and its trend (is the year-color drifting outward or inward over time?). The site builds its argument upward: an overview grid of many foods makes the existence of distinct seasonality signatures visible at a glance; the single-food detail views let the reader interrogate a specific rhythm; the annotated stories tie a handful of those rhythms to cultural events (Thanksgiving pumpkin, Easter lamb) or agricultural calendars (asparagus in April, corn in August).
What is effective?
The form genuinely matches the data. Seasonality is cyclical — December rolls into January. A linear line chart of weeks 1–52 forces the reader to mentally wrap the endpoints; a radial plot removes that translation step entirely. Decoding the chart as a clock is pre-cognitive, which is the right property for a chart that asks the reader to compare hundreds of foods.
Two variables in one mark, both used. Distance encodes interest; color encodes year. Most dual-encodings fail because one variable drowns out the other. Here it works because year is ordered and the color ramp is ordered — so a reader can see trend over years as a color gradient walking outward or inward, on top of the same radial shape that encodes within-year seasonality. Reading both axes at once is what makes “the rise of avocado” or “the collapse of kale” visible; a chart that showed only one axis would lose the argument.
Knowledge Graph disambiguation. “Apple” search interest is dominated by Apple Inc., not the fruit. The site uses Google Knowledge Graph topics rather than raw keyword strings, which resolves this and a long tail of similar ambiguities (rosemary the plant vs. the name, turkey the bird vs. the country). This is invisible engineering — it doesn’t appear in the UI — but it’s the difference between a dataset that supports the argument and a dataset that doesn’t. Naming it is worth a line because the tutorial-visualization genre often skips this step and then quietly serves the reader contaminated data.
The aggregate view earns the detail view. The overview grid is where the piece’s argument lives: looking at a wall of radial charts at once, you see that different foods have fundamentally different seasonality signatures — some spike sharply once, some have twin peaks, some are flat year-round. The detail view for any single food only lands because the aggregate has already established that “signatures exist” is a meaningful category.
What could be improved?
Search interest is not consumption. This is the central methodological issue and the site only glances at it. People search Google for pumpkin pie in November because Thanksgiving is coming and they don’t make pumpkin pie in the other eleven months — not because pumpkins are biologically in season. The rhythm being visualized is the rhythm of when people ask about food, which is correlated with but distinct from the rhythm of eating food. For stable-use commodity ingredients (salt, flour, oil), the signal is close to noise; for holiday-tied dishes, the signal is enormously amplified by a single cultural event. The title, The Rhythm of Food, collapses these two rhythms — cultural-attention rhythm and eating rhythm — into one word. A more careful framing would have been The Rhythm of Food Attention, which is a weaker title and a more honest one.
The radial form makes magnitude comparison harder, not easier. Polar coordinates turn linear differences in a value into nonlinear differences in visible area — area grows with the square of the radius. A week with search interest of 40 does not look twice as prominent as a week with interest of 20; it looks closer to four times as prominent. This is a well-documented failure mode of radial charts. The form’s elegance is not neutral: it visually exaggerates peak weeks and compresses trough weeks, which systematically inflates the apparent strength of any seasonality signature. The charts are more rhythmic-looking than the underlying data actually is.
Google Trends’ “search interest” is peak-normalized and opaque. Trends values are rescaled to 0–100 relative to the maximum within the query window, not absolute search counts. This means two foods’ radial charts cannot be compared by amplitude — each is normalized to its own peak. A reader who leaves the site thinking “pumpkin pie is more popular than oatmeal” has been misled, because the comparison is formally undefined by the data pipeline. This is disclosed on the methodology page but invisible in the main viewing experience.
“US data” is underdisclosed given the title. The site restricts to US searches. The rhythm detected is therefore Anglo-American search rhythm — which is not “the rhythm of food” in the universal sense the title implies. The seasonality of Lunar New Year foods, Diwali sweets, Ramadan breakfasts, Midsummer herring in Sweden, Jixi cold noodles in July — none of these appear, because the underlying searches don’t appear. A project that names itself after a universal would do well to either include the universal in the data or name its particularity in the title. “The Rhythm of American Food Searches” is the honest title; “The Rhythm of Food” is the marketable one.
The artifact is frozen; food culture is not. The data cuts off around 2019. It is now 2026. Foods that looked ascendant at launch (kale, turmeric, avocado) have continued their curves in either direction without being shown. A rhythm, by definition, continues. Presenting it as a finished object rather than a live feed converts an argument about ongoing cultural phenomenon into a historical exhibit.
Search-as-culture over-indexes on novelty. Google Trends captures a very specific slice of food attention — the “I want to know about X right now” kind, triggered by external stimulus (a TV mention, a magazine article, a holiday, a TikTok). It misses food culture that is so normalized it generates no search (daily rice, bread, eggs) and over-represents food culture driven by novelty (fads, diets, viral recipes). The rhythm you see is therefore the rhythm of food novelty cycling, not the rhythm of food as lived practice. This is the same bias that makes Google Trends a better tool for studying marketing than for studying consumption.

Construct relative file paths to read in data
