title: 13 — APIs I: JSON and jsonlite
section: 🔥 Data Story Critique — Cheaper Mortgages and Car Loans (NYT, 18 Sept 2024)
text:
🔥 Data Story Critique — Cheaper Mortgages and Car Loans (NYT, 18 Sept 2024)
Source: https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2024/09/18/business/economy/mortgages-car-loans-interest-rates.html
Author: Karl Russell (NYT graphics team).
What is the data story?
Published on the day the Federal Reserve announced its first rate cut in over four years — a 50-basis-point reduction that took the federal funds rate from 5.25–5.50% to 4.75–5.00% — the piece is a short explainer aimed at non-specialist readers. It situates the rate cut within a five-year window (2019–2024) using three overlaid time-series charts: (1) the federal funds rate alongside average 30-year mortgage and new-car-loan rates, (2) the federal funds rate alongside inflation, and (3) average credit-card APRs. The narrative takes six short paragraphs to walk the reader through three ideas: borrowing costs are easing; the Fed cut rates after years of fighting inflation; the transmission of rate changes to consumer products is not instantaneous.
What is effective?
The overlaid time-series chart is the right form for the claim. All three rates share a unit (percent interest) and a time axis, so putting them on one y-axis establishes comparability without the interpretive hazards of a secondary axis (which almost always misleads). What the reader sees is the co-movement of Fed funds and consumer borrowing rates across the tightening cycle — which is precisely the claim the piece is making. This is the least-astonishment principle in economic charting.
The 2019 baseline is well chosen for the explainer form. It captures pre-pandemic conditions, the COVID shock, the zero-rate era, the 2022–2023 tightening cycle, and the cut itself. Starting earlier would dilute attention on the recent event; starting later would lose the context that explains why the cut is news.
“Transmission is not instantaneous” is the most important line in the piece. Readers’ intuition is that when the Fed moves rates, consumer products follow immediately. They don’t — mortgage rates are mediated by the 10-year Treasury, credit-card APRs by lenders’ own repricing cycles. By explicitly flagging lag and using the credit-card chart as visual evidence (APRs kept climbing after Fed funds peaked), the piece sets the correct horizon expectation. This is a genuinely important piece of financial literacy that a lot of coverage skips.
Keeping credit-card APR on its own chart is a subtle analytical choice. At ~21%, it is an order of magnitude above mortgage and auto rates; putting all four on one chart would have compressed the mortgage/auto variation into invisibility. The separation also lets the reader notice an unspoken distributional point — that the people most reliant on credit-card borrowing (often lower-income, without home equity to draw on) pay rates that have barely moved while Fed policy swung dramatically.
What could be improved?
The Fed funds → mortgage rate causality is presented as direct, but it isn’t. Mortgage rates track the 10-year Treasury yield, not the federal funds rate — Fed funds drives short-end rates, and long-end rates respond through the term structure and market expectations. This is why the chart shows the mortgage rate already declining before the Fed cut on Wednesday: markets had priced the cut in weeks earlier. The piece doesn’t mention this, and the caption language (“Borrowing costs hit a two-decade high as part of the Fed’s inflation fight”) implies more direct causation than the data shows. A single sentence — “mortgage rates have been easing in anticipation of this cut, because markets set long-term rates based on where they expect Fed funds to go” — would be more honest and is arguably necessary to explain what the reader is looking at.
No distinction between real and nominal rates. The second chart places Fed funds against inflation on the same axis, but the Fed’s actual policy stance is the real rate — nominal minus inflation expectations. In 2022–2023, Fed funds rose steeply, but so did inflation, and the real rate’s trajectory is a different story from the nominal one the chart tells. Real-rate economics is outside an explainer’s scope, but a one-line note — “once you subtract out inflation, the Fed’s tightening was less aggressive than the blue line suggests” — would prevent the wrong inference that nominal rate level equals policy tightness.
The labour market is named in text but not visualized. The piece says “a variety of factors go into the Fed’s decision… including the strength of the labor market,” then describes unemployment rising and wage growth slowing — but no fourth chart supplies the data. In a piece whose default mode is “show, don’t tell,” this asymmetry is visible. A compact unemployment + wage-growth panel would give the claim visual weight.
The “waited too long” line hangs in isolation. “That has raised questions about whether the Fed waited too long to begin lowering rates” — who is raising these questions, with what argument? The Sahm rule trigger, the persistence of yield-curve inversion, the speed of labour-market cooling — these are the actual reasons the “too late” case is being made. The reader is left with a suggestion of debate but no substance. For a neutral explainer, either develop the point briefly or leave it out; in current form it reads as tacit editorial agreement without argument.
Proportionality. This is a same-day news explainer from a graphics reporter with very limited word count. It executes its brief well — correct reference frame, clean overlaid time series, honest horizon-setting. The critiques above are directed at what a slightly longer version of this piece could add, not at what this one got wrong.
crumbs: 48  13 — APIs I: JSON and `jsonlite`

title: 13 — APIs I: JSON and jsonlite
text:
JSON Basics
A minimal example

Code# JSON is just text — here stored as a string in R
json_basic <- '{
  "name": "John",
  "age": 30,
  "city": "New York",
  "hobbies": ["reading", "hiking", "coding"]
}'

# fromJSON() parses it into an R object
person <- fromJSON(json_basic)
person

$name
[1] "John"

$age
[1] 30

$city
[1] "New York"

$hobbies
[1] "reading" "hiking"  "coding" 



Codeclass(person)

[1] "list"

Codestr(person)

List of 4
 $ name   : chr "John"
 $ age    : int 30
 $ city   : chr "New York"
 $ hobbies: chr [1:3] "reading" "hiking" "coding"


The result is a named list because JSON objects map to named lists in R. The hobbies element inside the list is an unnamed collection that jsonlite simplified into a character vector — this is the “simplify” convention discussed in Ooms (2014) — a homogeneous JSON array becomes an atomic vector rather than a list.
Arrays of objects — the simplifyVector choice

Codejson_mario <- '[
  {"Name": "Mario",  "Age": 32, "Occupation": "Plumber"},
  {"Name": "Peach",  "Age": 21, "Occupation": "Princess"},
  {},
  {"Name": "Bowser",             "Occupation": "Koopa"}
]'


With simplifyVector = FALSE (no simplification): we get a list of four lists.

CodefromJSON(json_mario, simplifyVector = FALSE)

[[1]]
[[1]]$Name
[1] "Mario"

[[1]]$Age
[1] 32

[[1]]$Occupation
[1] "Plumber"


[[2]]
[[2]]$Name
[1] "Peach"

[[2]]$Age
[1] 21

[[2]]$Occupation
[1] "Princess"


[[3]]
named list()

[[4]]
[[4]]$Name
[1] "Bowser"

[[4]]$Occupation
[1] "Koopa"


With simplifyVector = TRUE (default): jsonlite detects that the records have compatible fields and returns a data frame.

CodefromJSON(json_mario, simplifyVector = TRUE)

    Name Age Occupation
1  Mario  32    Plumber
2  Peach  21   Princess
3   <NA>  NA       <NA>
4 Bowser  NA      Koopa


Three things worth noticing:

Where a record had no Name field (the third row), jsonlite inserted NA. This is the row-based-JSON / column-based-R translation at work — in the JSON, missing fields are simply absent; in a data frame, every row must have a value (or NA) for every column.
The third record ({}) maps to an all-NA row. It doesn’t disappear, which is the right behaviour: the presence of an empty record is itself information.
Once simplifyVector = TRUE gives us a tibble-shaped object, the result can flow directly into the tidyverse pipeline. This is the main reason the default is on.
When simplification fails — heterogeneous arrays
simplifyVector = TRUE is a best-effort heuristic. When records don’t share a consistent structure, jsonlite falls back:

Codejson_heterogeneous <- '[
  {"id": 1, "tags": ["red", "blue"]},
  {"id": 2, "tags": "red"},
  {"id": 3, "tags": null}
]'

fromJSON(json_heterogeneous, simplifyVector = TRUE)

  id      tags
1  1 red, blue
2  2       red
3  3      NULL


The tags column is a list-column — because in the three rows it’s respectively a length-2 character vector, a length-1 character vector, and NULL. A data frame column can’t be a regular atomic vector here because the lengths don’t match, so jsonlite produces a list-column. This is one of the places where JSON’s row-orientation collides with R’s column-orientation and the fix is not neat; the user is expected to tidyr::unnest() downstream.
crumbs: 48  13 — APIs I: JSON and `jsonlite`

title: 13 — APIs I: JSON and jsonlite
section: Edge Cases from the Ooms Paper
text:
Edge Cases from the Ooms Paper
Vectors of length 1
Ooms emphasizes that jsonlite always encodes length-1 vectors as JSON arrays (not as scalars), because inconsistency across lengths is a common source of bugs in dynamic-data pipelines.

Code# A length-1 vector stays an array in JSON
toJSON(pi)

[3.1416] 



Code# Round-trip preserves the vector
identical(fromJSON(toJSON(pi)), pi)

[1] FALSE


The rival packages rjson and RJSONIO handle this differently — rjson encodes a length-1 vector as a JSON primitive inside a list, which silently changes the type depending on vector length. For pipelines that consume API output of varying sizes, jsonlite’s consistency is a real feature, not a cosmetic one.
The “golden rule” — keys are names, not data
Ooms quotes Mike Dewar’s “golden rule”: JSON keys should be names, not data. A common violation is using timestamps as keys:

Code# Bad — keys are data (timestamps)
bad_json <- '[
  {"1325344443": 124},
  {"1325344456": 131},
  {"1325344478": 137}
]'

fromJSON(bad_json)

  1325344443 1325344456 1325344478
1        124         NA         NA
2         NA        131         NA
3         NA         NA        137


Every record has a different key, so no downstream code can address the “price” field by name. The fix is to promote the timestamp into its own field:

Code# Good — keys describe what the field means
good_json <- '[
  {"time": 1325344443, "price": 124},
  {"time": 1325344456, "price": 131},
  {"time": 1325344478, "price": 137}
]'

fromJSON(good_json)

        time price
1 1325344443   124
2 1325344456   131
3 1325344478   137


The “good” version simplifies cleanly to a tidy data frame; the “bad” version produces a list of list of named scalars that needs manual unpacking. The rule generalizes beyond JSON — any data-interchange format, and indeed any tabular data representation, works correctly when the schema (which keys exist, what they mean) is separated from the data (what values those keys take). Wickham’s tidy-data principle is the same insight, arrived at from the R side.
Nested records

Codenested_json <- '[
  {
    "driver": "Bowser",
    "vehicle": {"model": "Piranha Prowler", "stats": {"speed": 55, "weight": 67}}
  },
  {
    "driver": "Peach",
    "vehicle": {"model": "Royal Racer",     "stats": {"speed": 34, "weight": 24}}
  }
]'

nested <- fromJSON(nested_json, simplifyVector = TRUE)
nested

  driver   vehicle.model vehicle.stats.speed vehicle.stats.weight
1 Bowser Piranha Prowler                  55                   67
2  Peach     Royal Racer                  34                   24



Code# The `vehicle` column is itself a data frame — this is a nested data frame
str(nested)

'data.frame':   2 obs. of  2 variables:
 $ driver : chr  "Bowser" "Peach"
 $ vehicle:'data.frame':    2 obs. of  2 variables:
  ..$ model: chr  "Piranha Prowler" "Royal Racer"
  ..$ stats:'data.frame':   2 obs. of  2 variables:
  .. ..$ speed : int  55 34
  .. ..$ weight: int  67 24


For one-to-one nesting, tidyr::unnest_wider() flattens the structure cleanly:

Codenested |>
    tidyr::unnest_wider(vehicle) |>
    tidyr::unnest_wider(stats)

# A tibble: 2 × 4
  driver model           speed weight
  <chr>  <chr>           <int>  <int>
1 Bowser Piranha Prowler    55     67
2 Peach  Royal Racer        34     24


For one-to-many nesting (where a single record has an array of sub-records), unnest_longer() is the corresponding tool. Together, unnest_wider() and unnest_longer() cover most cases of bringing JSON-from-the-web into a tidy-data-frame shape.
crumbs: 48  13 — APIs I: JSON and `jsonlite`

title: 13 — APIs I: JSON and jsonlite
text:
A Real API Call
A typical use of fromJSON() is to fetch directly from a URL. GitHub’s public API returns JSON and does not require authentication for modest request volumes. Here we look up the latest releases of the tidyverse package:

Codetidy_releases <- fromJSON(
    "https://api.github.com/repos/tidyverse/tidyverse/releases"
)

# What did we get?
class(tidy_releases)

[1] "data.frame"

Codedim(tidy_releases)

[1]  9 21

Codenames(tidy_releases)

 [1] "url"              "assets_url"       "upload_url"       "html_url"        
 [5] "id"               "author"           "node_id"          "tag_name"        
 [9] "target_commitish" "name"             "draft"            "immutable"       
[13] "prerelease"       "created_at"       "updated_at"       "published_at"    
[17] "assets"           "tarball_url"      "zipball_url"      "body"            
[21] "reactions"       



Code# Pull out the columns that are actually useful for a release summary
tidy_releases |>
    as_tibble() |>
    transmute(
        tag       = tag_name,
        name      = name,
        published = lubridate::as_date(published_at),
        prerelease = prerelease
    ) |>
    arrange(desc(published))

# A tibble: 9 × 4
  tag    name            published  prerelease
  <chr>  <chr>           <date>     <lgl>     
1 v2.0.0 tidyverse 2.0.0 2023-02-23 FALSE     
2 v1.3.2 tidyverse 1.3.2 2022-07-18 FALSE     
3 v1.3.1 tidyverse 1.3.1 2021-04-15 FALSE     
4 v1.3.0 tidyverse 1.3.0 2019-11-21 FALSE     
5 v1.2.1 tidyverse 1.2.1 2017-11-14 FALSE     
6 v1.2.0 tidyverse 1.2.0 2017-11-14 FALSE     
7 v1.1.1 tidyverse 1.1.1 2017-01-27 FALSE     
8 v1.1.0 tidyverse 1.1.0 2017-01-20 FALSE     
9 v1.0.0 Tidyverse 1.0.0 2016-09-15 FALSE     


The GitHub API returns an array of release objects, which jsonlite simplifies into a data frame. Columns like author, assets, and reactions come through as nested data frames or list-columns (you can check with str(tidy_releases, max.level = 1)), reflecting the one-to-many relationships in the underlying schema — each release has one author but can have multiple assets. The rest of this course’s API material is essentially about this pattern: hit a URL, get JSON back, negotiate the nesting, reshape into a tibble.
crumbs: 48  13 — APIs I: JSON and `jsonlite`

title: 13 — APIs I: JSON and jsonlite
text:
Reflection
JSON and R have an impedance mismatch, and jsonlite is the translator. R thinks in columns; JSON thinks in rows. R has vectors as a first-class type; JSON has objects and arrays but no separate notion of a scalar. When you call fromJSON(), you are invoking a set of conventions — described in Ooms (2014) — for how to bridge the two. The single most important convention is simplifyVector = TRUE: when the structure permits, promote JSON arrays of compatible objects into data frames. When it doesn’t permit, fall back to list-columns rather than fail. This is not a mathematical isomorphism — fromJSON(toJSON(x)) is not always exactly x — but it is the practical mapping that makes JSON APIs usable from R.
Type consistency is a runtime contract you have to enforce yourself. API documentation tells you what fields should be returned, but reality will hand you missing fields, null values, length-1 arrays that could-have-been scalars, and the occasional structural change. jsonlite does its best — consistent encoding of length-1 vectors as arrays, NA insertion for missing fields in simplified data frames — but the consumer of the API still needs to validate that the shape of the data matches expectations. The map_*() functions from ICA 12 are relevant here: map_chr() will fail loudly if any element isn’t a character, which is what you want when the upstream JSON has silently started returning numeric IDs instead of string IDs.
The “golden rule” is a data-modeling principle, not a JSON one. Keys-describe-meaning and values-carry-data is the same rule that makes relational schemas normalise-able, that makes tidy data tidy, and that makes APIs versionable. When you design data structures that will cross system boundaries — whether as JSON over HTTP, as Parquet on disk, or as tables in a warehouse — holding the schema steady while the data varies is what makes downstream code possible to write. Everything in the next ICA about URL construction and query parameters is downstream of this principle.

library(jsonlite)

section: 🔥 Data Story Critique — How many high school stars make it in the NBA? (The Pudding, Feb 2019)
text:
🔥 Data Story Critique — How many high school stars make it in the NBA? (The Pudding, Feb 2019)
Source: https://pudding.cool/2019/03/hype/
Authors: Russell Samora & Amber Thomas.
What is the data story?
The piece asks whether high-school basketball recruiting rankings predict NBA success, using the Recruiting Services Consensus Index (RSCI) top-100 lists from 1998 to 2013 as the cohort and following those 1,563 players through a funnel of outcomes: college, draft, two-year-rookie-contract survival, and four performance tiers — below average, mediocre, great, superstar — defined by the mean of each player’s top-five-season ranking on combined VORP and Estimated Wins Added metrics. The visual spine is an animated circle-transition where each player is a dot that moves across stages, letting the reader watch the cohort thin out at each filter. The story unfolds through progressive refinement: top-100 overall (27% make the NBA, ~2% become superstars), then top-10 (84% make the NBA), then the 1998–2005 straight-to-NBA cohort (94% past rookie contract despite skipping college), then the 338 drafted but HS-unranked players (whose superstar list includes Wade, Curry, Lillard, Butler, Westbrook, Marc Gasol, and Paul George), and finally the Big-4 college pipeline (Kentucky, Kansas, Duke, UNC) plus a table of the top NBA-producing programs.
What is effective?
The animated funnel is the right form for the question. The quantity the piece is communicating is attrition across stages — how many of 1,563 players drop out at each filter, and what shape the surviving population takes. Static bar charts could show the endpoint but not the motion; sankey diagrams could show the flow but not the scale; the dot-transition form preserves both, and the “Replay” affordance acknowledges that the animation is the point.
Named individuals anchor the statistics. Donnell Harvey vs. LeBron James in the opening paragraph; Korleone Young as the cautionary case for removing the age limit; the seven unranked-but-superstar names. These are the specific instances that make an abstract rate (“27% reach the NBA”) legible — without them, the piece would be a series of percentages the reader would forget by the next section.
Methodology transparency is unusual for popular visual journalism. The bottom note specifies the tier thresholds (Superstar = top 30 by VORP + EWA, Great = top 60, Mediocre = top 120, Below Average = 120+), the time window (1998–2013), sample sizes per view, and the data sources (RSCI, Basketball Reference). Most pieces in this genre hide these decisions; The Pudding shows them, and that’s part of why the argument holds up under scrutiny.
The structure of progressive refinement is pedagogically sound. Each “but what about…” view — top 100 → top 10 → straight-to-NBA → unranked-but-drafted → Big 4 — reframes the question more precisely. By the end the reader has a multi-view answer (“HS rank predicts better than chance but there’s a long tail of underranked superstars”) rather than a single headline statistic. This is closer to how an empirical question actually gets answered than most popular treatments manage.
What could be improved?
The framing measures within-pipeline prediction, not talent identification. The piece asks whether RSCI rank predicts NBA outcome among players who entered the top-100 HS list. That is a narrower question than the title implies. “Do HS rankings identify basketball talent?” would require the denominator to include players who never made the top-100 — all US high-school basketball players, international players, junior-college players — and to ask how well the ranking system would have done against that population. The “unranked but drafted” section partially addresses this, but it still conditions on having been drafted, which is itself a selected outcome. The reader is implicitly invited to conclude that HS rank is a reasonable predictor of NBA success; the tighter conclusion the data actually supports is that HS rank is a reasonable predictor conditional on the player already being in the scouting pipeline.
RSCI is a constructed instrument whose evaluation heuristics partly overlap with NBA draft heuristics. The 84% top-10-to-NBA rate looks like strong predictive validity, but it’s confounded by selection: high HS rank gets a player better college offers, which means better NCAA exposure, which means higher draft position, which means more NBA opportunity. Some of what the funnel is measuring is pipeline access (ranked players are placed into environments that produce NBA players) rather than latent talent (ranked players are fundamentally better). Disentangling these would require something like comparing ranked vs. unranked players within the same college program, or looking at late-rising players who weren’t ranked in HS but performed at comparable college levels. The piece doesn’t make this distinction, and without it, “HS ranking works” conflates prediction with construction.
The 2010–2013 cohort is right-censored. Players drafted in 2013 had six years of NBA careers by the time of publication (February 2019). The “superstar” tier is defined by the mean of a player’s top-five seasons — but a player whose best seasons will be years 5–9 of their career (Giannis Antetokounmpo was drafted in 2013 and didn’t reach MVP level until 2018–19) hasn’t had time to register as a superstar in this dataset. Later cohorts are therefore systematically undercounted at the top tiers while earlier cohorts have fully resolved. The piece treats the data as complete. A cleaner version would either restrict the cohort to players with at least N seasons of career, or visibly flag the censoring.
Superstar count is small enough that the specific numbers have substantial uncertainty. With 31 superstars across 1,563 players (~2%), the 95% confidence interval on the rate is roughly 1.4%–2.8% by normal approximation. This matters because the piece reports rates with two significant digits (“1%”, “2%”, “<1%”) as if they were stable quantities. For tier proportions derived from small integer counts, visualizing or at least mentioning the uncertainty would keep the reader calibrated about what the funnel is and isn’t pinning down.
The animation-only visual carries accessibility costs. Dot-transition visualizations are hostile to screen-reader users, keyboard-only navigation, and users with vestibular disorders or motion sensitivity. The “Replay” button shows the designers knew the animation was load-bearing, but there is no “skip / show final state” option — the reader has to sit through the motion to reach the summary. A parallel static-summary view (final positions, counts, percentages in a text table) would add minutes of work for the designers and open the piece to a meaningfully wider audience.
The NCAA labor question is gestured at, not engaged. The piece notes “the college funnel is an interesting discussion, especially when it surrounds the lack of pay for NCAA student athletes” — but then pivots to the table of top NBA-producing programs without developing that thread. The data The Pudding collected is directly relevant: Kentucky and Duke convert high HS rankings into NBA pipelines at rates far above other schools, and players whose HS rankings attracted them to those programs generated substantial revenue without compensation during this study window. That is an argumentative opportunity, and the piece walks past it. This is an editorial choice rather than a failure, but worth naming as the choice it is.
Proportionality. This is among the best sports data journalism of its year — the methodology is transparent, the question is genuinely well-posed, the visual form fits the quantity being communicated, and the named-individual anchoring works. The critiques above are directed at the piece’s second-order structure (framing, censoring, selection), not at its execution.

title: 14 — APIs II: Web Requests, XML, and Nested JSON
section: Census API — Building a URL with httr2
text:
Census API — Building a URL with httr2

The Census API allows small queries without a key. Large production use requires a (free) key from https://api.census.gov/data/key_signup.html; store it locally in census_api_key.txt and load with readLines().

Codecensus_api_key <- if (file.exists("census_api_key.txt")) {
    readLines("census_api_key.txt")
} else {
    ""
}


Building the request

Codereq <- request("https://api.census.gov") |>
    req_url_path_append("data") |>
    req_url_path_append("2019") |>
    req_url_path_append("acs") |>
    req_url_path_append("acs1") |>
    req_url_query(
        get    = c("NAME", "B02015_009E", "B02015_009M"),
        `for`  = I("state:*"),
        key    = census_api_key,
        .multi = "comma"
    )

req


Three craftsmanship points worth naming:


I() around "state:*". Without the “as-is” wrapper, httr2 would percent-encode the : and * into %3A and %2A, which the Census API would then fail to interpret. I() tells httr2 to pass the value through literally. The same pattern applies any time a query value contains characters that look special but are meaningful to the target API.

`for`. Backticks are required because for is a reserved word in R (the for-loop keyword). Any query-parameter name that collides with an R reserved word, or that contains spaces/dashes/dots, needs backtick escaping.

.multi = "comma". The Census API expects comma-separated lists for multi-valued parameters (get=NAME,B02015_009E,B02015_009M). Other APIs use different conventions — some repeat the key (?tag=red&tag=blue), some use brackets (?tag[]=red&tag[]=blue). .multi is where you configure this per API.
Performing the request and parsing the response

Coderesp <- req_perform(req)

resp_json <- resp |> resp_body_json(simplifyVector = TRUE)

head(resp_json, 3)

     [,1]          [,2]          [,3]          [,4]   
[1,] "NAME"        "B02015_009E" "B02015_009M" "state"
[2,] "Mississippi" NA            NA            "28"   
[3,] "Missouri"    "953"         "1141"        "29"   


The response comes back as a matrix with the first row as column headers — Census’s convention, not JSON’s. janitor::row_to_names() promotes the first row to column names, and then we convert the numeric variables:

Coderesp_df <- resp_json |>
    row_to_names(1) |>
    as_tibble() |>
    mutate(across(starts_with("B"), as.numeric))

resp_df |>
    arrange(desc(B02015_009E)) |>
    head(10)

# A tibble: 10 × 4
   NAME           B02015_009E B02015_009M state
   <chr>                <dbl>       <dbl> <chr>
 1 California           95738       10184 06   
 2 Minnesota            91051        7256 27   
 3 Wisconsin            51816        4827 55   
 4 North Carolina       17317        3062 37   
 5 Michigan              8955        3337 26   
 6 Oklahoma              4967        1497 40   
 7 Washington            4601        2651 53   
 8 Georgia               3162        1336 13   
 9 Oregon                3087        2214 41   
10 Colorado              3071        1267 08   


B02015_009E is the estimated count of persons self-identifying as Korean in the 2019 ACS 1-year sample; _009M is the margin of error. The _E/_M convention is pervasive in Census data and worth knowing — every estimate is paired with a margin of error, and responsible analysis respects that pairing.

title: 14 — APIs II: Web Requests, XML, and Nested JSON
section: New York Times API — Function-Wrapped Requests and Nested JSON
crumbs: 49  14 — APIs II: Web Requests, XML, and Nested JSON

title: 14 — APIs II: Web Requests, XML, and Nested JSON
text:
Reflection
URLs are structured objects, not strings. The habit of pasting together URLs with paste0() works for simple cases and fails quietly on anything with spaces, ampersands, unicode characters, or special regex-adjacent punctuation. httr2’s stepwise construction — request() + req_url_path_append() + req_url_query() — makes the structure explicit and handles encoding correctly. The I() “as-is” wrapper and the .multi parameter are the two places this structure leaks from documentation into code. Once you’ve internalized the pattern, it carries across any HTTP API — GitHub, Twitter, Spotify, Reddit, Slack — with only the endpoint and parameter names changing.
JSON and XML are both hierarchical, but their tooling is different. jsonlite::fromJSON(simplifyVector = TRUE) aggressively collapses JSON arrays of compatible objects into data frames — which is what you want 80% of the time for data-on-the-web. xml2::xml_find_all() with XPath expressions walks the tree node by node — which is what you have to do because XML makes no assumption that the tree has tabular structure. The Census/NYT JSON pipelines look very different from the BGG XML pipeline in this document, and that difference is inherent to the formats rather than accidental. When working with a new API, the first thing to check is which format it speaks, because that decides which tool family you’ll spend the next hour in.
API-wrapped-as-function is where the iteration patterns from ICA 12 pay off. create_nyt_url() takes one query string and returns a request. Once that’s in place, issuing a thousand queries is crossing() + pmap() — each row of a crossing grid becomes one query, each query becomes one request, each request gets performed in a map() step, results get parsed with resp_body_json() in another map(), and the whole thing lands in a tidy data frame via bind_rows(). The architecture for a sensitivity analysis over model specifications and the architecture for a rate-limited scrape of a JSON API are the same skeleton. Everything in ICAs 11–14 has been constructing the vocabulary for that skeleton; the APIs layer is where it starts to do work you couldn’t do by hand.
crumbs: 49  14 — APIs II: Web Requests, XML, and Nested JSON

title: 15 — Web Scraping with rvest
section: 🔥 Data Story Critique — Equity in Community Development (City of Saint Paul)
text:
🔥 Data Story Critique — Equity in Community Development (City of Saint Paul)
Source: https://storymaps.arcgis.com/collections/7ccaef0b7a4b4166804f36e64e43c4cd?item=5
What is the data story?
The piece is a municipal data dashboard produced by the City of Saint Paul as part of its open-data initiative, framed explicitly around the vision of “a city where race does not predetermine opportunities in education, employment, housing, health, and safety.” It presents a racial-demographic dot map of Saint Paul (each resident a dot randomly placed within their census block group, color-coded by race/ethnicity from ACS responses) followed by six “core racial equity metrics” with definitions and charts: unemployment, labor force participation, per capita income, homeownership, cost-burdened households, and business ownership. The dashboard sits inside the city’s broader Open Information Saint Paul portal — so it functions both as a standalone piece of civic communication and as an entry point into the city’s public data infrastructure. Unlike the NGO and news pieces we’ve critiqued, this is a government document, which imposes a distinct set of responsibilities: residents-as-audience rather than donors, public-accountability obligations, and compliance with Minnesota’s open-records and open-data expectations.
What is effective?
Upfront operational definitions for every metric. Unemployment is defined as “16 years and older, actively looking for work, not currently employed,” and explicitly notes what it excludes — discouraged workers, underemployed, skills-mismatched. This kind of operational transparency is unusual for municipal communication and is what separates data-as-civic-infrastructure from data-as-rhetoric. A reader can check what the number means and what it doesn’t.
Self-awareness about metric limitations. The cost-burdened households section names the known shortcomings of the 30%-of-income threshold: “a low-income household that earns $12,000 per year may not have enough money left over for other needed expenses after paying 30% of their income for housing. Additionally, the statistic makes no adjustment for family size.” This caveat rarely appears in government dashboards, and its presence here signals that the authors understand the residual-income critique of the HUD affordability threshold. Naming the limitation is the first step toward choosing better metrics.
The dot map is ethically designed. Random placement within census block groups, rather than at address-level precision, prevents the map from functioning as a targeting tool for anyone who might use racial demographics for discriminatory purposes. This is the design pattern pioneered by the University of Virginia’s Racial Dot Map (2013); Saint Paul correctly adopts it. The choice of block-group aggregation is also right — finer than tracts (preserves neighborhood texture), coarser than parcels (respects privacy).
Integration with the broader open-data portal. The dashboard is not a standalone marketing piece — it links out to Open Information Saint Paul, the city’s hub for public data. This positions the dashboard as part of an infrastructure of transparency rather than as a communications artifact, which is the correct institutional framing for municipal data work.
What could be improved?
“People of color” as a single category flattens heterogeneity that is uniquely important in Saint Paul. The city has one of the largest Hmong-American communities in the United States (~11% of the population, one of the highest concentrations nationally), plus substantial Somali, Ethiopian, Karen, and Latino populations. These communities have different immigration histories, different generational depths in Minnesota, and therefore different economic and housing trajectories — Hmong homeownership rates are much higher than Somali homeownership rates for structural reasons tied to arrival timing and refugee resettlement cohorts, not to any cross-group comparison of effort or values. Presenting aggregate “white vs. people of color” gaps obscures where the equity work is most acute and where it has been partially successful. The city has the ACS data to disaggregate — the decision not to is a communications choice that costs policy precision.
All six metrics are outcome metrics; none are process metrics under the city’s direct control. Unemployment, income, homeownership, cost-burden, business ownership — these are places in the system where inequity arrives. Missing are the upstream processes the city government itself has levers on: mortgage rejection rates by race (HMDA data is public), eviction filings by neighborhood demographic, police stop rates by neighborhood demographic, school-funding disparities, city contracting disaggregated by owner demographic, Section 8 voucher placement rates. A municipal equity dashboard without process metrics is a dashboard of the problem; adding process metrics turns it into a dashboard the city can be held accountable to.
The framing is point-in-time for a question that is fundamentally about trajectory. The piece notes that “the gap has widened in per capita income between white people and people of color” but the standard presentation is a snapshot. Equity work is about direction and rate of change: is the gap closing, at what pace, and relative to what target? A commitment to “a city where race does not predetermine opportunities” is meaningful only when paired with a temporal trajectory and a target trajectory. The ArcGIS host may support this through tabs or sliders, but the document as written does not foreground trend.
No commitments, no targets, no accountability mechanisms. The dashboard describes where Saint Paul is. It does not tell the reader what the city has promised to be, by when, measured how, or with what consequences if the gaps do not close. In municipal governance scholarship, the distance between describing inequity and committing to measurable correction is sometimes called “equity theater” — the performance of awareness without the commitment to change. For a city that has positioned this dashboard as part of an equity framework, the absence of linked commitments is the single most important next step.
Causal framing is absent where it is most needed. “People of color drove much of that [population] growth, while the white population has primarily remained the same over time” — presented as observation. The forces producing this pattern are not named: immigration, differential birth rates, white flight to suburban ring, differential cost-of-living displacement, the city’s own historical role (redlining of Rondo, the construction of I-94 through the heart of that Black neighborhood in the 1960s). A municipal equity dashboard that does not name the city government’s own historical production of current disparities — including the documented Rondo displacement — is accepting the disparities as demographic rather than structural. The accountability question is sharper when the production is acknowledged.
Proportionality. This is a credible municipal effort — above-median for city-produced equity dashboards nationally on definitional clarity, open-data integration, and ethical map design. The critiques above are directed at the next iteration: within-POC disaggregation, process metrics under city control, temporal trajectory, explicit targets with accountability, and causal acknowledgment. These are the moves that separate a civic-transparency document from a governance-accountability instrument.

title: 15 — Web Scraping with rvest
text:
Ethics — Reading robots.txt

Before scraping, check the site’s robots.txt file. For the National Institutes of Health, this is at https://www.nih.gov/robots.txt. The key fields to look for:


User-agent: * — who is being addressed. * means everyone; specific bot names (Googlebot, Bingbot) mean only those.

Disallow: /path/ — paths you must not scrape.

Crawl-delay: N — minimum wait between requests in seconds.

Visit-time: HH:MM-HH:MM — permitted hours of scraping (rare).

For the NIH news releases path we’re targeting, none of these are restrictive: scraping is allowed, no crawl-delay specified, no time-of-day restriction. Even so, the polite default is to Sys.sleep(2) between page requests — you’re sharing the server with the hospital systems and researchers who are the actual intended users.

title: 15 — Web Scraping with rvest
section: Scraping One Page of NIH News Releases

title: 15 — Web Scraping with rvest
15 — Web Scraping with rvest
section: Iterating Across Pages — map() and for Loop
text:
Iterating Across Pages — map() and for Loop
URL pattern

Code# Mousing over the pagination buttons at the bottom of the NIH page reveals
# that page 1 is the base URL, and subsequent pages append `?page=1`, `?page=2`, etc.
base_url <- "https://www.nih.gov/news-events/news-releases"
urls_5   <- c(base_url, str_c(base_url, "?page=", 1:4))
urls_5

[1] "https://www.nih.gov/news-events/news-releases"       
[2] "https://www.nih.gov/news-events/news-releases?page=1"
[3] "https://www.nih.gov/news-events/news-releases?page=2"
[4] "https://www.nih.gov/news-events/news-releases?page=3"
[5] "https://www.nih.gov/news-events/news-releases?page=4"


With purrr::map()


Codepages_map <- map(urls_5, scrape_nih_page)
df_map    <- bind_rows(pages_map)

nrow(df_map)

[1] 50

Codehead(df_map, 5)

# A tibble: 5 × 4
  title                                                pub_date abstract src_url
  <chr>                                                <chr>    <chr>    <chr>  
1 Symptom-based approach treats opioid withdrawal in … April 2… NIH-fun… https:…
2 Clinical trial finds no difference in fluid treatme… April 2… NIH-sup… https:…
3 NIH-funded AI model predicts cancer survival from s… April 2… Study l… https:…
4 Dr. Jonathan M. Green selected as CEO of the NIH Cl… April 2… The NIH… https:…
5 NIH-funded breakthrough shrinks CRISPR for precisio… April 1… Smaller… https:…


With a for loop

Codepages_for <- vector("list", length = length(urls_5))

for (i in seq_along(urls_5)) {
    pages_for[[i]] <- scrape_nih_page(urls_5[i])
}

df_for <- bind_rows(pages_for)

nrow(df_for)

[1] 50


Equivalence check

Codeidentical(df_map, df_for)

[1] TRUE


The map() version is shorter, reads more like a sentence (“map scrape_nih_page over the URL vector”), and avoids explicit storage allocation. The for loop earns its place when there’s iteration state that map() can’t carry — adaptive backoff after a rate-limit error, for instance, where iteration i+1’s behaviour depends on iteration i’s response. For straight parallel application, map() wins on readability every time.

Scraped Data — Summary

Codedf_map |>

 3 April 21, 2026 2026-04-21      NIH-funded AI model predicts cancer survival …
 4 April 21, 2026 2026-04-21      Dr. Jonathan M. Green selected as CEO of the …
 5 April 13, 2026 2026-04-13      NIH-funded breakthrough shrinks CRISPR for pr…
 6 April 8, 2026  2026-04-08      Low-cost care model reduces blood pressure in…
 7 April 1, 2026  2026-04-01      NIH researchers discover pain-relieving drug …
 8 March 30, 2026 2026-03-30      NIH awards top scientific teams for innovatio…
 9 March 30, 2026 2026-03-30      Dr. Elisabeth Armstrong named NIH Chief of St…
10 March 25, 2026 2026-03-25      Chronic inflammation leaves long-lasting impr…


Fifty news releases across five pages. The parsed publication dates let us see the temporal range the NIH News page is currently surfacing, which is useful context for any downstream analysis of news-release volume, topic cadence, etc.

title: 15 — Web Scraping with rvest
text:
Reflection
CSS selectors are the interface between page design and scraping code. A selector like .thumbnail-teaser__heading is not a universal semantic ID — it is a class name chosen by the NIH web team for styling purposes. If they redesign the site tomorrow and rename that class to .news-item__title, every scraper keyed on the old name silently breaks. This is a genuine structural fragility of web scraping that APIs do not have: an API’s field names are part of a documented contract, but a website’s class names are an internal styling convention that can change without notice. The ICA’s warning — “save and date a copy of the website” — is the practical response. For any scraping pipeline intended to run beyond a single session, snapshot the HTML, re-verify selectors periodically, and write the code defensively (check that the number of elements returned matches expectations, and fail loudly if it doesn’t).
Scraping etiquette is not optional, even when robots.txt permits it. A scraper that hammers a server with unbounded parallel requests is a denial-of-service event, regardless of what the text file says. Sys.sleep(2) between requests, User-Agent identification when the library permits, caching of results to avoid re-scraping on every iteration, and running scrapes during off-peak hours are the minimum discipline. The NIH example is a public health agency whose website serves researchers, clinicians, and patients looking for trial information; a well-behaved scraper is a precondition for the long-term viability of scraping as a research technique.
The crossing() + pmap() skeleton still holds; scraping is just another pmap() target. The pattern of this ICA — write a function that handles one input, iterate over many inputs, bind results — is identical to the pattern in the API ICA, the case-study ICA in Loops I, and the simulation ICA in Missing Data. The only difference is what the function does inside: here it hits a URL, parses HTML, extracts fields; in the API case it hit a URL, parsed JSON, extracted fields. Almost every “do this analysis many times” pattern you will encounter in empirical work reduces to this skeleton. The skill being built across these weeks is the instinct for recognizing when a problem has that shape, and the discipline for wrapping the inner step cleanly enough that the outer iteration is trivial.

title: 16 — Databases and SQL
section: 🔥 Data Story Critique — UNODC Synthetic Drug Strategy 2021–2025
text:
🔥 Data Story Critique — UNODC Synthetic Drug Strategy 2021–2025

Source: https://vvv.vev.site/unodc-syntheticdrugstrategy-en/
What is the data story?
The piece is the public-facing Vev microsite for UNODC’s (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime) five-year Synthetic Drug Strategy. It opens on the structural claim that defines the document’s thesis: despite escalating law-enforcement seizures and rising user populations, synthetic-drug prices have been falling while purity has been maintained or increasing — a market signal that UNODC explicitly labels a “perfect storm.” Downstream sections develop the explosion of New Psychoactive Substances (6× growth since 2009 to 1,047 unique substances; ~80 new substances per year), the gender dimension of synthetic-drug use and treatment, and the organization’s “Four Spheres of Action” framework (monitoring, health, law enforcement, international cooperation). The microsite closes with a signed quote from Executive Director Ghada Waly citing a 71% rise in opioid-use-disorder deaths and “record high” methamphetamine seizures.
A framing note before the critique: UNODC is a first-rate drug-research institution, and its flagship World Drug Report is the global reference document for this data. The microsite is not UNODC’s analytical ceiling — it’s the register the organization chose for non-specialist public communication. The critique therefore targets editorial and institutional-positioning choices, not research capacity.
What is effective?
The price-purity-seizure co-movement is a genuinely rigorous economic diagnostic. Standard supply-and-demand logic predicts that rising seizures combined with rising user populations should either increase prices or reduce purity. Observing the opposite — falling prices, maintained or rising purity, rising user counts, rising seizures — is a signature of supply decoupling from interdiction: production capacity has scaled past what enforcement can intercept, making seizures a rounding error on the supply curve rather than a binding constraint. UNODC articulating this publicly is consequential because it is the UN body responsible for coordinating supply-interdiction regimes; the diagnosis implicitly concedes that the past five decades’ dominant policy tool has lost marginal effectiveness for this drug class. That is institutional candor, and it deserves credit.
The NPS figures carry the sharpest policy implication in the document. A 6× increase in the number of catalogued new psychoactive substances since 2009, with approximately one new molecule introduced every 4.5 days on average, means the international scheduling system — which operates through the 1961, 1971, and 1988 Conventions with scheduling processes measured in ~18-month units — is structurally unable to keep pace. This is the most analytically compelling numerical fact in the microsite and the one with the clearest regulatory-design consequence: the scheduling-based control paradigm has been outflanked by the chemistry.
“Sought-after commodities” is a deliberate framing choice. By placing synthetic drugs inside an economic framework — a market with supply, demand, producers, and intermediaries — rather than a moralistic framework (“evil substances”) or a purely clinical one (“public health crisis”), UNODC is signaling an analytical register appropriate to the problem’s actual structure. For a UN enforcement-oriented agency, this is non-trivial.
Women-and-drugs appears as a top-level section, not a footnote. Sex-disaggregated patterns in synthetic-drug use, treatment-access gaps for women, and the specific outcomes for female users are well-documented but routinely marginal in enforcement narratives. Placing this as a peer section rather than an appendix is a small editorial choice that signals the right analytical frame.
What could be improved?
The diagnosis and the prescription do not align. If the “perfect storm” claim is correct — production capacity has so outstripped interdiction that the marginal dollar spent on enforcement returns near zero in supply terms — then the policy conclusion should be a reallocation of effort toward demand reduction, harm reduction, treatment expansion, and precursor-chemical governance. But the “Four Spheres of Action” framework, when expanded, continues to weight law enforcement as a coequal sphere without revisiting its marginal-return logic. The document diagnoses A and prescribes B, without acknowledging the tension. This is the microsite’s most important unresolved problem.
Measurement uncertainty in the public version is flatter than in UNODC’s own technical documents. The price and purity indices anchoring the “perfect storm” argument come from seizure-based lab analysis, undercover purchases, and user self-report — each with systematic biases that the World Drug Report’s methodology annexes discuss at length. The lab-analysis purity data, in particular, reflects only what was seized, and if interdiction preferentially captures certain product categories (bulk shipments vs. street-level inventory), purity trends partly index sampling rather than market reality. The public microsite presents the co-movement as self-evident; the organization’s own technical documentation acknowledges it is partly interpretive. The register shift matters because the public version carries weight in policy debates where the caveats in the annexes are invisible.
The opioid citation at the close is rhetorical rather than analytical. The microsite is thematically about amphetamine-type stimulants and NPS; the mechanisms driving the opioid crisis (prescription-opioid-to-heroin-to-illicit-fentanyl transition in North America) are distinct from the mechanisms driving synthetic-stimulant expansion (precursor-control evasion, modular small-batch production). Appending “opioid-use-disorder deaths up 71%” to the executive-director quote bundles these under a unified urgency frame that the document’s own analysis does not support. This is the genre convention of closing on an affect-heavy number, but it muddies the analytical case the rest of the piece built.
Demand-side dynamics go un-interrogated. The microsite reasons as if the demand curve has been static while supply has expanded. But synthetic-drug user demographics over the past decade have shifted substantially — from recreational male users to a more mixed population, from urban middle-class to rural working-class in several regions, from exclusively party contexts to functional and workplace use. A demand curve that has become more elastic because new price-sensitive cohorts have entered the market is consistent with the same observations (falling prices, rising quantities) without requiring the supply-side-only explanation the piece leans on. The analytical cost of not naming this is that readers infer supply interdiction failed when part of the story is that demand itself shifted.
Geographic heterogeneity is flattened into “global.” Synthetic-drug markets are strongly regional in ways that matter for policy. Captagon’s concentration in the Middle East (with well-documented Syrian state involvement in the production chain), methamphetamine’s distinct markets in North America versus East/Southeast Asia versus Oceania, the ketamine supply chain’s different geography — these are different political economies, not instances of one problem. The “1 in 200 of the global population” per-capita figure is the flattening stripped to a single number. UNODC has the regional data; the microsite chose not to surface it.
Proportionality. UNODC is a top-tier research institution and this is a public communications document, not its analytical output. The critiques above are about the translation from research to public communication — a hard editorial problem that all such institutions face. Within that constraint, the diagnostic framing is sharp and the NPS point is genuinely important. The next version of this document should close the diagnosis-prescription gap, import the technical-annex uncertainty language, and let geography and demand carry their own weight.

title: 16 — Databases and SQL
section: Setting Up a Local Database with DuckDB
text:
Setting Up a Local Database with DuckDB
DuckDB is an in-process columnar database — fast, zero-configuration, and well-suited to interactive analysis. We create an in-memory database, write two nycflights13 tables to it, and then query them through dbplyr.

Codecon <- DBI::dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb())

# Write datasets to the database
dbWriteTable(con, "flights", nycflights13::flights)
dbWriteTable(con, "planes",  nycflights13::planes)

# Confirm what lives in the database
dbListTables(con)

[1] "flights" "planes" 



Code# `tbl()` returns a lazy table — no data is pulled into R until needed
flights <- tbl(con, "flights")
planes  <- tbl(con, "planes")

class(flights)

[1] "tbl_duckdb_connection" "tbl_dbi"               "tbl_sql"              
[4] "tbl_lazy"              "tbl"                  

Codedim(flights)   # nrow is NA because the data hasn't been loaded

[1] NA 19


The dim() returning NA for rows is intentional. A remote table is a handle to something that lives in the database, not a materialized object — dbplyr translates R verbs into SQL, ships the SQL to the database, and only pulls the result back when asked with collect() or when a value is needed for printing.

title: 16 — Databases and SQL
text:
dplyr ↔︎ SQL Translation
A concise mapping







tidyverse verb
SQL clause
notes



filter(col == x)
WHERE col = x
single = for equality in SQL


filter(a & b)
WHERE a AND b



filter(a \| b)
WHERE a OR b



filter(col %in% c(...))
WHERE col IN (...)



filter(!is.na(col))
WHERE col IS NOT NULL



select(a, b)
SELECT a, b FROM table



rename(new = old)
SELECT old AS new



mutate(new = expr)
SELECT ..., expr AS new



arrange(col)
ORDER BY col
ascending by default


arrange(desc(col))
ORDER BY col DESC



head(n)
LIMIT n

TOP(n) in T-SQL


group_by(col)
GROUP BY col



summarize(x = sum(y))
aggregate in SELECT

no explicit summarize keyword


inner_join(...)
INNER JOIN ... ON ...



left_join(...)
LEFT JOIN ... ON ...



Building the translation with show_query()

show_query() is the pedagogical key — it prints the SQL that dbplyr would send to the database for any pipeline. Seeing the translation side-by-side is the fastest way to internalize both.

Codeflights |>
    filter(dest == "IAH") |>
    arrange(dep_delay) |>
    show_query()

<SQL>
SELECT flights.*
FROM flights
WHERE (dest = 'IAH')
ORDER BY dep_delay



Codeflights |>
    filter(dest == "IAH") |>
    arrange(dep_delay) |>
    head(n = 10) |>
    show_query()

<SQL>
SELECT flights.*
FROM flights
WHERE (dest = 'IAH')
ORDER BY dep_delay
LIMIT 10


Three things to read off the SQL:


filter() becomes WHERE, and R’s == becomes SQL’s single =.

arrange() becomes ORDER BY; the direction defaults to ascending, and desc() maps to DESC.
dbplyr conservatively wraps the filter as a subquery (FROM (SELECT * FROM flights WHERE dest = 'IAH')), which is functionally equivalent to a flat query but makes the pipeline’s compositionality explicit.
Logical operators

Codeflights |>
    filter(dest == "IAH" & origin == "JFK") |>
    arrange(dep_delay) |>
    show_query()

<SQL>
SELECT flights.*
FROM flights
WHERE (dest = 'IAH' AND origin = 'JFK')
ORDER BY dep_delay



Codeflights |>
    filter(dest == "IAH" | origin == "JFK") |>
    arrange(year, month, day, desc(dep_delay)) |>
    show_query()

<SQL>
SELECT flights.*
FROM flights
WHERE (dest = 'IAH' OR origin = 'JFK')
ORDER BY "year", "month", "day", dep_delay DESC


& and | become AND and OR. The multi-column arrange() produces a comma-separated ORDER BY list with DESC attached per column.
Set membership and missing values

Codeflights |>
    filter(dest %in% c("IAH", "HOU")) |>
    show_query()

<SQL>
SELECT flights.*
FROM flights
WHERE (dest IN ('IAH', 'HOU'))



Codeflights |>
    filter(!is.na(dep_delay)) |>
    show_query()

<SQL>
SELECT flights.*
FROM flights
WHERE (NOT((dep_delay IS NULL)))


%in% maps to SQL’s IN (...), which is the standard way to express set membership. is.na() becomes IS NULL, and !is.na() becomes IS NOT NULL — note that SQL uses a different operator for null testing (IS) rather than equality (=), because null in SQL is the absence of a value, not a value equal to itself. This is the NULL ≠ NULL rule, and it’s the single most common source of subtle SQL bugs.
Select, rename, and mutate

Codeplanes |>
    select(tailnum, type, manufacturer, model, year) |>
    show_query()

<SQL>
SELECT tailnum, "type", manufacturer, model, "year"
FROM planes



Codeplanes |>
    select(tailnum, type, manufacturer, model, year) |>
    rename(year_built = year) |>
    show_query()

<SQL>
SELECT tailnum, "type", manufacturer, model, "year" AS year_built
FROM planes



Codeflights |>
    mutate(speed = distance / (air_time / 60)) |>
    show_query()

<SQL>
SELECT flights.*, distance / (air_time / 60.0) AS speed
FROM flights


Both rename() and mutate() produce the same underlying SQL structure: old_column AS new_name or expression AS new_column. Under the hood, renaming and computing a new variable are the same operation in SQL — the difference is only whether the right-hand side is a column reference or an expression.
Joins

Codeflights |>
    left_join(planes, by = "tailnum") |>
    show_query()

<SQL>
SELECT
  flights."year" AS "year.x",
  "month",
  "day",
  dep_time,
  sched_dep_time,
  dep_delay,
  arr_time,
  sched_arr_time,
  arr_delay,
  carrier,
  flight,
  flights.tailnum AS tailnum,
  origin,
  dest,
  air_time,
  distance,
  "hour",
  "minute",
  time_hour,
  planes."year" AS "year.y",
  "type",
  manufacturer,
  model,
  engines,
  seats,
  speed,
  engine
FROM flights
LEFT JOIN planes
  ON (flights.tailnum = planes.tailnum)


The USING (tailnum) syntax is shorthand for ON flights.tailnum = planes.tailnum. If the join key had different names in the two tables, dbplyr would emit an explicit ON clause.
Aggregation — group_by() + summarize()


Codeflights |>
    group_by(origin) |>
    summarize(
        n_flights    = n(),
        mean_dep_del = mean(dep_delay, na.rm = TRUE),
        mean_arr_del = mean(arr_delay, na.rm = TRUE)
    ) |>
    show_query()

<SQL>
SELECT
  origin,
  COUNT(*) AS n_flights,
  AVG(dep_delay) AS mean_dep_del,
  AVG(arr_delay) AS mean_arr_del
FROM flights
GROUP BY origin


Two characteristics of SQL aggregation worth naming:


There is no explicit SUMMARIZE keyword. Aggregation is implicit — any column in SELECT that isn’t in GROUP BY must be inside an aggregate function (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN, etc.). Violating this produces the classic “column must appear in GROUP BY clause or be used in aggregate function” error.

Aggregate expressions get aliased with AS. The same expr AS name pattern from mutate() applies — the aggregate column needs a name to be referred to downstream, including in ORDER BY.
Computing aggregation on the remote table

Codeflights |>
    group_by(origin) |>
    summarize(
        n_flights    = n(),
        mean_dep_del = mean(dep_delay, na.rm = TRUE)
    ) |>
    arrange(desc(n_flights)) |>
    collect()   # pull results into R

# A tibble: 3 × 3
  origin n_flights mean_dep_del
  <chr>      <dbl>        <dbl>
1 EWR       120835         15.1
2 JFK       111279         12.1
3 LGA       104662         10.3


collect() is where the query actually executes and the result comes back as an R tibble. Everything before it was lazy — composing SQL, not running it. On a large database, this distinction is what makes dbplyr workable: you can iterate on the pipeline without pulling data until you’re ready.

title: 16 — Databases and SQL
text:
Travel Stack Exchange — SQL Exercises
The following SQL queries target the Travel Stack Exchange Data Explorer (https://data.stackexchange.com/travel), which uses T-SQL (so TOP(n) works but the standard LIMIT n does not). They are not evaluated here — the Stack Exchange data lives remotely and the queries are demonstrated at the Data Explorer — but the SQL syntax is what you would paste there.
Exercise 1 — Posts with score ≥ 110
SELECT Title, Score
FROM   Posts
WHERE  PostTypeId = 1       -- restrict to questions
  AND  Score      >= 110
ORDER BY Score DESC;
No TOP() is needed because the result set is naturally small — the cutoff does the limiting. PostTypeId = 1 filters to questions (as opposed to answers, which have PostTypeId = 2).
Exercise 2 — Posts whose title mentions a place
SELECT Title, Score
FROM   Posts
WHERE  PostTypeId = 1
  AND  Title LIKE '%Rwanda%'
ORDER BY Score DESC;
LIKE with % wildcards is SQL’s substring match. %Rwanda% matches any title containing the substring “Rwanda” regardless of position. Note that LIKE is case-sensitive in some SQL dialects (PostgreSQL) and case-insensitive in others (T-SQL, MySQL default collation) — a genuine portability gotcha when moving queries across databases.
Exercise 3 — Posts with two co-occurring tags
SELECT TOP(50) Title, Score, Tags
FROM   Posts
WHERE  PostTypeId = 1
  AND  Tags LIKE '%<rwanda>%'
  AND  Tags LIKE '%<visas>%'
ORDER BY Score DESC;
The Stack Exchange schema stores tags as a single string field with <tag> delimiters (e.g., <rwanda><visas><east-africa>). To match a specific tag cleanly, use the angle-bracket form %<tag>% — otherwise a search for %visa% would also match posts tagged <visa-free> or <visa-on-arrival>. This is one of the places the Stack Exchange schema’s denormalization (multiple tags in one field instead of a separate Tags table) forces pattern-matching solutions that a properly normalized schema would avoid.
Exercise 4 — Count posts by type, ordered by frequency
SELECT
    PostTypeId,
    COUNT(Id) AS numPosts
FROM   Posts
GROUP BY PostTypeId
ORDER BY numPosts DESC;
The aggregate column alias numPosts is what makes it possible to ORDER BY numPosts — referring to the aggregate by the expression COUNT(Id) works in some dialects but not all. Naming it once and referring to it by name is the portable pattern.
Exercise 5 — Most common tagsets
SELECT TOP(20)
    Tags,
    COUNT(Tags) AS numTagsets
FROM   Posts
WHERE  PostTypeId = 1
GROUP BY Tags
ORDER BY numTagsets DESC;
This counts occurrences of exact tagset strings (<rwanda><visas> is different from <rwanda><visas><east-africa>) rather than individual tag frequencies. Individual-tag counts would require splitting the Tags field apart, which in T-SQL would involve STRING_SPLIT() or a recursive CTE — considerably more complex.
Exercise 6 — Join top comments with user info
SELECT TOP(100)
    c.Text,
    c.Score,
    u.DisplayName,
    u.Reputation
FROM   Comments c
JOIN   Users    u  ON c.UserId = u.Id
ORDER BY c.Score DESC;
Three pieces of syntax worth naming:


Comments c and Users u are table aliases — within this query, c refers to Comments and u to Users. Qualifying columns with c. and u. prevents ambiguity when both tables have a column of the same name.

JOIN without INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, or FULL defaults to INNER JOIN — only rows present in both tables are returned. Comments whose UserId doesn’t match a user (deleted accounts, for example) are dropped.

ON c.UserId = u.Id specifies the join predicate. The column names differ across tables here — UserId in Comments, Id in Users — so the ON form is required rather than the shorthand USING(col).

title: 16 — Databases and SQL
text:
Closing the Connection

CodedbDisconnect(con, shutdown = TRUE)


Always disconnect when finished. For in-memory DuckDB this is low-stakes, but the same pattern applies to networked databases where an unclosed connection can hold a connection-pool slot open and eventually exhaust the server.

title: 16 — Databases and SQL
text:
Reflection
SQL and dplyr describe the same shape of operation with different syntax conventions. filter() + select() + arrange() + head() is WHERE + SELECT + ORDER BY + LIMIT. group_by() + summarize() is GROUP BY + aggregate-in-SELECT. *_join() is JOIN ... ON. The core conceptual vocabulary of data manipulation — select rows, pick columns, compute new values, group, aggregate, combine tables — is the same across both. What differs is where the work happens: dplyr materializes every intermediate step into an R object in memory; SQL composes a single query and ships it to the database, which optimizes execution across the whole plan before returning anything. For data that fits in RAM, this difference is cosmetic. For data that doesn’t, SQL is the only viable path.
show_query() is the best teacher I’ve found for this material. Rather than memorizing translation tables, the practical way to build SQL fluency is to write what you want in dplyr, see the SQL it produces, and adjust. After a few iterations, you internalize the mapping without consciously consulting it. This is the same pedagogical move as inspecting compiled code to learn the language underneath — in this case the language underneath is what every database in your future professional life speaks.
The NULL ≠ NULL rule is the single trap worth pinning. In SQL, NULL is the absence of a value, not a value that happens to equal other NULLs. Therefore WHERE x = NULL matches no rows, ever — you have to write WHERE x IS NULL. This is a semantic decision (null is “unknown,” and two unknowns cannot be asserted equal) that trips up almost every new SQL user. The R is.na() / !is.na() → SQL IS NULL / IS NOT NULL mapping handled by dbplyr hides this, but the moment you write raw SQL by hand, you meet the rule directly.
