title: 17 — Effective Visualization
section: 🔥 Data Story Critique — What Africa will look like in 100 years (The Telegraph, March 2016)
text:
🔥 Data Story Critique — What Africa will look like in 100 years (The Telegraph, March 2016)
Source: http://s.telegraph.co.uk/graphics/projects/Africa-in-100-years/index.html
Author: Ashley Kirk, Telegraph data journalist.
What is the data story?
The piece takes UN population projections — Africa reaching 4.4 billion by 2100, a roughly fourfold increase — and builds a continent-scale prognostic narrative around the challenges and opportunities of that growth. Chapters move through urbanization (projecting 56.5% urban-dweller share by 2050), housing and slums (with a profile of Lydia, a young woman from Nairobi’s Kibera), public health (HIV, malaria, maternal mortality), conflict (Boko Haram, South Sudan, the Fragile States Index), education (primary-school enrollment, illiteracy projections), and life expectancy. Each chapter interleaves data charts with expert quotes — primarily from UN agencies (Joan Clos, Dr Davison Munodawafa), UK government bodies (Justine Greening, DFID figures), and Western-linked institutions (Barclays Africa, Africa Educational Trust, The Borgen Project). The piece closes on a qualified note of opportunity: African urbanization is a late-starting transition that could embrace “new urban paradigms,” provided it is accompanied by international support and better data.
What is effective?
The underlying demographic data is solid and the projection is important to communicate. UN population projections showing Africa’s quadrupling by 2100 are a consequential fact that most readers have not internalized. Getting this number into broadsheet readership with the accompanying structural implications (working-age cohort composition, urbanization pace, health-system strain) is a legitimate journalistic contribution.
Interleaving a named first-person testimony — Lydia from Kibera — gives the abstract demographic projection a texture that pure charts cannot. Whatever the problems with how her voice is positioned (see below), having an African woman speak in her own words at substantial length is above the norm for this genre and register.
Naming specific success cases alongside the challenges. Burundi’s doubling of primary-school enrollment between 2000 and 2010 and Mozambique’s 35-percentage-point gain in the same window are given explicit space. This is not a piece that argues every African country is on the same trajectory; it at least gestures at heterogeneity in outcomes.
The closing framing borrows from demographic-dividend thinking. “Given the comparatively late onset of the urban transition in Africa, there exist real opportunities for embracing new urban paradigms” is drawn from the scholarly literature that treats Africa’s youth-heavy demographic profile as an asset if paired with right policy. That framing appears only at the end and is not developed, but its presence at all is worth noting.
What could be improved?
The piece operates in an external-observation register that flattens Africa’s analytical autonomy. The entire document reads as the Telegraph looking at Africa and reporting on its future — not as Africans authoring their own trajectory. No comparable Telegraph feature would ask “What will America look like in 100 years?” in the same anxious-prognostic register, despite America’s demographic shifts being similarly substantial. The genre convention this piece sits inside — broadsheet European development journalism — has a long history going back to colonial-era reportage, and the piece does not distance itself from that inheritance. A reader learns what the world should worry about regarding Africa, not what Africans are doing about Africa.
“Africa” is treated as a single analytical object when the data does not support aggregation at that level. The continent contains 54 states with radically different state capacities, resource profiles, governance configurations, colonial-inheritance structures, and demographic trajectories. Rwanda and DRC are not one story. Botswana and Zimbabwe are not one story. Tunisia and Sudan are not one story. The piece moves between “Africa” generalizations (“Africa’s poor health is linked to its people’s poverty”) and country-specific data points as if the two scales are interchangeable — this is the same analytical error as treating “Europe” as a single object when discussing Greek debt. The charting compounds this: “Continent populations” plots Africa as a single line against Asia, Europe, LAC, and North America, baking the aggregation into the visual grammar.
Causal framing is absent where it is most needed. Poverty, conflict, low education, weak health systems are presented as conditions of the continent, not as outcomes of identifiable historical and structural processes. The piece does not name colonial boundary-drawing (every African state border was externally imposed within two lifetimes), structural adjustment programs of the 1980s–90s that systematically defunded African health and education under IMF/World Bank conditionalities, extractive trade arrangements that still favor raw-commodity exports, or capital flight — the African Development Bank and others have estimated net illicit financial outflows from Africa at magnitudes exceeding inward aid. By presenting effects without causes, the piece frames African poverty as something to be alleviated by intervention rather than as something produced by interaction with the global economic order. This is the single most serious framing problem in the piece.
The epistemic hierarchy between who gets to analyze and who gets to illustrate is stark. Analytical authority speaking about Africa in the piece: UN Under-Secretary-General Joan Clos, the UK’s International Development Minister Justine Greening, the UK’s Department for International Development, a World Bank lead economist, the CEO of Barclays Africa, the executive director of the Africa Educational Trust (UK-based), the Borgen Project (US-based). The Kenyan voices quoted are Lydia (Kibera resident, positioned as experiential) and Dr Munodawafa (WHO programme manager, speaking within the international-institutional frame). No African economist, African demographer, African political scientist, or African development scholar is quoted as an analyst of Africa’s future. This is a systematic editorial pattern. The Telegraph has the resources to reach African scholars. It didn’t.
Lydia’s voice is selected to confirm the narrative, not to challenge it. Her childhood memory — “I would make dolls from thread and plastic bags” — is the conventional slum-poverty aesthetic, poverty-as-innocence in visual grammar. When she offers analysis, it aligns with Western development-NGO orthodoxy: skills training, self-employment. What’s absent is any critique by her of the structural conditions — land tenure in Nairobi, urban-planning decisions that created Kibera’s informal status, Kenyan state response to slum dwellers — that a resident of Kibera could offer and that would contradict the piece’s frame of “opportunity combined with right government intervention.” A meaningful inclusion of a slum resident’s voice would let her analyze, not just feel.
The “data-collection flaws in Africa” disclaimer papers over a deeper epistemological problem. The piece notes that “there are flaws in data collection and publication processes in Africa” — framing African data infrastructure as deficient — and then proceeds to trust aggregate figures to drive the narrative. What it doesn’t name is that much of the data about Africa is produced by non-African institutions (UN, World Bank, IMF, Western-based indices like the Fragile States Index from the Fund for Peace) whose methodological choices and political-economic interests shape what gets measured and how. “African data” in this piece is often “data about Africa produced by people who are not African,” and the gatekeeping that entails is invisible.
Visual grammar is dominated by crisis imagery. The photographs in the piece are dominated by Ebola signage, rubbish offloading, military patrols, armed security. A piece that wanted to center the “opportunity” framing would have chosen images that aren’t exclusively drawn from the crisis aesthetic — images of African urban innovation, African scholars, African science, African economic production. The ratio of crisis imagery to opportunity imagery tells the reader what the piece actually thinks Africa is.
Proportionality. This is a serious broadsheet feature by a competent data journalist using real UN data. The problems above are not technical deficits but framing choices, and the framing is specifically the inherited British-development-journalism frame that Africa-studies scholars have critiqued for decades. Naming these problems is fair because this piece is positioned as authoritative public communication, not as student work. A 2026 version of this piece — with African analytical voices centered, causal framing acknowledged, the aggregation of “Africa” disciplined — would be a meaningfully different document.

title: 17 — Effective Visualization
text:
Exercise — Present Your Visual
This is a scaffold for presenting visualizations from my course project. The presentation walks the audience through four stages — motivation, orientation, highlight, big picture — and applies the six guidelines from Mastering Software Development in R.
Motivation & Context
The research question my project addresses is [fill in: the specific question this visual is answering]. It matters because [fill in: why the question has stakes — who is affected by the answer, what decision or understanding is downstream]. The audience for this presentation is [fill in: course reviewers / peers / an imagined external reader], which shapes both what prior context I can assume and what level of terminology I should use.
Orientation
Before the audience can interpret the visual, they need the following orientation: [fill in: x-axis meaning and units, y-axis meaning and units, colour legend, any filters or subsets applied to the data, time window and geographic scope, what each point/line/bar represents]. The specific data decisions that shaped this visual — what was included, what was excluded, what transformations were applied — are [fill in].
Highlight
The most important single data point or pattern the visual shows is [fill in the specific observation]. It matters because [fill in the interpretive consequence]. The visual design choices that make this highlight legible are [fill in: the colour that isolates it, the annotation that labels it, the reference line it sits against, the ordering that foregrounds it].
Big Picture
The overall comparison the visual invites is [fill in: A vs B, trend over time, distribution across groups]. The takeaway is [fill in]. The implication for the project’s larger argument is [fill in].
Applying the Six Guidelines






Guideline
How my visual implements it



Aim for high data density
[fill in]


Use clear, meaningful labels
[fill in]


Provide useful references
[fill in: reference lines, benchmark values, comparison groups]


Highlight interesting aspects
[fill in: colour, annotation, subset-emphasis]


Consider small multiples
[fill in: did I break comparison into facets? why/why not?]


Make order meaningful
[fill in: sorted bars by value, chronological lines, ordinal axis]



The overarching test: what comparison am I making, and have I made it as easy as possible for the viewer to perform that comparison? If the viewer has to work to see what the visual is arguing, the visual is still rhetoric waiting to happen.

title: 17 — Effective Visualization
text:
Exercise — Human-Centered Data Science
What the Dear Abby letters reveal that no dataset would
The Pudding’s 30 Years of American Anxieties works with the text of letters to an advice column — and the letters themselves do analytical work that a tabular dataset cannot do. Five kinds of context that are present in letters and absent from aggregated survey data:
Temporal-emotional register. A letter written at 2 a.m. after a fight with a spouse has a different tone and content than the same person’s answer to a survey question about “marital satisfaction” six weeks later in a calmer moment. The letter captures the state; the survey captures the summary. Aggregated data cannot reconstruct the states that produced it.
Relational and circumstantial context. Letters embed their subject matter in explanation — “I have been married forty years, have eight children, am sixty and feel eighty-five” (from a 1985 letter in the dataset). This is the full scaffold of a life situation. A survey variable “age = 60” flattens it entirely.
Social norms that govern what people can say aloud. What letter-writers worry about, what they assume the advice columnist and her public audience will find reasonable, and what they frame as shameful or forbidden all reveal the prevailing moral framework of the decade more precisely than any explicit measure of “values.” The letters document norms by performing within them.
The selection and gatekeeping of what becomes data. Not all letters are published. Editorial choices by the columnist and newspaper determine which anxieties become visible and which stay private. The dataset The Pudding worked with is already a filtered artefact — the worries of people who wrote in, as curated by editors for public readership.
Language itself as context. Word choice, formality, structure, length — the same underlying problem articulated in 1985 uses different vocabulary, different euphemisms, different appeals to authority than the same problem articulated in 2017. Computational text analysis can surface these shifts, but only because the texts carry them; a numeric survey could not.
Hidden context in my project’s dataset
The questions below are scaffolded for reflection on my course project dataset; I complete them in context of the project write-up.


Who collected this data and why? [fill in: the producing institution, the institutional purpose that motivated the collection, the audience the data was produced for]


What might have been the collectors’ agenda, and how might that have shaped what is in the data? [fill in: what interests the producer had in measuring certain outcomes and not others]


What cases are present and absent? [fill in: eligibility criteria, sampling frame, non-response, censoring, selection-into-observation dynamics]


What variables are available, and in what format? [fill in: what gets binned into categories, which categories are treated as ordered/unordered, which continuous quantities are recorded at what precision]


Whose labor is visible in the data, and whose is invisible? [fill in: the visible producers — named authors, named agencies — and the invisible producers — enumerators, data entry workers, domain-expert respondents, households providing unpaid information]


What additional information would give a more full picture of the lives connected to this data? [fill in: qualitative interviews, administrative texture, historical records, first-person testimony, community-held knowledge]


The move this exercise is asking for — treating the dataset as a produced artefact rather than as a natural object — is the move that separates descriptive data work from accountable data work. A dataset is not the thing itself; it is a specific institutional choice about what counted as worth recording.

title: 17 — Effective Visualization
text:
Exercise — Project in 3 Visuals
If the digital deliverable could only show three visuals, the decision rule is: what three views together answer the research question with the least redundancy and the most interpretive coverage? The framework below is what I use to choose.
Candidate roles for the three visuals
Visual 1 — Context / Scale. Establishes the problem. Answers: what is the magnitude of the phenomenon? how does it compare across time, place, or groups? This visual earns its space by making the reader care before any analysis happens.
Visual 2 — Structure / Mechanism. Shows the internal shape of the phenomenon. Answers: what is the pattern behind the headline number? what is the distribution, the relationship between key variables, the within-group variation? This is where the analytical work lives.
Visual 3 — Implication / Comparison. Drives toward the project’s argument. Answers: what does this mean? what is the comparison that carries the interpretive payoff? what should the reader take away? This visual should be the one the reader remembers.
For my project
The candidate list, ordering, and combination decisions below are scaffolded; I complete them with project specifics.


Visual 1 (Context): [fill in: the chart type and data behind it — e.g., a time series showing the trend, a map showing the geographic distribution, a bar chart showing comparative scale]


Visual 2 (Structure): [fill in]


Visual 3 (Implication): [fill in]


Ordering. The default sequence is Context → Structure → Implication, which moves the reader from “what are we looking at” through “what is happening inside it” to “what should we conclude.” Sometimes inverting — opening on the implication and then building back — works if the implication is counterintuitive enough to function as a hook.
Combining. Two visuals can sometimes fuse into one without losing analytical content: a small-multiples panel where each facet answers a different question, a dual-axis chart that pairs magnitude with rate, a map with an inset time series. The test for whether combining works: does the combined figure still have one clear reading order, or does it fragment the reader’s attention?

title: 17 — Effective Visualization
text:
Reflection
Effective visualization is a chain from question → data → decisions → audience. Every one of the six guidelines — data density, clear labels, useful references, highlights, small multiples, meaningful order — is really a question about the audience’s cognition: what will they pay attention to, what do they already know, what comparison do they need to perform, what would make that comparison easier? When any visualization feels wrong, the diagnosis almost always traces back to a mismatch at one of these links. The chart has insufficient context because the decision about what the audience needs was unexamined; the highlight is buried because the designer hadn’t decided which comparison was load-bearing.
Human-centered data science is what stops the chain from running on autopilot. The Dear Abby letters exercise is a compact version of the broader discipline: treat the data as a produced artefact, ask who produced it and why, ask whose labor is invisible, ask what additional context a complete picture would require. A data scientist who skips these questions can still make technically competent visualizations — but those visualizations will carry forward whatever biases, omissions, and silences were already embedded in the data. The technical discipline and the human-centered discipline are not separate skills; the first is an application of the second.
The Africa-in-100-years critique earlier in this document is, at the end of the day, a failure of human-centered practice. The piece used real UN data and produced charts that are technically fine. What it did not do is ask the human-centered questions: whose analysis is this? who gets to speak about this? what causal framing have I inherited from my editorial tradition, and is it the right one? The consequence is a piece that describes Africa to an external audience rather than letting Africa’s people and scholars author their own representation. Every chart in that piece could be re-plotted with the same data and produce an entirely different document, once the human-centered questions were answered differently.

title: 18 — Interactive Visualization: Shiny and plotly

Setup

title: 18 — Interactive Visualization: Shiny and plotly
section: 🔥 Data Story Critique — SDG Index Dashboards (SDSN / Bertelsmann Stiftung)
text:
🔥 Data Story Critique — SDG Index Dashboards (SDSN / Bertelsmann Stiftung)
Source: https://dashboards.sdgindex.org/map/goals/SDG4/
Producers: Sustainable Development Solutions Network (SDSN) and Bertelsmann Stiftung, with SDSN under Jeffrey Sachs at Columbia. The flagship annual product is the Sustainable Development Report (previously the SDG Index and Dashboards Report); the dashboard is its interactive companion.
What is the data story?
The dashboard is the best-known non-UN monitoring instrument for the Sustainable Development Goals — a global choropleth framework that scores all 193 UN member states across the 17 SDGs plus an overall composite index. The linked URL drops the reader directly into the SDG 4 (Quality Education) map view: a world choropleth in four colours (green / yellow / orange / red, plus grey for “data not available”), a trend arrow per country indicating whether progress is on-track / improving / stagnating / decreasing, and a sidebar panel exposing the indicator composition (primary completion, secondary completion, literacy rate, tertiary attainment, etc.). Each country page drills into the contributing indicators and their most recent values. The same pattern holds across all 17 goals. The story it tells, across the SDG system as a whole, is where the world stands — which countries are achieving, which are struggling, where the trend is bending in the right direction, and where it is stagnating with four years left on the 2030 deadline.
What is effective?
Choropleth is the correct first view for a problem of this dimensionality. The underlying data cube is 17 goals × 193 countries × 230+ indicators × 2015–current timeline. Any single view collapses several of those dimensions; a world map with colour-coded countries collapses “which country, at what level, on which goal” into a two-second scan. The reader can locate themselves, locate countries they care about, and see the overall spatial pattern without reading a table. A table of 193 rows or a 17×193 heatmap would fail this test.
The dual-encoding of level (colour) and trend (arrow) is a genuinely good design decision. These are two orthogonal pieces of information — where a country currently stands, and which direction it is moving — and using a single channel would lose one of them. The encoding surfaces the Green-but-stalling and Red-but-improving cases that single-dimension visualizations tend to blur. Ethiopia on SDG 4 is the classic example of the latter profile (low score, strong improvement trend); showing both avoids a misread in either direction.
Methodology transparency is higher than most comparable products. SDSN publishes a detailed methodology chapter with the Annual Sustainable Development Report that documents every indicator’s normalization formula, the handling of missing values, confidence bounds, and the inclusion criteria. The SDG Transformation Center hosts downloadable data and a full technical note. Most public users will not audit this, but users who want to can, which is the appropriate institutional posture.
Indicator composition is exposed per goal. Clicking into SDG 4 shows which indicators combine to produce the goal score, with weights, normalization bounds, and the year of the most recent data point. This is more than many composite indices offer — the World Bank’s Human Capital Index and similar composites frequently do not expose weights publicly — and it is the right default for an instrument that will be cited in policy debates.
What could be improved?
The composite structure masks failures through arithmetic. The overall SDG Index is the arithmetic mean of 17 goal scores; each goal score is the mean of its indicators. Flat averaging has well-known problems — a country that scores at ceiling on SDG 8 (economic growth) effectively earns credit that offsets a failure on SDG 13 (climate action), because both enter the mean with equal weight. This is the canonical Sen–Stiglitz critique of composite indices, and the design choice to use arithmetic mean rather than a penalty function like geometric mean (which is more sensitive to low scores) is a methodological decision that shapes every ranking downstream. It is defensible, but it belongs upfront in the dashboard UI, not buried in the methodology paper. A reader looking at a country’s “overall progress” has no way to see, from the map view, whether that score is an even average or an aggregate that hides a failing component.
Country-level aggregation is blind to within-country inequality — which is exactly what “development” as a concept is supposed to track. The US national score on SDG 3 (health) hides a Black–white life-expectancy gap of several years. China’s SDG 1 score hides the material differences between Xinjiang and Shenzhen. India’s SDG 4 score hides the order-of-magnitude gap between Kerala and Bihar education systems. The dashboard displays the who of national averages, while the animating moral question of the SDG framework is the distribution beneath those averages. SDG 10 (Reduce Inequality) exists as a goal, which partly addresses this — but framing inequality as “one of 17 goals” rather than as a cross-cutting dimension of every other goal is itself a framework-level decision that the dashboard inherits from the UN SDG agenda and does not interrogate. A more ambitious dashboard would surface sub-national data where it exists (the Human Development Report does this for some indicators).
Data lag is invisible in the current-status UI. Many SDG 4 indicators in low-income countries have latest available data from DHS/MICS surveys conducted in 2015–2019. The dashboard presents scores as current state without displaying per-indicator data years in the hover tooltip. A reader in 2026 sees a country coloured orange and assumes the colour reflects 2025 reality; it may reflect the most recent available observation from 2018. A per-indicator data-year label in the tooltip, or a transparency channel encoding recency on the map, would be a low-cost honesty fix.
Assessment against SDG targets naturalizes a political artefact. The 2030 deadline and the specific numerical targets (“by 2030, ensure all girls and boys complete free, equitable, and quality primary and secondary education”) are the outputs of a 2015 UN negotiation — political compromises among member states, not independent scientific benchmarks. The dashboard translates distance-from-target into technical-looking assessment language (“on track,” “challenges remain”) that launders the political origin of the targets themselves. A country rated “on track” on SDG 4 is on track relative to a politically negotiated target; whether that target is adequate is a separate question that the dashboard’s framing forecloses. As 2030 approaches and most goals are visibly missing their targets, the dashboard does not have a framework for asking whether the targets themselves need to be revised — it just continues scoring against them.
The grey “Not Available” countries carry political information the UI flattens. North Korea, Somalia, Eritrea, parts of conflict-affected Sub-Saharan Africa — the countries where data is most absent are not randomly distributed. They are the countries where state capacity is weakest, where conflict has disrupted statistical infrastructure, or where governments have chosen not to participate in international reporting regimes. These are frequently also the places where development outcomes are most consequential. Rendering them as grey is an honest acknowledgement of the data gap, but it positions them as absent from the conversation rather than as cases where the absence of data is itself a finding. A dashboard committed to equity framing would name this explicitly: the countries most in need of developmental attention are also the countries least visible in the global measurement apparatus.
The interactive-vs-narrative trade-off has tipped the wrong way for the dashboard’s most common users. The Annual Sustainable Development Report PDF provides narrative context — structural drivers of progress or regression, cross-cutting analysis, country case studies — that the dashboard strips away in favour of numbers and colours. Policy users who treat the dashboard as their primary reference (which increasingly they do — UN agency briefings, government strategy documents, and media reports cite SDG Index rankings directly) are consuming interpretation-free data. The pre-class reading for this ICA (the Medium piece on “the death of interactive infographics”) makes this exact point about interactive journalism: most users don’t actually explore; they want guided interpretation. SDSN has that interpretation in the report. The dashboard doesn’t import enough of it.
Proportionality. SDSN / Bertelsmann produce one of the most methodologically serious composite-index products in the field, and the critique above is not about analytical capacity. It is about editorial choices specific to the dashboard medium — composition opacity, within-country blindness, data-lag invisibility, target naturalization, grey-country invisibility, stripped narrative — that the annual PDF report either addresses or has room to address. The drift the dashboard is under is that it is being cited as an authoritative source in contexts where the caveats that live in the report are invisible. That drift is the institutional problem worth naming.

title: 18 — Interactive Visualization: Shiny and plotly
section: Reflection — Kyle Walker’s Neighborhood Diversity App
text:
Reflection — Kyle Walker’s Neighborhood Diversity App
The app’s core layout is a sidebar-main split: sidebar holds user inputs (city dropdown, span slider), main holds three linked outputs — a scatterplot of diversity score versus distance to city hall, a choropleth map of diversity by census tract, and a stacked bar of the city’s overall racial composition. Selecting a different city updates all three plots simultaneously; adjusting the span slider re-smooths the scatterplot without touching the other two.
Is the interactivity necessary? Yes, and for a specific reason: the analytical question here is how diversity varies within and across US metros, and there are ~70 metros of interest. A static panel would need roughly seventy small-multiple scatterplots plus seventy maps plus seventy bars — well over two hundred subplots — which would defeat comparison. The interactive version lets the reader perform the comparison one metro at a time and choose which comparisons matter to them. This is the canonical case where interactivity earns its place: many possible slices, only a few of interest to any given reader, and no single static view can serve them all.
What comparisons would I want that the app doesn’t support? A side-by-side view of two metros would be the first addition — right now the reader switches city and loses the previous view, forcing them to hold the previous metro in working memory. A time dimension (the data_by_year dataset suggests this is in the data but not in this app view) would let the reader see whether a metro’s diversity profile is changing. And a brush-and-link between the map and the scatterplot — clicking a census tract on the map highlights its point on the scatter, or vice versa — would make the distance-to-city-hall story legible per-tract, not just in aggregate.
crumbs: 53  18 — Interactive Visualization: Shiny and plotly

title: 18 — Interactive Visualization: Shiny and plotly
text:
The Shiny App Structure
A Shiny app has three logical components:


ui — what the user sees. Nested functions for page layout, input controls, and output placeholders.

server — what the app does in response to user input. A function that takes input and output objects and defines render*() functions that tie them together.

shinyApp(ui, server) — launches the app.

The design contract between ui and server is that every inputId in the ui becomes input$that_id in the server, and every outputId in the ui is populated by assignment to output$that_id in the server. Get the IDs right and the data flows; get them wrong and the app runs but shows nothing.
Complete app.R for the neighborhood-diversity app
The following is what would go in neighborhood_diversity/app.R. It is not evaluated in this QMD — Shiny requires a live R process, not a rendered HTML — but running shiny::runApp("neighborhood_diversity/") in the RStudio console will launch it. The two data files (diverse_data_by_dist.rds and diverse_data_by_year.csv) need to sit in neighborhood_diversity/data/.
# neighborhood_diversity/app.R
library(shiny)
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(plotly)

# ---- Data ----
data_by_dist <- read_rds("data/diverse_data_by_dist.rds")
data_by_year <- read_csv("data/diverse_data_by_year.csv")

metro_names <- data_by_dist |> pull(metro_name) |> unique() |> sort()

# ---- UI ----
ui <- fluidPage(
    titlePanel("Neighborhood Diversity Across US Metros"),

    sidebarLayout(
        sidebarPanel(
            selectInput(
                inputId  = "metro",
                label    = "Metropolitan area:",
                choices  = metro_names,
                selected = "Minneapolis-St. Paul-Bloomington, MN-WI"
            ),
            sliderInput(
                inputId = "span",
                label   = "Smoothing span:",
                min     = 0.1,
                max     = 1.0,
                value   = 0.5,
                step    = 0.1
            )
        ),

        mainPanel(
            plotlyOutput("scatter_plot"),
            plotOutput("map_plot"),
            plotlyOutput("bar_plot")
        )
    )
)

# ---- Server ----
server <- function(input, output) {

    # Reactive: city-filtered dataset, re-computed whenever `metro` changes
    metro_data <- reactive({
        data_by_dist |> filter(metro_name == input$metro)
    })

    # Scatterplot — diversity entropy vs. distance to city hall
    output$scatter_plot <- renderPlotly({
        p <- ggplot(metro_data(), aes(x = distmiles, y = entropy)) +
            geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
            geom_smooth(span = input$span, se = FALSE, colour = "#b6345c") +
            labs(
                x = "Distance to city hall (miles)",
                y = "Diversity entropy",
                title = str_glue("Diversity vs. distance — {input$metro}")
            ) +
            theme_minimal()
        ggplotly(p)
    })

    # Map — choropleth of entropy by census tract
    output$map_plot <- renderPlot({
        ggplot(metro_data()) +
            geom_sf(aes(fill = entropy), colour = NA) +
            scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma") +
            labs(fill = "Entropy",
                 title = str_glue("Diversity by tract — {input$metro}")) +
            theme_void()
    })

    # Bar chart — total population by race in the selected metro
    output$bar_plot <- renderPlotly({
        race_totals <- metro_data() |>
            st_drop_geometry() |>
            summarise(across(c(aian, asian, black, hispanic,
                               two_or_more, white), sum, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
            pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "race", values_to = "n") |>
            arrange(desc(n))

        p <- ggplot(race_totals, aes(x = fct_reorder(race, n), y = n)) +
            geom_col(fill = "#3b7a9b") +
            coord_flip() +
            labs(x = NULL, y = "Population",
                 title = str_glue("Racial composition — {input$metro}")) +
            theme_minimal()
        ggplotly(p)
    })
}

# ---- Launch ----
shinyApp(ui, server)
Annotated design choices


reactive({ ... }) for the filtered dataset. The filtering happens once per metro change, and all three plots read from the same reactive. Without it, every plot would re-run the filter independently, and a slow filter step would get executed three times per interaction.

plotlyOutput() and renderPlotly() for the scatter and bar, but plotOutput() and renderPlot() for the map. The scatter and bar benefit substantially from hover tooltips and zoom — plotly adds those for free on top of ggplot. The sf map is more finicky under ggplotly() (coordinate reference systems don’t always survive the translation); keeping it as a static ggplot is the safer choice.

sort() on the metro names. The dropdown gets long; sorting alphabetically is a user-comfort decision.

selected = ... picks a default that makes the app useful on first load (Minneapolis–St. Paul — and if I’m honest, a small nod to the Twin Cities as home to both Macalester and Saint Paul).
Publishing to shinyapps.io

Once the app runs locally, deployment follows three steps:

Install rsconnect, register at shinyapps.io, and paste the connection token into the R console.
From RStudio, open app.R and click the blue “Publish” icon in the top-right of the source pane.
Confirm the files to include (everything in the neighborhood_diversity/ folder, including the data/ subdirectory) and wait for the first-time deploy to complete.

The free tier allows five apps with 25 active hours per month — enough for coursework and portfolio pieces but not for production traffic.
crumbs: 53  18 — Interactive Visualization: Shiny and plotly

title: 18 — Interactive Visualization: Shiny and plotly
text:
plotly — Static ggplot to Interactive
plotly::ggplotly() is the lowest-effort path from a static ggplot to an interactive widget. The ggplot stays the same; the wrapper does the translation.
Line chart — variants of “Brianna” over time

Codebnames <- babynames |>
    filter(name %in% c("Brianna", "Briana", "Breanna"))

p_bnames <- ggplot(bnames, aes(x = year, y = prop, colour = sex, linetype = name)) +
    geom_line() +
    labs(
        x = "Year", y = "Proportion of births",
        colour = "Sex", linetype = "Name",
        title = "Three spellings of Brianna, 1880–2017"
    ) +
    theme_classic()

ggplotly(p_bnames)





Hovering over any line reveals the exact year, proportion, and which spelling/sex combination you are on — information that a static chart could only convey with manual annotation per point.
Animation — Gapminder

Codep_gap <- ggplot(gapminder,
                aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp,
                    colour = continent, size = pop)) +
    geom_point(aes(frame = year, ids = country), alpha = 0.7) +
    scale_x_log10() +
    scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
    labs(
        x = "GDP per capita (log scale)",
        y = "Life expectancy (years)",
        colour = "Continent", size = "Population",
        title = "Life expectancy vs. GDP per capita, 1952–2007"
    ) +
    theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p_gap)





Two aesthetic arguments carry the animation:


frame = year tells plotly that each year is a separate frame in a temporal sequence. A play button appears, and a year slider underneath.

ids = country tells plotly that the same point across frames should be treated as the same object. Without ids, points appear and disappear between frames; with ids, they slide smoothly, preserving object constancy and making longitudinal trajectories legible.

Together these make the animation far more informative than a series of snapshots: the reader can see a country move through the state space, which is the analytic quantity of interest in the Gapminder / Hans Rosling tradition.
crumbs: 53  18 — Interactive Visualization: Shiny and plotly

title: 18 — Interactive Visualization: Shiny and plotly
section: When to Use Interactive vs. Static
text:
When to Use Interactive vs. Static
The pre-class reading (the Medium piece on the death of interactive infographics) is overstated in places but correct on its central claim: most readers do not explore. Evidence from page-level analytics at several major publishers shows median interaction time on interactive infographics measured in seconds, not minutes. If the piece’s argument depends on the reader exploring to find the story, most readers will walk away with the default view.
A working rubric I apply when deciding between static and interactive:


Static wins when there is one primary comparison, the argument can be made in two or three views, and the readership is general. A static chart forces the designer to choose the one story they are telling; the reader gets that story or none at all, but the choice is honest.

Interactive wins when the analytical question genuinely requires the reader to choose their own cross-section — many cities, many industries, many countries — and the reader has a specific interest that the designer cannot anticipate. The neighborhood-diversity app is the canonical good case: no static version could serve a reader interested in Houston, Chicago, and Seattle equally well.

Both lose when the designer uses interactivity to avoid making editorial choices (“let the reader decide”). Interactivity as editorial abdication produces products that feel rich and communicate nothing.

The SDG Index Dashboard critiqued at the top of this document is partly in the third category — it defaults to a view with no guided narrative, and most readers leave the dashboard having scanned colours without registering any specific finding. The SDSN’s own annual PDF report handles the same data with far better interpretive lift. The dashboard is not wrong to exist; it is wrong to be treated as a replacement for the report.
crumbs: 53  18 — Interactive Visualization: Shiny and plotly

title: 18 — Interactive Visualization: Shiny and plotly
text:
Reflection
Shiny’s architectural insight is that ui and server are the same thing described from two angles. The ui says “there is an input called metro” and “there will be a plot called scatter_plot”; the server says “when something reads input$metro, give it the current value” and “when output$scatter_plot is accessed, run this ggplot code.” The IDs are the contract between the two sides. Most of the hard-to-debug Shiny errors are contract violations — a typo in an inputId, a plotOutput paired with a renderPlotly, a reactive expression that depends on an input not actually in the UI. Getting the contract right is more of a design discipline than a coding skill, and it’s the thing that transfers between dashboarding tools (Shiny, Dash, Streamlit) more than any specific syntax does.
plotly is the easy on-ramp; Shiny is where the real architecture lives. ggplotly() on a ggplot takes one line and adds tooltip/zoom/pan for free; for most cases where a reader wants “what’s the exact value of this point” the translation is sufficient. Shiny is a larger commitment — separate files, reactivity, deployment infrastructure — and its payoff is proportional to how much branching interaction the analysis requires. A one-slider-one-plot tool can live in a plotly widget embedded in a static HTML; a many-input, many-linked-view application is where Shiny’s architectural choices start to earn their complexity.
Interactive design is editorial discipline by another name. The temptation with interactive tools is to expose every choice to the reader — every filter, every toggle, every view — under the banner of “letting them explore.” What this actually produces, most of the time, is a reader who hits the default view, doesn’t know where to start, and leaves. The best interactive work is still heavily edited: a curated set of views, a clear default state, a guided narrative path that a reader can follow without any interaction, and then optional depth for the reader who wants it. The SDG Index Dashboard would be a sharper instrument with a tenth of its current interactivity and ten times its current narrative framing. The same applies to most dashboards. Interactivity is not a substitute for editorial thinking — it is an amplifier of the quality of the editorial thinking behind it.

title: 19 — Quality Control: Code and Data Hygiene
section: 🔥 Data Story Critique — Counting Happiness and Where it Comes From (FlowingData, 2021)
text:
🔥 Data Story Critique — Counting Happiness and Where it Comes From (FlowingData, 2021)
Source: https://flowingdata.com/2021/07/29/counting-happiness/
Author: Nathan Yau.
Note on repeated observation. This is the second Nathan Yau piece we have critiqued — the first being Seeing How Much We Ate Over the Years in ICA 9. The critique here is calibrated against that prior observation, treating this piece as an iteration in the same body of work rather than as a standalone artefact. That framing makes some improvements and some regressions visible that would not be visible from a single reading.
What is the data story?
The underlying dataset is HappyDB (Asai et al., LREC 2018), a corpus of ~100,000 “happy moments” collected from ~10,000 Amazon Mechanical Turk workers who were asked to list ten things that recently made them happy. Yau’s analytical move is to parse each sentence into a subject-verb-object triple (“I watched a good movie yesterday” → I / watched / movie), and then to tabulate the most common SVO combinations. The piece opens on the observation that the subject field splits almost cleanly into “I” versus “Others” — which Yau uses as the top-level partition. Three ribbon-style flow diagrams follow: (1) a full self-vs-others view, (2) a drill-down on the “Others” group showing family terms (husband, son, friend, wife, daughter), and (3) a specialized view of pet-related moments with much more specific verbs (snuggled, jumped, curled, rather than the generic had/got/watched of the human view). The story’s closing claim is that happiness comes “in many forms, from many places, and through many paths.”
What is effective?
The SVO parsing is a genuinely good analytical choice for this data. The naïve approach to a corpus of 100,000 text fragments is a bag-of-words frequency count — a word cloud. Bag-of-words tells you that dinner, movie, family, dog are common, but it cannot distinguish “I watched a movie” from “My son gave me a movie” because both contribute the same tokens. Parsing into SVO triples promotes the grammatical relationship into a first-class analytical object, so who acts on what becomes visible. For a seven-paragraph blog post, that is substantive analytical lift.
The “Self” vs. “Others” partition is load-bearing rather than arbitrary. Yau tells the reader explicitly that the subject field is bimodal — “I” on one side and everyone else on the other — and uses that empirical structure as the piece’s primary scaffolding. It is not a random slice chosen for visual convenience; it is the shape of the data, and building the argument on top of that shape is the right move.
The “team won game” surprised-then-verified moment is a discipline tell. When an unexpected result appears in a top-five list, the professional move is to go back to the raw text and check whether the parser captured something real or created an artefact. Yau does this, reports what he found (“mostly people who were happy their favorite sports team won a game”), and updates the interpretation accordingly. This is the minimum standard of data analysis honesty, and explicitly showing the verification step in a blog post register is a small but real piece of craft.
The Mechanical Turk caveat is in the main text, not buried in a footnote. This is a genuine improvement over Yau’s 2021 USDA food-availability piece, where the fundamental availability-vs-consumption caveat was confined to a single end-of-post paragraph. Here, the sampling limitation — skewed to 20–40-year-olds, likely boosting children and spouses, likely suppressing girlfriend/boyfriend and workplace references — is written into a paragraph that sits between two main charts. That is the correct placement for a methodological limitation that affects the interpretation of the next visual.
The pets section is a well-chosen narrative ending. The shift from generic verbs (had, got, watched) in the human view to specific verbs (snuggled, jumped, curled) in the pet view makes the same analytical point visible in two different textures: human happiness is often described with abstract gestures, pet happiness with concrete actions. Ending on the high-information-density pet view gives the piece a small analytical payoff rather than a flat stop.
What could be improved?
The SVO parser’s error rate is not quantified. Of 100,000 happy moments, how many yielded a clean triple? How many were dropped (moments like “the weather was nice” have no agentive verb)? How many were mis-parsed in ways the “team won game” check didn’t catch? For a computational text pipeline driving a top-five ranking, the parser’s precision and recall are first-order metadata. A single sentence — “about X% of moments could not be cleanly parsed, and among the parseable subset the top rankings are robust to reasonable alternative parsers” — would let the reader calibrate how much the rankings depend on the specific NLP pipeline used.
“Recently” is undefined and the ranking conflates frequency with salience. Participants were asked about things that “recently” made them happy, without temporal anchoring. A person listing yesterday’s dinner, last week’s game, and last month’s child’s graduation all count equally in the tabulation. This means the output is not frequency of happy events but recall-salience of happy events — two different quantities. Dinner happens daily but most dinners are not memorable; a team win is rare but often highly memorable. The top-five list mixes “frequent and ordinary” with “rare and memorable” without distinguishing them, which changes what the piece can and cannot claim. The argument would be more precisely stated as “dinner is the most commonly recalled form of happy moment,” not “dinner is what makes people most happy.”
Single-token subjects flatten heterogeneous relational categories. “Husband,” “son,” “wife,” “daughter” come through as distinct subjects because English vocabulary marks them separately. “Friend” comes through as a single label, even though new/old, close/casual, work/personal friendships produce different kinds of happy moments. The method’s resolution is inherited from the language’s vocabulary structure — family relationships are pre-disaggregated at the word level, social relationships are not — and the analysis reproduces that asymmetry without flagging it. A piece that wanted to be analytically careful about relational sources of happiness would need either a secondary tagging pass on “friend” contexts or an explicit acknowledgement that “friend” is an aggregation under which substantial variation is compressed.
The Mechanical Turk caveat is acknowledged but not given direction. Yau says the sample isn’t representative and gives age-skew as an example, but does not extend the reasoning to the piece’s conclusions. Mturk workers in 2018 were well-documented to skew US and Indian, to be lower-income on average, to work from home, to be male-overrepresented among Indian workers. These demographic skews have directional implications for the happy-moment distribution: low-income participants may systematically over-represent low-cost daily happiness (dinner, TV, family) and under-represent travel, major purchases, and career milestones. That is a specific prediction the caveat should make, not just the abstract statement that “the sample isn’t representative.”
The Western positive-psychology framing of “happiness” is not named. The HappyDB prompt — “list ten things that recently made you happy” — sits inside a research tradition (Seligman-era positive psychology) that has its own assumptions about what “happiness” means as a category, what counts as an instance of it, and how people self-report it. In East Asian affect vocabularies, the distinctions between 幸福 / 快乐 / 满足 / 顺心 are not all captured by a single English word “happy,” and cross-cultural affect research has documented that the prompt design shapes what gets listed. A piece whose closing claim is that “happiness comes in many forms, from many places” has, in fact, shown the many forms of a specific culturally bounded concept of happiness. Naming this would be one line of editorial honesty.
The ribbon diagram visually resembles the alluvial form from the earlier food-availability piece but is doing different work. In the USDA piece, alluvial diagrams were the correct form because the data had a temporal axis and items crossed rank over time. Here, the flow between subject / verb / object is a two-step bipartite matching without a temporal dimension; the visual language is sankey-ish rather than alluvial. A reader who has seen both pieces may interpret the new ribbons through the old reading habit, importing an implicit time axis that isn’t there. This is the reuse of a visual idiom across differently structured problems, and the piece does not disambiguate.
Proportionality. This remains a piece of careful blog-register data work with above-median analytical lift. The critique is calibrated against Yau’s own prior baseline: the Mturk caveat improved; the parser error rate is still not quantified; the temporal-ambiguity issue (“recently”) is new to this piece and unaddressed; the cultural framing of “happiness” was not named and arguably could not be in a piece of this length. For a blog with Yau’s readership and craft, the next step is the move the USDA piece never made either: bring the analytical caveat up from the footnote into the chart itself.
crumbs: 54  19 — Quality Control: Code and Data Hygiene

title: 19 — Quality Control: Code and Data Hygiene
text:
Code Quality Checklist — Worked Examples
The seven principles below are abstract until worked through on real data. What follows is one small example per principle, drawn where possible from patterns that came up across this semester’s ICAs.
1. DRY — Don’t Repeat Yourself
Anti-pattern. Computing the same summary four times with slightly different column names.

Code# Don't do this — three nearly-identical lines asking to be parameterized
diamonds |> summarise(mean_x = mean(x, na.rm = TRUE))

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  mean_x
   <dbl>
1   5.73

Codediamonds |> summarise(mean_y = mean(y, na.rm = TRUE))

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  mean_y
   <dbl>
1   5.73

Codediamonds |> summarise(mean_z = mean(z, na.rm = TRUE))

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  mean_z
   <dbl>
1   3.54


Refactor. Extract the pattern into across() or a function.

Codediamonds |>
    summarise(across(c(x, y, z), \(v) mean(v, na.rm = TRUE)))

# A tibble: 1 × 3
      x     y     z
  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1  5.73  5.73  3.54


The moment the same transformation is typed a second time with a changed column name, the correct move is to parameterize — either via a function argument or via a tidyverse helper like across(). Copy-paste is where bugs come from; the copy that got fixed and the copy that didn’t stay fixed are the same code until they aren’t.
2. Readability
Readability is a cognitive claim about the person who will read the code next — which is usually you, six months from now, with no memory of what you were doing. Two practical habits:


Pipe chains over nested function calls. x |> filter(...) |> mutate(...) |> summarise(...) reads like a sentence; summarise(mutate(filter(x, ...), ...), ...) requires the reader to mentally unwrap the nesting.

One idea per line. When a pipe step does more than one thing, it is often worth splitting into two steps with a named intermediate.


Code# Readable: each step is one idea, and the intermediate name explains the filter
# adult penguins with complete measurements
heavy_male_penguins <- penguins |>
    filter(sex == "male", !is.na(body_mass_g)) |>
    filter(body_mass_g > 4500)

heavy_male_penguins |>
    count(species, island) |>
    arrange(desc(n))

# A tibble: 5 × 3
  species   island        n
  <fct>     <fct>     <int>
1 Gentoo    Biscoe       61
2 Adelie    Biscoe        3
3 Adelie    Dream         2
4 Adelie    Torgersen     2
5 Chinstrap Dream         2


3. Meaningful Names
Anti-pattern. df, tmp, x, result — these carry no information. By the time you come back to the script you have to reconstruct what each was supposed to hold.
Pattern. Names should describe the content and, where relevant, the stage of processing:


raw_survey → cleaned_survey → survey_with_outcomes — a pipeline where the name tracks the transformation state

is_complete_case, has_outlier_bmi — boolean flags named as predicates so filter(is_complete_case) reads naturally

fit_lm_with_controls(), extract_ci_at_level() — functions named as verb phrases describing what they do

The test I apply: can I guess what an object holds or a function returns from its name alone, without looking at the definition? If not, the name is underspecified.
4. Testing
For any function that will be reused — even once — write a handful of test cases. testthat is the standard R tool.

Code# A small function to rescale a numeric vector to [0, 1]
rescale01 <- function(x) {
    rng <- range(x, na.rm = TRUE)
    if (diff(rng) == 0) return(rep(0.5, length(x)))
    (x - rng[1]) / (rng[2] - rng[1])
}

# Tests covering normal case, constant vector, NA handling, negative values
test_that("rescale01 behaves correctly", {
    expect_equal(rescale01(c(0, 5, 10)), c(0, 0.5, 1))
    expect_equal(rescale01(c(5, 5, 5)), c(0.5, 0.5, 0.5))
    expect_equal(rescale01(c(-10, 0, 10)), c(0, 0.5, 1))
    expect_equal(rescale01(c(1, NA, 3)), c(0, NA, 1))
    expect_length(rescale01(numeric(0)), 0)
})

── Warning: rescale01 behaves correctly ────────────────────────────────────────
no non-missing arguments to min; returning Inf
Backtrace:
    ▆
 1. ├─testthat::expect_length(rescale01(numeric(0)), 0)
 2. │ └─testthat::quasi_label(enquo(object), arg = "object")
 3. │   └─rlang::eval_bare(expr, quo_get_env(quo))
 4. └─global rescale01(numeric(0))
 5.   └─base::range(x, na.rm = TRUE)
 6.     └─base::.rangeNum(..., na.rm = na.rm, finite = finite, isNumeric = is.numeric)

── Warning: rescale01 behaves correctly ────────────────────────────────────────
no non-missing arguments to max; returning -Inf
Backtrace:
    ▆
 1. ├─testthat::expect_length(rescale01(numeric(0)), 0)
 2. │ └─testthat::quasi_label(enquo(object), arg = "object")
 3. │   └─rlang::eval_bare(expr, quo_get_env(quo))
 4. └─global rescale01(numeric(0))
 5.   └─base::range(x, na.rm = TRUE)
 6.     └─base::.rangeNum(..., na.rm = na.rm, finite = finite, isNumeric = is.numeric)


Tests that fail loudly when behaviour changes are the closest thing to institutional memory a codebase has. They are also the primary document of what the function is supposed to do — the tests express the contract.
5. Efficiency
Efficiency is worth worrying about when (a) the pipeline is slow enough to disrupt analysis, or (b) it is slow enough to prevent iteration. Below that threshold, readability wins over micro-optimization.

Codebench::mark(
    grow_vector = {
        x <- integer()
        for (i in 1:1e4) x <- c(x, i)
        x
    },
    preallocate = {
        x <- vector("integer", 1e4)
        for (i in 1:1e4) x[i] <- i
        x
    },
    vectorized = seq_len(1e4),
    check = TRUE,
    iterations = 10
)[, c("expression", "median", "mem_alloc")]

# A tibble: 3 × 3
  expression    median mem_alloc
  <bch:expr>  <bch:tm> <bch:byt>
1 grow_vector   64.8ms   191.3MB
2 preallocate  790.1µs    48.8KB
3 vectorized      41ns        0B


Growing a vector with c() inside a loop is ~100× slower than pre-allocating and ~1,000× slower than the vectorized form. The mem_alloc column explains why: every c(x, i) allocates a fresh vector one element longer, so total allocation scales quadratically with the iteration count.
6. Reproducibility
Reproducible code produces the same output on a different machine, in a different session, by a different user. The basic disciplines:


Relative paths, not absolute. read_csv("data/survey.csv") works when the project folder is cloned anywhere; read_csv("/Users/zhijun/Documents/.../survey.csv") works only on my laptop.

here::here() for paths when the working directory is unstable (e.g., Quarto renders from the document’s directory, R scripts from the project root). here::here("data", "survey.csv") resolves consistently.

set.seed() before any stochastic operation that the analysis depends on — simulation, sampling, bootstrap, random imputation. Without it, ?_map and rnorm give a different answer every run, and the figure in today’s draft can’t be reproduced tomorrow.

Pin package versions with renv for serious projects. This is the difference between a project that works in 2026 and a project that stops working when a dependency updates.

Include sessionInfo() in the rendered output for any analysis meant to travel.


Code# Capture the environment state at render time
sessionInfo()

R version 4.4.2 (2024-10-31)
Platform: aarch64-apple-darwin20
Running under: macOS 26.3

Matrix products: default
BLAS:   /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.4-arm64/Resources/lib/libRblas.0.dylib 
LAPACK: /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.4-arm64/Resources/lib/libRlapack.dylib;  LAPACK version 3.12.0

locale:
[1] en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8/C/en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8

time zone: America/Chicago
tzcode source: internal

attached base packages:
[1] stats     graphics  grDevices utils     datasets  methods   base     

other attached packages:
 [1] palmerpenguins_0.1.1 testthat_3.2.3       bench_1.1.4         
 [4] janitor_2.2.1        naniar_1.1.0         lubridate_1.9.4     
 [7] forcats_1.0.0        stringr_1.5.1        dplyr_1.1.4         
[10] purrr_1.0.4          readr_2.1.5          tidyr_1.3.1         
[13] tibble_3.2.1         ggplot2_3.5.2        tidyverse_2.0.0     

loaded via a namespace (and not attached):
 [1] utf8_1.2.4        generics_0.1.3    stringi_1.8.4     hms_1.1.3        
 [5] digest_0.6.37     magrittr_2.0.3    evaluate_1.0.3    grid_4.4.2       
 [9] timechange_0.3.0  pkgload_1.5.0     fastmap_1.2.0     rprojroot_2.1.1  
[13] jsonlite_2.0.0    brio_1.1.5        scales_1.3.0      cli_3.6.5        
[17] rlang_1.1.7       munsell_0.5.1     withr_3.0.2       yaml_2.3.10      
[21] tools_4.4.2       tzdb_0.5.0        colorspace_2.1-1  profmem_0.7.0    
[25] vctrs_0.6.5       R6_2.6.1          lifecycle_1.0.4   snakecase_0.11.1 
[29] htmlwidgets_1.6.4 waldo_0.6.1       pkgconfig_2.0.3   desc_1.4.3       
[33] pillar_1.10.1     gtable_0.3.6      glue_1.8.0        visdat_0.6.0     
[37] xfun_0.52         tidyselect_1.2.1  knitr_1.51        htmltools_0.5.8.1
[41] rmarkdown_2.30    compiler_4.4.2   


7. Simple Tasks in Functions
One function should do one thing. A function that does two things is harder to test, harder to re-use, harder to compose.
Anti-pattern. One function that loads data, cleans it, fits a model, extracts coefficients, and formats a table.
Pattern. Five functions, each doing one step, composed in a pipeline.

Code# Toy-sized versions of each step, so the composition pattern is demonstrable.
# In a real project each function would live in its own file under R/.
load_survey_data     <- function(path)              read_csv(path, show_col_types = FALSE)
clean_survey         <- function(data)              data |> clean_names() |> drop_na()
restrict_to_adults   <- function(data, min_age = 18) data |> filter(age >= min_age)
fit_outcome_model    <- function(data, formula)     lm(formula, data = data)
summarise_model_fit  <- function(fit)               broom::tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE)

# Stand-in dataset so the pipeline actually runs end-to-end
demo_path <- tempfile(fileext = ".csv")
write_csv(
    tibble(
        ID = 1:6,
        Age = c(17, 22, 34, 45, 29, 51),
        X1  = c(1.2, 2.4, 3.1, 4.0, 2.8, 5.5),
        Y   = c(10, 21, 29, 42, 25, 58)
    ),
    demo_path
)

# The composition — each step one idea, each step independently testable
raw      <- load_survey_data(demo_path)
cleaned  <- clean_survey(raw)
filtered <- restrict_to_adults(cleaned)
fit      <- fit_outcome_model(filtered, formula = y ~ x1)
summary  <- summarise_model_fit(fit)

summary

# A tibble: 2 × 7
  term        estimate std.error statistic  p.value conf.low conf.high
  <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
1 (Intercept)    -8.42     1.70      -4.95 0.0159      -13.8     -3.00
2 x1             12.2      0.457     26.7  0.000115     10.7     13.6 


Each step is independently testable, independently reusable, and independently debuggable. When something breaks, the failing function is the scope of the bug hunt — not the whole pipeline.
crumbs: 54  19 — Quality Control: Code and Data Hygiene

title: 19 — Quality Control: Code and Data Hygiene
text:
Data Quality Checklist — Worked Examples
Always keep the raw data
The first rule. The raw data file should be read-only, never modified in place. Every transformation creates a derived object or file; the original stays untouched. This is the single discipline that allows you to back out from a mistake six weeks later when you realize the 2,000 rows you “cleaned” included a systematic misinterpretation of one column.

Code# Demonstrate the pattern against a temp directory so this chunk actually runs.
# In a real project, swap `tmp_dir` for your project's `data/raw` and `data/processed` folders.
tmp_dir <- tempfile("survey_demo_"); dir.create(tmp_dir)
raw_path       <- file.path(tmp_dir, "survey_2024.csv")
processed_path <- file.path(tmp_dir, "survey_cleaned.csv")

# Seed the raw file once (standing in for a vendor-supplied dataset)
write_csv(tibble(ID = 1:3, Name = c("A", "B", NA)), raw_path)

# --- The pattern itself ---
raw_survey <- read_csv(raw_path, show_col_types = FALSE)  # read once, never write back
cleaned    <- raw_survey |> clean_names() |> drop_na(id)  # derived object
write_csv(cleaned, processed_path)                         # written to a different path

cleaned

# A tibble: 3 × 2
     id name 
  <dbl> <chr>
1     1 A    
2     2 B    
3     3 <NA> 


Check missing values before trusting anything

Code# A quick audit on any new dataset
penguins |> miss_var_summary()

# A tibble: 8 × 3
  variable          n_miss pct_miss
  <chr>              <int>    <num>
1 sex                   11    3.20 
2 bill_length_mm         2    0.581
3 bill_depth_mm          2    0.581
4 flipper_length_mm      2    0.581
5 body_mass_g            2    0.581
6 species                0    0    
7 island                 0    0    
8 year                   0    0    



Codepenguins |> vis_miss()







The pattern: every time you read data, immediately run miss_var_summary() to count NAs per variable. If a column has unexpected NAs, that is evidence the data parsing or the data source is not what you thought. Two percent missing on a variable you expected to be complete is a research question, not a nuisance.
Parse dates with eyes open
Dates are the single most common source of silent data corruption because date strings look like dates but can be in any of a dozen formats.

Codedates_raw <- c("2024-03-15", "03/15/2024", "15/03/2024", "March 15, 2024")

# lubridate tries, but the US/EU ambiguity can silently swap month and day
tibble(raw = dates_raw) |>
    mutate(
        ymd_parsed = lubridate::ymd(raw, quiet = TRUE),
        mdy_parsed = lubridate::mdy(raw, quiet = TRUE),
        dmy_parsed = lubridate::dmy(raw, quiet = TRUE)
    )

# A tibble: 4 × 4
  raw            ymd_parsed mdy_parsed dmy_parsed
  <chr>          <date>     <date>     <date>    
1 2024-03-15     2024-03-15 NA         NA        
2 03/15/2024     NA         2024-03-15 NA        
3 15/03/2024     NA         NA         2024-03-15
4 March 15, 2024 NA         2024-03-15 NA        


Each parser fails on the strings it cannot interpret (returning NA), but for an ambiguous string like "03/04/2024" — is that March 4 or April 3? — the two parsers will both succeed and produce different, equally plausible dates. The only defence is knowing the data’s provenance (which country’s conventions? which system exported it?) and verifying with a small sample.
Parse strings with test cases
When using regular expressions to extract structured information from text, always test against both cases you want to match and cases you want to exclude.

Codeemails <- c("les@mac.edu", "les@mac.net", "les@mac.", "les@macedu", "not an email")

tibble(
    raw = emails,
    is_valid = str_detect(emails, "^[^@\\s]+@[^@\\s]+\\.[^@\\s]+$")
)

# A tibble: 5 × 2
  raw          is_valid
  <chr>        <lgl>   
1 les@mac.edu  TRUE    
2 les@mac.net  TRUE    
3 les@mac.     FALSE   
4 les@macedu   FALSE   
5 not an email FALSE   


The pattern: one list of positive cases (should match), one list of negative cases (should not match), a single tibble showing the regex decision on each. Any surprise in either column means the regex is wrong.
Join discipline
Joins are where datasets most often go wrong. Three checks before merging:
Row-count prediction. Before the join, predict how many rows the result should have. If an inner-joined table has 9,800 rows and you expected 10,000, that’s 200 missing matches to investigate.
Key-representation consistency. id = 1 and id = "01" will not match. Check class() and spot-check values on both sides before joining.
Anti-join for unmatched rows. anti_join(x, y) shows the rows in x that had no match in y. This is the single most useful diagnostic for understanding why a join returned fewer rows than expected.
The following simulation demonstrates the failure mode. patients$id is integer; outcomes$id is character — same values, different types.

Codepatients <- tibble(id = 1:5, name = c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E"))
outcomes <- tibble(id = c("1", "2", "3"), outcome = c(10, 20, 30))

class(patients$id)

[1] "integer"

Codeclass(outcomes$id)

[1] "character"


What goes wrong. Current dplyr (≥1.1) refuses the mismatched join outright — the error message itself is the diagnostic. Older dplyr would silently coerce or return a table where every outcome was NA, which was the original motivation for type discipline. Either way, the lesson is the same: reconcile key types before joining, or your pipeline will either crash or (worse) fail silently.

Code# Capture the error so the chunk keeps running. In older dplyr this would
# instead return a tibble with outcome column entirely NA — no error, no warning.
tryCatch(
    left_join(patients, outcomes, by = "id"),
    error = function(e) {
        cat("left_join refused to proceed:\n", conditionMessage(e), sep = "")
    }
)

left_join refused to proceed:
Can't join `x$id` with `y$id` due to incompatible types.
ℹ `x$id` is a <integer>.
ℹ `y$id` is a <character>.


The fix. Coerce the keys to a common type on both sides before joining. Integer-to-character and character-to-integer are both safe here (the character values are clean integers); for messier keys, coercing both to character is the conservative default.

Codeoutcomes_fixed <- outcomes |> mutate(id = as.integer(id))

left_join(patients, outcomes_fixed, by = "id")

# A tibble: 5 × 3
     id name  outcome
  <int> <chr>   <dbl>
1     1 A          10
2     2 B          20
3     3 C          30
4     4 D          NA
5     5 E          NA


The diagnostic for partial matches. Even with types reconciled, only three of five patients have outcome data. anti_join() shows which rows in patients had no match — the starting point for investigating whether those are truly missing, filtered elsewhere in the pipeline, or indicative of a deeper data problem.

Codeanti_join(patients, outcomes_fixed, by = "id")

# A tibble: 2 × 2
     id name 
  <int> <chr>
1     4 D    
2     5 E    


Check for duplicates before aggregating
A duplicate record silently counted twice in a group_by() |> summarise(n = n()) will inflate the group count without any warning. janitor::get_dupes() shows you duplicate rows:

Code# Add a synthetic duplicate to demonstrate
penguins_with_dup <- penguins |>
    slice(c(1:344, 1))   # duplicate the first row

# `get_dupes` takes the key columns and returns the duplicated rows
penguins_with_dup |>
    get_dupes(species, island, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, sex, year) |>
    head()

# A tibble: 2 × 9
  species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
  <fct>   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
1 Adelie  Torgersen           39.1          18.7               181        3750
2 Adelie  Torgersen           39.1          18.7               181        3750
# ℹ 3 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>, dupe_count <int>


For real data the “key” is whatever combination of columns should uniquely identify a record — often a subset of the available columns, not all of them.
Sanity-visualize early and often
The cheapest bug catcher in data analysis is a quick plot. Before any substantive modelling, plot the outcome and the key predictors:

Codepenguins |>
    drop_na() |>
    ggplot(aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, colour = species)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
    labs(
        x = "Bill length (mm)", y = "Body mass (g)",
        title = "Sanity check: bill length vs. body mass by species"
    ) +
    theme_minimal()







Four questions to ask of any such plot:


Does the number of points look right? If you expected 340 and see 330, something dropped.

Are the values in the expected range? Penguins with body mass of 50 kg are a data-entry error.

Do the relationships make sense? Positive slope where you expected negative is a sign flip.

Do the groups behave as expected? Same intercept-and-slope across groups where you expected heterogeneity means your group variable may be wrong.
crumbs: 54  19 — Quality Control: Code and Data Hygiene

title: 19 — Quality Control: Code and Data Hygiene
text:
Reflection
Code quality is an institutional-memory problem, not a style problem. The seven principles above are often framed as aesthetics — “write clean code” — but they are really disciplines for future communication. The person most likely to read my code is me in six months, with no memory of what I was doing. The person second most likely is a reviewer, collaborator, or successor who needs to verify or extend the work. DRY, readable naming, tests, reproducibility — these are the minimum standard for code that communicates across time and across people. They are exactly the same problem that good academic writing solves: making the reasoning legible to someone who doesn’t already share your context.
Data quality is about respecting the gap between the world and the dataset. The raw-data principle, missing-value audit, date parsing, join discipline, duplicate checks, sanity visualization — these are all versions of the same move: don’t trust that the data is what you think it is until you’ve checked. The world is noisy and data-collection pipelines are imperfect, and the dataset you inherit is the output of many human decisions you did not witness. Each check is a place where that gap between the world and the representation can be surfaced before it contaminates downstream results. This is exactly the point of the human-centered data science exercise from ICA 17 — the checklist in this ICA is its operational counterpart.
The same discipline that makes code reproducible makes research defensible. For empirical work at scale — the work that a thesis, a journal submission, or a policy brief rests on — the question “does this analysis hold up under scrutiny” reduces to: can a reviewer re-run your pipeline from your data and code, and get the same numbers? If the answer is no, the conclusions are not defensible regardless of how sophisticated the analysis looked. Relative paths, seed-setting, environment capture, and function decomposition are not bureaucratic overhead — they are the infrastructure that makes honest research possible. The habits formalized in this ICA are the minimum standard for any work that is meant to travel beyond a single laptop.
crumbs: 54  19 — Quality Control: Code and Data Hygiene

title: Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
Background
The previous analysis of this dataset established a macro-level capital paradox: the Brazilian formal sector exhibits extreme capital concentration, with Gini coefficients above 0.98 in every size tier. That finding tells us the shape of inequality but not its sources. It also treats each size category as internally homogeneous, which it is not.
Brazil’s official firm-size classification (Simples Nacional) is based on annual gross revenue, not capital stock. A sole proprietor earning 360,000 BRL per year is legally a “micro-enterprise” regardless of whether they hold 180,000 BRL or 10,000,000 BRL in declared capital stock. This classification logic creates a natural experiment embedded in the data: firms within the same regulatory category can have wildly different capital endowments. The spread within size tiers is as analytically interesting as the gaps between them.
A second underexplored dimension is governance. The owner_qualification field records the legal role of the controlling person: a Managing Partner who actively runs the business, a Beneficial Owner who holds equity but may be a passive investor or part of a holding-company chain, or a solo Entrepreneur operating under their own identity. These governance archetypes map onto well- established predictions from agency theory: passive beneficial ownership is associated with access to external capital, while sole entrepreneurs face the highest information asymmetries with lenders.
crumbs: 55  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
Research Questions
Three analytical questions guide this extended analysis:


Within-tier inequality: Are micro-enterprises themselves internally unequal in capital endowments, or is the category relatively flat?

Governance as a capital predictor: Does owner qualification predict capital accumulation independently of firm size, and by how much?

The “capital-rich micro”: Among micro-enterprises with unusually high capital stock, what governance and legal-form features distinguish them from the capital-poor majority?
