text:
Design Conventions

Show codelibrary(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(ggridges)
library(patchwork)

# ── Shared palette ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
pal_size <- c(
  "Micro"        = "#c3553a",   # terracotta
  "Small"        = "#d9a441",   # amber
  "Medium/Large" = "#2a5a6b"    # deep teal
)

pal_gov <- c(
  "Sole Entrepreneur"                    = "#d9a441",   # amber (solo)
  "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator"    = "#5b8a9a",   # slate blue (active)
  "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)" = "#1d3557"    # deep navy (passive)
)

# ── Shared theme ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
theme_cwc <- function(base_size = 13) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      plot.title        = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size + 2,
                                       colour = "#1d3557", margin = margin(b = 4)),
      plot.subtitle     = element_text(colour = "grey35", size = base_size - 2,
                                       lineheight = 1.35, margin = margin(b = 18)),
      plot.caption      = element_text(colour = "grey55", size = base_size - 5,
                                       hjust = 0, margin = margin(t = 12)),
      plot.title.position   = "plot",
      plot.caption.position = "plot",
      strip.text        = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size - 1,
                                       colour = "#1d3557"),
      strip.background  = element_rect(fill = "grey96", colour = NA),
      panel.grid.minor  = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major  = element_line(colour = "grey92", linewidth = 0.3),
      panel.spacing     = unit(1.4, "lines"),
      legend.position   = "top",
      legend.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size - 2),
      legend.text       = element_text(size = base_size - 2),
      axis.title        = element_text(colour = "grey30", size = base_size - 2),
      axis.text         = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = base_size - 3)
    )
}

title: Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
Data Preparation

Show codecompanies <- read_csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2026/2026-01-27/companies.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

co <- companies |>
  filter(!is.na(company_size), !is.na(capital_stock), !is.na(owner_qualification),
         capital_stock > 0) |>
  mutate(
    size = case_when(
      company_size == "micro-enterprise" ~ "Micro",
      company_size == "small-enterprise" ~ "Small",
      TRUE                               ~ "Medium/Large"
    ),
    size = factor(size, levels = c("Micro", "Small", "Medium/Large")),

    governance = case_when(
      str_detect(owner_qualification, regex("beneficial owner", ignore_case = TRUE))         ~ "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      str_detect(owner_qualification, regex("entrepreneur|business owner", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Sole Entrepreneur",
      str_detect(owner_qualification, regex("managing partner|administrator", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    governance = factor(governance, levels = c(
      "Sole Entrepreneur",
      "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      "Other"
    )),

    log_capital = log10(capital_stock),

    capital_q75_micro = quantile(capital_stock[size == "Micro"], 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    is_capital_rich_micro = (size == "Micro") & (capital_stock >= capital_q75_micro)
  )

cat("Observations:", comma(nrow(co)), "\n")

Observations: 141,332 

Show codecat("Governance archetypes:\n"); print(count(co, governance, sort = TRUE))

Governance archetypes:


# A tibble: 4 × 2
  governance                                  n
  <fct>                                   <int>
1 "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator"    122620
2 "Sole Entrepreneur"                     15201
3 "Other"                                  3069
4 "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)"    442

Show codecat("\nCapital-rich micro threshold: BRL ",
    comma(unique(co$capital_q75_micro[!is.na(co$capital_q75_micro)])[1]), "\n", sep = "")


Capital-rich micro threshold: BRL 500,000
crumbs: 55  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
section: Visualization 1 — The Power-Law Signature of Brazilian Capital
text:
Visualization 1 — The Power-Law Signature of Brazilian Capital
A plain Lorenz curve compresses the story because every tier is extremely concentrated — the three Ginis (all above 0.98) plot as almost the same curve. To read the structure honestly, we need two views: a log-rank concentration curve that separates the tiers where the action lives, and a direct decomposition of who sits at the top.

Show code# ── Gini helper ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
gini <- function(x) {
  x <- sort(x[!is.na(x) & x > 0])
  n <- length(x)
  2 * sum((seq_len(n) / n) * (x / sum(x))) - (n + 1) / n
}

# Compute Gini + Top 1% share per tier; burn both into the legend label
tier_stats <- co |>
  group_by(size) |>
  summarise(
    g    = gini(capital_stock),
    top1 = sum(sort(capital_stock, decreasing = TRUE)[1:ceiling(n() * 0.01)]) /
           sum(capital_stock),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    label = sprintf("%-13s   Gini %.3f    Top 1%% holds %s",
                    size, g, percent(top1, accuracy = 1))
  )

# ── Panel A: Concentration curve on log-x, zero in-plot annotations ─────────
conc_curve <- co |>
  group_by(size) |>
  arrange(desc(capital_stock), .by_group = TRUE) |>
  mutate(
    rank_pct = row_number() / n(),
    cum_top  = cumsum(capital_stock) / sum(capital_stock)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(rank_pct <= 0.5)

p_conc <- ggplot(conc_curve, aes(x = rank_pct, y = cum_top, colour = size)) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0.01, xend = 0.01, y = 0, yend = 1,
           colour = "grey75", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.45) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.01, y = 1.04, label = "Top 1%",
           colour = "grey40", size = 3.3, fontface = "italic") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.5, alpha = 0.92) +
  scale_x_log10(
    breaks = c(0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5),
    labels = c("Top 0.1%", "Top 1%", "Top 10%", "Top 50%"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.05))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(),
    limits = c(0, 1.08),
    expand = c(0, 0),
    name   = "Cumulative share of tier's capital"
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal_size,
                      labels = tier_stats$label,
                      name   = NULL) +
  labs(
    x = "Rank of firms (log scale, highest capital first) →",
    subtitle = "(A)  Concentration curve — log-x rank reveals the separation\n        between tiers that linear Lorenz cannot show"
  ) +
  theme_cwc() +
  theme(
    legend.position      = c(0.02, 0.98),
    legend.justification = c(0, 1),
    legend.background    = element_rect(fill = alpha("white", 0.9), colour = NA),
    legend.key.width     = unit(1.5, "lines"),
    legend.text          = element_text(family = "mono", size = 10),
    plot.subtitle        = element_text(colour = "grey35", size = 11,
                                        lineheight = 1.3, margin = margin(b = 10))
  )

# ── Panel B: Stacked decomposition by rank bucket ───────────────────────────
decomp <- co |>
  group_by(size) |>
  arrange(desc(capital_stock), .by_group = TRUE) |>
  mutate(
    rank_pct = row_number() / n(),
    segment  = case_when(
      rank_pct <= 0.01 ~ "Top 1%",
      rank_pct <= 0.10 ~ "Next 9%",
      rank_pct <= 0.50 ~ "Next 40%",
      TRUE             ~ "Bottom 50%"
    ),
    segment  = factor(segment,
                      levels = c("Bottom 50%", "Next 40%", "Next 9%", "Top 1%"))
  ) |>
  group_by(size, segment) |>
  summarise(cap = sum(capital_stock), .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(size) |>
  mutate(share = cap / sum(cap)) |>
  ungroup()

pal_segment <- c(
  "Top 1%"     = "#1d3557",
  "Next 9%"    = "#457b9d",
  "Next 40%"   = "#a8dadc",
  "Bottom 50%" = "#e9ecef"
)

p_decomp <- ggplot(decomp, aes(x = size, y = share, fill = segment)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.62, colour = "white", linewidth = 1.4) +
  geom_text(
    data = filter(decomp, share >= 0.04),   # suppress tiny stray labels
    aes(label = percent(share, accuracy = 1),
        colour = segment %in% c("Top 1%", "Next 9%")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    fontface = "bold", size = 4, show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = pal_segment,
    breaks = c("Top 1%", "Next 9%", "Next 40%", "Bottom 50%"),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "white", `FALSE` = "grey25")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(),
                     expand = c(0, 0),
                     name   = "Share of tier's total capital") +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    subtitle = "(B)  In every tier, the top 1% of firms holds ~96–99%\n        of the tier's capital. The bottom half is a sliver."
  ) +
  theme_cwc() +
  theme(
    legend.position    = "top",
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(colour = "grey35", size = 11,
                                      lineheight = 1.3, margin = margin(b = 10))
  )

# ── Combine ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
(p_conc | p_decomp) +
  plot_layout(widths = c(1.15, 1)) +
  plot_annotation(
    title    = "Capital concentration is near-total in every Brazilian firm-size tier",
    subtitle = "Gini coefficients exceed 0.98 across Micro, Small, and Medium/Large — 'micro-enterprise' is not uniquely unequal.\nThe capital trap is a universal power-law structure, not a feature of small firms.",
    caption  = caption_text,
    theme    = theme_cwc()
  ) &
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16, colour = "#1d3557"))






Figure 55.1




Panel A uses a log-scaled rank axis to surface a separation that linear Lorenz curves flatten away. The legend carries the precise numbers: each tier’s Gini and its top-1% capital share. Panel B decomposes the same fact along a cleaner axis — every tier’s top 1% of firms holds roughly 96–99% of that tier’s capital, while the bottom half is a sliver barely visible at the top of each bar. The “capital trap” described in earlier work is not a peculiarity of micro-enterprises. It is a universal power-law structure across the Brazilian formal sector. This reframes the rest of the analysis: what differentiates tiers is not their inequality, but the who at the top — the question governance can answer.
crumbs: 55  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
section: Visualization 2 — Governance as a Distributional Shape, Not Just a Level
text:
Visualization 2 — Governance as a Distributional Shape, Not Just a Level

Show codegov_stats <- co |>
  filter(governance != "Other") |>
  group_by(size, governance) |>
  summarise(
    median_cap = median(capital_stock),
    mean_cap   = mean(capital_stock),
    p90_cap    = quantile(capital_stock, 0.90),
    n          = n(),
    .groups    = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    med_lab = paste0("median ", number(median_cap, scale = 1e-3, suffix = "k",
                                       big.mark = ",", accuracy = 1))
  )

ggplot(
  co |> filter(governance != "Other"),
  aes(x = log_capital, y = governance, fill = governance)
) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    alpha = 0.85, scale = 0.92, rel_min_height = 0.01,
    quantile_lines = TRUE, quantiles = 2,
    colour = "white", linewidth = 0.45
  ) +
  # mean marker (red tick) — surfaces the heavy-tail of Beneficial Owners
  geom_segment(
    data = gov_stats,
    aes(x = log10(mean_cap), xend = log10(mean_cap),
        y = as.numeric(governance) - 0.02,
        yend = as.numeric(governance) + 0.38),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    colour = "#c3553a", linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  # median label placed above the ridge
  geom_text(
    data = gov_stats,
    aes(x = log10(median_cap), y = as.numeric(governance) + 0.72,
        label = med_lab),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    size = 3.0, colour = "grey25", hjust = 0.5
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~size, nrow = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_gov, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(4, 5, 6, 7, 8),
    labels = c("10k", "100k", "1M", "10M", "100M"),
    name   = "Capital stock (BRL, log scale)",
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.04))
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(expand = expansion(add = c(0.3, 1.1))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Governance shapes the distribution — Managing Partners lead the median, Beneficial Owners carry the tail",
    subtitle = "Density ridges show capital-stock distribution by owner archetype within each size tier.\nWhite line = median  •  Red tick = mean  •  X-axis: log₁₀ scale",
    y        = NULL,
    caption  = caption_text
  ) +
  theme_cwc() +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())






Figure 55.2




The refined picture flips the original interpretation. Looking at medians:


Managing Partners hold the highest median capital, and — crucially — they are the only archetype whose median scales with firm size: 300k at Micro, 360k at Small, 1,000k at Medium/Large. Active operators accumulate capital as they grow.

Sole Entrepreneurs show a flat median (~300k) that barely moves across tiers — consistent with the information-asymmetry prediction from agency theory.

Beneficial Owners are the most distinctive: their median is low and flat (~300k across all three tiers), but the mean (red tick) sits far to the right of the median, and the distribution has a visible long right tail. This is a heavy-tail archetype — most BO firms are small, but a minority hold enormous capital. That shape is the statistical fingerprint of holding-company architecture: passive ownership is rare, and when it appears, it spans the full range from shell companies to major investment vehicles.

The governance premium, then, is not uniform. It shows up as a level premium for Managing Partners (especially in larger tiers) and as a variance premium for Beneficial Owners. Policies that assume a single governance hierarchy miss this distributional shape.
crumbs: 55  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
section: Visualization 3 — The Escape Route Is a Partnership, Not an LLC
text:
Visualization 3 — The Escape Route Is a Partnership, Not an LLC

Show codemicro_profile <- co |>
  filter(size == "Micro", governance != "Other") |>
  mutate(
    legal_short = case_when(
      str_detect(legal_nature, "LLC")                 ~ "LLC",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Sole Proprietorship") ~ "Sole\nProprietary",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Individual Limited")  ~ "EIRELI",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Simple")              ~ "Simple\nPartnership",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Silent")              ~ "Silent\nPartnership",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Corporation")         ~ "Corporation",
      TRUE                                            ~ "Other"
    )
  )

escape_rate <- micro_profile |>
  group_by(legal_short, governance) |>
  summarise(
    n_total    = n(),
    n_rich     = sum(is_capital_rich_micro),
    escape_pct = n_rich / n_total,
    .groups    = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(n_total >= 20) |>
  mutate(
    combo_label = paste0(legal_short, " × ", str_replace(governance, "\\n", " "))
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(escape_pct))

ggplot(
  escape_rate,
  aes(x = fct_reorder(combo_label, escape_pct),
      y = escape_pct,
      fill = governance)
) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7, alpha = 0.95) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(escape_pct, accuracy = 0.1)),
            hjust = -0.18, size = 3.3, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0("n = ", comma(n_total)), y = 0.003),
            hjust = 0, size = 2.8, colour = "white", fontface = "bold") +
  coord_flip(clip = "off") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(),
    limits = c(0, 0.58),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05)),
    name   = "Share of cell above the 75th capital percentile (BRL 500,000)"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_gov, name = "Governance archetype",
                    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)) +
  labs(
    title    = "The micro-enterprise escape profile is a partnership with a passive equity holder",
    subtitle = "Share of capital-rich micros (>BRL 500k) by legal form × governance cell.\nCells with fewer than 20 firms excluded. Counts shown in white.",
    x        = NULL,
    caption  = caption_text
  ) +
  theme_cwc() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = 10, colour = "grey20"),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 40, 10, 10)
  )






Figure 55.3




Ranking the legal-form × governance cells that survive the n ≥ 20 cutoff:


Simple Partnership × Beneficial Owner (48.2%) — almost half of micros in this cell are capital-rich.

Simple Partnership × Managing Partner (41.6%) — second highest.
Sole Proprietary × Sole Entrepreneur (34.3%), LLC × Managing Partner (29.0%), EIRELI × Beneficial Owner (24.1%) trail behind.

The pattern is not about LLCs as a legal form — it is about multi-party partnership structures. The Simple Partnership requires two or more parties on paper; when a Beneficial Owner sits behind them, the cell’s escape rate nearly doubles the base rate. This is the fingerprint of holding-company architecture operating through partnership law: a passive equity holder channelling capital into a nominally two-party vehicle whose revenue happens to sit below the Simples threshold.
EIRELI (Brazil’s individual limited liability form, created for single-person companies to separate personal from business liability) appears further down the list. The popular framing of “EIRELI as escape route” is not what the data show. The escape route is partnership law plus passive ownership.
crumbs: 55  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
Synthesis
This extended analysis reframes the capital trap finding in three directions.
First, capital concentration is universal across the Brazilian formal sector, not a micro-enterprise pathology. Gini coefficients above 0.98 in every size tier — and top-1% capital shares of 96–99% — mean that treating “micro-enterprise” as a uniquely unequal category misreads the structure. The same power-law sits behind every regulatory label.
Second, governance does not deliver a single capital hierarchy. Managing Partners lead at the median and scale with firm size. Sole Entrepreneurs stay flat. Beneficial Owners are a heavy-tail archetype — most are small, a few are very large. Size and governance are orthogonal dimensions, but they interact differently depending on which moment of the distribution (median vs. tail) one cares about.
Third, the capital-rich micro profile concentrates in Simple Partnership structures under Beneficial Ownership — a signature consistent with holding-company architecture rather than organic small-business growth. The policy implication is direct: if Brazil’s development goal is to support genuinely capital-constrained entrepreneurs, the targeting instrument must incorporate legal form and governance alongside revenue thresholds. Revenue alone is porous — partnership law plus passive ownership is the channel through which capital-rich firms wear micro-enterprise clothing.
crumbs: 55  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Why replace_na(mean(…)) Lies, and MICE Fixes It
text:
The Dataset
We use the nhanes dataset that ships with the mice package — a 25-row subset of the US National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey containing age, BMI, hypertension status, and cholesterol. It already has real missing values, which makes it a perfect teaching case.
Codedata(nhanes, package = "mice")
glimpse(nhanes)

Rows: 25
Columns: 4
$ age <dbl> 1, 2, 1, 3, 1, 3, 1, 1, 2, 2, 1, 2, 3, 2, 1, 1, 3, 2, 1, 3, 1, 1, …
$ bmi <dbl> NA, 22.7, NA, NA, 20.4, NA, 22.5, 30.1, 22.0, NA, NA, NA, 21.7, 28…
$ hyp <dbl> NA, 1, 1, NA, 1, NA, 1, 1, 1, NA, NA, NA, 1, 2, 1, NA, 2, 2, 1, 2,…
$ chl <dbl> NA, 187, 187, NA, 113, 184, 118, 187, 238, NA, NA, NA, 206, 204, N…
How much is missing, and where?
Codemiss_var_summary(nhanes)

# A tibble: 4 × 3
  variable n_miss pct_miss
  <chr>     <int>    <num>
1 chl          10       40
2 bmi           9       36
3 hyp           8       32
4 age           0        0
Codevis_miss(nhanes) +
  labs(title = "Missingness map — nhanes dataset",
       caption = "Zhijun He") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: 56  Why replace_na(mean(...)) Lies, and MICE Fixes It

title: Why replace_na(mean(…)) Lies, and MICE Fixes It
text:
Strategy 1: Mean Imputation (the wrong way)
The most common default: replace every NA with the column mean.

Codenhanes_mean <- nhanes |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ replace_na(.x, mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE))))

# Confirm no NAs remain
colSums(is.na(nhanes_mean))

age bmi hyp chl 
  0   0   0   0 


Now fit a simple regression (cholesterol ~ BMI) on the mean-imputed data:

Codefit_mean <- lm(chl ~ bmi, data = nhanes_mean)
summary(fit_mean)


Call:
lm(formula = chl ~ bmi, data = nhanes_mean)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-63.932  -7.400   0.000   6.263  90.199 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)  
(Intercept)   91.340     53.873   1.695   0.1035  
bmi            3.767      2.013   1.871   0.0741 .
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 32.86 on 23 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.1321,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.0944 
F-statistic: 3.502 on 1 and 23 DF,  p-value: 0.07408


What does the distribution of bmi look like after mean imputation?

Codebind_rows(
  nhanes |> mutate(source = "Original (NAs removed)") |> drop_na(bmi),
  nhanes_mean |> mutate(source = "After mean imputation")
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = bmi, fill = source)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 12, alpha = 0.7, position = "identity") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#E74C3C", "#3498DB")) +
  labs(
    title   = "BMI distribution: original vs. mean imputation",
    subtitle = "Mean imputation creates an artificial spike at the mean",
    fill    = NULL, x = "BMI", y = "Count",
    caption = "Zhijun He"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")








Problem: every missing BMI becomes 26.6. The distribution is distorted, variance is deflated, and downstream standard errors will be too small.
crumbs: 56  Why replace_na(mean(...)) Lies, and MICE Fixes It

title: Why replace_na(mean(…)) Lies, and MICE Fixes It
text:
Strategy 2: MICE (the right way)
MICE (Multivariate Imputation by Chained Equations) creates m = 5 complete datasets, each with different plausible imputed values drawn from the conditional distribution of each variable given all others.

Codeset.seed(42)
imp <- mice(nhanes, m = 5, method = "pmm", printFlag = FALSE)
imp

Class: mids
Number of multiple imputations:  5 
Imputation methods:
  age   bmi   hyp   chl 
   "" "pmm" "pmm" "pmm" 
PredictorMatrix:
    age bmi hyp chl
age   0   1   1   1
bmi   1   0   1   1
hyp   1   1   0   1
chl   1   1   1   0


What did MICE actually impute?
stripplot shows the 5 imputed values (red) overlaid on the observed data (blue) for each variable. Good imputation should look like it came from the same distribution.

Codestripplot(imp, pch = 20, cex = 1.2,
          sub = "Zhijun He")







Fit the model across all 5 imputed datasets and pool

Codefit_mice <- with(imp, lm(chl ~ bmi))
pooled   <- pool(fit_mice)
summary(pooled)

         term  estimate std.error statistic       df    p.value
1 (Intercept) 123.39189 61.258722  2.014275 16.69685 0.06038126
2         bmi   2.64382  2.271805  1.163753 17.18086 0.26043170
crumbs: 56  Why replace_na(mean(...)) Lies, and MICE Fixes It

title: Why replace_na(mean(…)) Lies, and MICE Fixes It
text:
Side-by-Side Comparison

Code# Extract coefficients from both approaches
results <- bind_rows(
  broom::tidy(fit_mean) |>
    filter(term == "bmi") |>
    mutate(method = "Mean imputation"),
  summary(pooled) |>
    as_tibble() |>
    filter(term == "bmi") |>
    rename(estimate = estimate, std.error = std.error,
           statistic = statistic, p.value = p.value) |>
    mutate(method = "MICE (pooled)")
)

results |>
  select(method, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "BMI coefficient: mean imputation vs. MICE")


BMI coefficient: mean imputation vs. MICE

method
estimate
std.error
statistic
p.value



Mean imputation
3.767

1.871
0.074


MICE (pooled)
2.644
2.272

Coderesults |>
  mutate(
    lo = estimate - 1.96 * std.error,
    hi = estimate + 1.96 * std.error
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = method, y = estimate, color = method)) +
  geom_point(size = 4) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi), width = 0.15, linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#E74C3C", "#3498DB")) +
  labs(
    title    = "BMI coefficient estimate ± 95% CI",
    subtitle = "MICE produces a wider, more honest confidence interval",
    y        = "Coefficient estimate", x = NULL, color = NULL,
    caption  = "Zhijun He"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

title: Why replace_na(mean(…)) Lies, and MICE Fixes It
section: Why the Intervals Differ: Rubin’s Rules
text:
Why the Intervals Differ: Rubin’s Rules
When we call pool(), MICE combines estimates across \(m = 5\) datasets using Rubin’s Rules (1987):
\[\bar{Q} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\hat{Q}_i\]
\[T = \bar{U} + \left(1 + \frac{1}{m}\right) B\]
where \(\bar{U}\) is the within-imputation variance (model uncertainty) and \(B\) is the between-imputation variance (imputation uncertainty). Mean imputation sets \(B = 0\) by construction — it pretends there is no uncertainty about the imputed values. MICE does not.

Code# Inspect the pooled variance components
pool(fit_mice) |> getElement("pooled") |>
  select(term, estimate, ubar, b, t, riv) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 4,
    caption = "Rubin's Rules components (ubar = within, b = between, t = total, riv = relative increase in variance)")


Rubin’s Rules components (ubar = within, b = between, t = total, riv = relative increase in variance)

term
estimate
ubar
b
t
riv



(Intercept)
123.3919
3221.0444
442.9889
3752.6310
0.1650


bmi
2.6438
4.4967
0.5537
5.1611
0.1478





riv (relative increase in variance due to missingness) quantifies exactly how much wider MICE makes the confidence interval compared to a complete-data analysis. Mean imputation would report riv = 0 — which is simply a lie.
crumbs: 56  Why replace_na(mean(...)) Lies, and MICE Fixes It

title: Why replace_na(mean(…)) Lies, and MICE Fixes It
text:
Key Takeaway








Mean imputation
MICE



Variance of imputed variable

Deflated (spike at mean)
Preserved


Correlation structure
Weakened
Preserved


Standard errors
Too small
Correct via Rubin’s Rules


Between-imputation uncertainty

Ignored (\(B = 0\))
Accounted for


Lines of code
1
~4



replace_na(mean(...)) is not “safe” — it is a modeling assumption disguised as data cleaning. It treats imputed values as if they were observed facts. Use it for quick EDA. For any inferential analysis, use MICE.

title: Verifying Pagination Strategies: A Monte Carlo Simulation
text:
Motivation
In our API unit we learned to paginate like this:
This is the pattern almost every introductory data-science class teaches, and on a well-behaved API during a quiet afternoon it works fine. My Exam 3 report argued that the same pattern is a silent production bug on any API that is live, rate-limited, or occasionally flaky — which is to say, any real one. I claimed three failure modes:
On the exam slide I put concrete completeness numbers on a bar chart: 62% / 84% / 97% / 100% across four strategies. Those numbers were plausible-looking but made up. This is exactly the kind of move I spend my thesis criticizing — data that is directionally right but unverified — and it does not belong in a portfolio. This chapter replaces those numbers with a reproducible Monte Carlo.
crumbs: 57  Verifying Pagination Strategies: A Monte Carlo Simulation

title: Verifying Pagination Strategies: A Monte Carlo Simulation
crumbs: 57  Verifying Pagination Strategies: A Monte Carlo Simulation

title: Verifying Pagination Strategies: A Monte Carlo Simulation
text:
Model
The simulation is deliberately minimal. It models one client retrieving 50 paginated resources from an API with a sliding-window rate limit and an independent transient failure rate. Four strategies are compared.
Assumptions







Parameter
Value
Justification



Total pages
50
Typical medium-scale scrape.


Records per page
10
Standard API page size.


Rate limit (peak)
5 req per 1-sec sliding window
Representative of many public REST APIs (e.g. GitHub unauthenticated, Twitter v2 per-user).


Transient failure rate
8% per admitted request
Typical for “non-rate-limit” failures: transient 5xx, network timeout, upstream hiccup. Measured in the 5–10% range on real services.


Retry policy (where enabled)
5 attempts, exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 8s); ≥ window wait on 429
Matches httr2::req_retry() defaults.


Monte Carlo runs
2,000
Gives standard error on the mean completeness of ≈ 0.1 percentage points — enough to distinguish strategies that differ by > 1 pp.



What the model does not capture:

Variable network latency (GET is modeled as instantaneous).
API-specific behaviors like Retry-After headers, burst-bucket semantics, or separate daily quotas.
Connection errors that prevent the request from being counted at all.
The cursor-drift problem (Fig. 1 on the exam slide) — that is a correctness bug, not a completeness bug, and requires a different kind of test.

These omissions all make the naive strategy look better than it would on a real API. The gaps below are therefore conservative lower bounds on the real problem.
Strategies







Strategy
Request interval
Retry?



Naive for loop (httr::GET)
0.05 s (20 req/s)
No



+ Sys.sleep(1) between calls
1.00 s
No


httr2 + req_throttle(4 /1)
0.25 s
No


httr2 + req_throttle + req_retry(5)
0.25 s
Yes

pal <- c("#C23B22", "#E09E3E", "#3E6BA8", "#2F7E7E")

summary_stats |>
  mutate(name = factor(name, levels = order)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = name, y = mean_pct, fill = name)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.56, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = p05, ymax = p95), width = 0.12, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f%%", mean_pct), color = name),
            vjust = -1.4, size = 4.3, fontface = "bold", show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.4) +
  annotate("text", x = 4.4, y = 101, label = "ground truth",
           color = "grey55", size = 2.8, fontface = "italic", hjust = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal) +
  scale_color_manual(values = pal) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 118), breaks = c(0, 25, 50, 75, 100)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Records retrieved  (% of true count)",
       title = "What fraction of the data do you actually get back?") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11, color = "grey15"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8.5, color = "grey25")
  )






Figure 57.1: Completeness across 2,000 Monte Carlo runs. Bars show mean; whiskers span the 5th–95th percentile. Sys.sleep(1) and req_throttle() are statistically indistinguishable — the 8-percentage-point jump to 100% comes from req_retry(), not from pacing.

title: Verifying Pagination Strategies: A Monte Carlo Simulation
text:
Discussion
The finding I did not expect
The exam-slide story was: naive is bad, pacing helps a lot, throttling helps more, retry closes the final gap. The simulation says the middle two steps are the same. Under the model assumptions, Sys.sleep(1) and req_throttle() produce indistinguishable completeness distributions (both ≈ 92.1%, 5–95% band 86–98). The only statistically meaningful gap is naive → paced (≈ 65 pp) and paced → paced-with-retry (≈ 8 pp).
This is more interesting than my made-up story, because it reframes the argument for httr2. httr2 is not better than a careful Sys.sleep() loop because it paces more accurately. It is better because it retries. Retry is the ingredient that takes a dataset from “looks complete” to actually complete.
That said, req_throttle() has two real advantages the simulation cannot see:


Robustness to variable latency. In the simulation each GET is instantaneous; in reality Sys.sleep(1) after a 2-second GET means an effective rate of 0.33 req/s — much slower than you intended. req_throttle(rate = 1) enforces the target rate by measuring time since the last request, so latency variance does not slow you down.

Correct handling of Retry-After. req_retry() honors the Retry-After header emitted by well-behaved APIs on 429. A hand-rolled retry in a for loop usually does not.

Both matter in production. Neither shows up in a simple completeness-only metric. The simulation is a floor, not a ceiling.
What a paced-but-no-retry run actually looks like
The 8% transient failures are not uniformly distributed across runs. Some simulation runs have 0 failed pages (48/50 × 10 = 480 records = 96% — wait, 50/50 = 100%). Other runs have 7+ failures (43/50 = 86%). The 5–95% band (86–98%) captures this variance.
What this means in practice: if you run a paced-but-unretried scrape once and it happens to complete every page, you will believe your code is correct. Run it next week and lose 14% of the data. The failure mode is probabilistic, not deterministic. This is how bugs survive code review.
What this simulation deliberately does not test


Offset drift (Exam 3 Fig. 1). That is a correctness failure in which the naive strategy returns duplicates or misses even if 100% of requests succeed. Testing it would require simulating record insertions between calls and a ground-truth set of unique records. The completeness metric above cannot see drift because it counts pages returned, not records uniquely retrieved.

Cursor vs offset pagination semantics. The simulation uses a single page-indexed request model. Drift is what makes offset pagination incorrect in principle; cursor pagination is what makes it correct. That is a structural argument, not a Monte Carlo one.

A future extension would model a changing dataset during the scrape and measure both completeness and uniqueness simultaneously.
Takeaway
The three things a for + GET() loop silently skips — drift protection, retry, throttle — are not optimizations. They are correctness guarantees. Pacing alone (either via Sys.sleep() or req_throttle()) gets you most of the way. Retry is what closes the remaining gap. httr2 is valuable because it makes all three defaults.
